topics.title: California Bearing Ratio Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCEL607 - TRANSPORTATION ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** California Bearing Ratio Test # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (As per IS 2720 Part 16 / IS 2720 Part 31) The **California Bearing Ratio (CBR) Test** is an empirical, penetration-type laboratory test conducted on a compacted or undisturbed soil specimen to determine its **bearing capacity** relative to that of a standard crushed rock material. Formally, it is defined as: > [!IMPORTANT] > **California Bearing Ratio (CBR):** The ratio, expressed as a percentage, of the unit load required to cause a plunger of standard dimensions to penetrate the soil specimen at a specified rate to the unit load required to produce the same depth of penetration in a standard sample of crushed stone (standard material). Mathematically, $$\text{CBR (\%)} = \frac{\text{Test Load at given penetration}}{\text{Standard Load at same penetration}} \times 100$$ The standard reference loads adopted (corresponding to a standard crushed stone of California origin) are: - At **2.5 mm penetration** → **1370 kg** (≈ 70 kg/cm²) - At **5.0 mm penetration** → **2055 kg** (≈ 105 kg/cm²) The test is standardized under **IS 2720 (Part 16) – 1987** (for laboratory determination on soils) and **IS 2720 (Part 31) – 1990** (for in-situ determination). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "How Hard Does the Road Push Back?" Imagine you are pressing your thumb firmly into two different surfaces: 1. A freshly compacted mound of garden soil. 2. A mound of densely packed crushed granite aggregate. Your thumb experiences very different resistance. The **CBR test replicates this exact "push-back" action** using a standardized cylindrical plunger (50 mm diameter) pushed into the soil at a constant rate of **1.25 mm/min**. The harder the soil pushes back against the plunger, the **higher its CBR** and the stronger its ability to support pavement loads without failure. In simple words: > [!NOTE] > - **Low CBR (Poor Soil)** → Soil is soft; pavement will sink, rut, and fail quickly. > - **High CBR (Good Soil)** → Soil is stiff and strong; pavement will last decades. ## 1.3 Why is CBR Critical for Highway Engineering? The CBR value is the **single most important subgrade strength parameter** used in the design of flexible pavements as per **IRC:37-2018 (Tentative Guidelines for Design of Flexible Pavements)**. The required pavement thickness is directly a function of the CBR of the subgrade soil. Therefore, this test determines: - The thickness of the granular sub-base and base course. - The suitability of locally available soil as a subgrade material. - The traffic-bearing capacity of the road. Typical indicative CBR values (for student reference): | Soil Type | Typical CBR (%) | |---|---| | Clay (soft, saturated) | 2 – 5 | | Clay (medium) | 5 – 8 | | Sandy clay / Silt | 8 – 15 | | Well-graded sand | 15 – 30 | | Crushed stone (reference) | 100 | ## 1.4 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Mapping > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, this experiment directly maps to: > - **CO2:** Perform quality control tests on pavement materials and subgrade soils (Cognitive Level: Apply / Analyse — Bloom Level 3 & 4). > - **PO5 (Modern Tool Usage):** Use of calibrated loading frame and dial gauges for engineering data acquisition. > - **PSO1 (Civil Engineering Practice):** Apply geotechnical laboratory techniques for transportation infrastructure design. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Mechanics of CBR The CBR test is fundamentally a **comparative shear-strength test** that measures the resistance of soil to **punching shear** under a localized compressive load. The key mechanical principles are: - The cylindrical plunger applies a **localized compressive stress** through a small contact area (≈ 19.35 cm²), creating high shear stresses immediately beneath the piston tip. - As penetration increases, the failure zone expands outward in a **conical wedge pattern** through the soil mass. - The penetration resistance is therefore a function of the **cohesion (c) and angle of internal friction (φ)** of the soil. - Because the test is performed at a **controlled rate (1.25 mm/min)**, undrained (for cohesive soils) or partially drained conditions prevail, making the result representative of the **worst-case field behavior** during and immediately after rainfall. > [!NOTE] > **Principle:** The CBR of a soil directly reflects its shear strength and stiffness, which are the two parameters that govern pavement subgrade performance under wheel loads. ## 2.2 Apparatus Required (IS 2720 Part 16 Specifications) The complete CBR test apparatus consists of: 1. **CBR Mould** — A rigid cylindrical metal mould, **150 mm internal diameter** and **175 mm height**, fitted with a detachable base plate and a top collar (collar height ≈ 50 mm). 2. **Spacer Disc** — A circular metal disc, **150 mm diameter** and **61.4 mm height**, placed inside the mould at the bottom during compaction (so that the effective soil specimen depth becomes 127.5 mm = 175 − 61.4 − (− compaction allowance by collar)). 3. **Compaction Rammer** — Either a **2.5 kg light rammer** (51 mm drop) or a **4.89 kg heavy rammer** (450 mm drop) — as per Proctor / Modified Proctor compaction. 4. **Loading Machine** — A motorized mechanical/hydraulic compression testing machine (capacity **50 kN**) fitted with a proving ring (calibrated). 5. **Penetration Piston (Plunger)** — A hardened steel cylinder of **50 mm diameter** with a minimum length of **100 mm**. 6. **Surcharge Weights** — Annular metal discs (each ≈ 2.5 kg) to simulate overburden pressure of the proposed pavement. 7. **Dial Gauges** — Two dial gauges (0.01 mm least count) to measure penetration and deformation. 8. **Soaking Tank** — A water tank to immerse the specimen for 4 days (96 hours). 9. **Swell Gauge / Tripod Assembly** — To measure swell during soaking. 10. **Filter Papers** and **Perforated Plates** — For drainage. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes **every formula** you will need for the KTU university exam, lab record, and viva. Memorize these. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | CBR at any penetration | $\text{CBR} = \dfrac{P_t}{P_s} \times 100$ | % | | 2 | Standard Load at 2.5 mm | $P_s = 1370 \text{ kg}$ (constant) | kg | | 3 | Standard Load at 5.0 mm | $P_s = 2055 \text{ kg}$ (constant) | kg | | 4 | Unit Load on Plunger | $p = \dfrac{P_t}{A}$ where $A = 19.35 \text{ cm}^2$ | kg/cm² | | 5 | Bulk (Wet) Density | $\rho_b = \dfrac{M_t}{V}$ | g/cc | | 6 | Dry Density | $\rho_d = \dfrac{\rho_b}{1 + w}$ | g/cc | | 7 | Initial Volume of Mould | $V = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 H$ (D = 150 mm, H = 127.5 mm) | cm³ | | 8 | Volume of Mould (numerical) | $V = 2251 \text{ cm}^3$ (constant) | cm³ | | 9 | Swell Percentage | $\%S = \dfrac{\Delta h}{h_0} \times 100$ | % | | 10 | Proving Ring Load Conversion | $P_t = \text{Proving Ring Reading} \times \text{Proving Ring Constant}$ | kg | > [!NOTE] > **Proving Ring Constant (PRC):** Always provided with the equipment. Typical values range from **0.5 kg/div to 5 kg/div** depending on proving ring capacity (commonly **500 kg, 1000 kg, 2000 kg, or 5000 kg** rated rings are used). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Application The CBR value obtained from this test directly feeds into the **IRC:37-2018** design charts for flexible pavement design. The pavement thickness is inversely proportional to CBR — meaning, the weaker the subgrade (lower CBR), the thicker the pavement layers must be to protect the subgrade from traffic-induced stresses. For example: - A **CBR = 8%** soil may require 600 mm of total pavement crust. - A **CBR = 15%** soil may require only 350 mm of total pavement crust for the same traffic loading. This single test therefore **controls the cost, life, and safety** of every national highway, state highway, and rural road in India. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Mould Volume (Standard Constant) The CBR mould is a hollow cylinder. By the standard geometric formula for the volume of a cylinder: $$V = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot H$$ Substituting the standard CBR mould dimensions: $$V = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (15.0 \text{ cm})^2 \times 12.75 \text{ cm}$$ $$V = \frac{3.14159}{4} \times 225 \times 12.75 \text{ cm}^3$$ $$\boxed{V = 2251.0 \text{ cm}^3}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is a **constant value** — examiners expect you to know it. If you are using a mould with the spacer disc, the effective soil height becomes **H = 175 − 61.4 − collar correction ≈ 127.5 mm**. ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Calculation Methodology ### Step 1: Compute the Load on the Plunger For every penetration depth, the dial gauge reading on the **proving ring** (in divisions) is converted to force using the **proving ring constant (PRC)**: $$P_t = (\text{Proving Ring Reading in divisions}) \times (\text{PRC in kg/div})$$ This gives the **actual test load** in **kg**. ### Step 2: Plot the Load-Penetration Curve A graph of **Load (kg, Y-axis)** versus **Penetration (mm, X-axis)** is plotted. The curve typically rises monotonically, then may flatten or show slight irregularities near 5 mm and beyond. ### Step 3: Apply Convexity Correction (If Required) > [!IMPORTANT] > If the load-penetration curve is **concave upward (i.e., the 5.0 mm ordinate lies on or above the extension of the steepest tangent)**, a **convexity correction** must be applied by drawing a corrected origin and shifting the curve to the right. ### Step 4: Read Corrected Loads at 2.5 mm and 5.0 mm - Read $P_{2.5}$ (in kg) at **2.5 mm penetration** on the corrected curve. - Read $P_{5.0}$ (in kg) at **5.0 mm penetration** on the corrected curve. ### Step 5: Calculate CBR Values $$\text{CBR}_{2.5} = \frac{P_{2.5}}{1370} \times 100 \text{ \%}$$ $$\text{CBR}_{5.0} = \frac{P_{5.0}}{2055} \times 100 \text{ \%}$$ ### Step 6: Adopt the Final CBR Value (Rule of Adoption) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU / IS 2720 Rule:** If $\text{CBR}_{5.0} > \text{CBR}_{2.5}$, the test must be repeated. **If the discrepancy persists after repeat**, **CBR at 5 mm penetration is adopted**. Otherwise, **CBR at 2.5 mm is adopted**. $$\boxed{\text{CBR}_{\text{adopted}} = \max(\text{CBR}_{2.5}, \text{CBR}_{5.0})}$$ (Provided the convexity rule for 5 mm is satisfied.) ## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU Exam Pattern) **Problem Statement:** During a CBR test on a soaked soil sample (compacted using the standard heavy compaction effort), the following readings were recorded on a proving ring with **Proving Ring Constant = 2.5 kg/division**. The mould had a mass of wet soil **= 5.20 kg**, and the water content was **w = 12.5%**. The volume of the mould is **2251 cm³**. | Penetration (mm) | Proving Ring Reading (div) | |---|---| | 0.0 | 0 | | 0.5 | 4 | | 1.0 | 9 | | 1.5 | 17 | | 2.0 | 28 | | 2.5 | 42 | | 3.0 | 55 | | 3.5 | 66 | | 4.0 | 74 | | 4.5 | 80 | | 5.0 | 85 | | 6.0 | 92 | | 7.0 | 96 | | 8.0 | 99 | | 10.0 | 103 | **Determine the CBR of the soil and identify the soil type.** ### Solution: **Step A — Convert readings to loads (multiply each by PRC = 2.5):** | Penetration (mm) | Load (kg) | |---|---| | 0.5 | 10.0 | | 1.0 | 22.5 | | 1.5 | 42.5 | | 2.0 | 70.0 | | 2.5 | **105.0** | | 3.0 | 137.5 | | 3.5 | 165.0 | | 4.0 | 185.0 | | 4.5 | 200.0 | | 5.0 | **212.5** | | 6.0 | 230.0 | | 7.0 | 240.0 | | 8.0 | 247.5 | | 10.0 | 257.5 | **Step B — Compute CBR at 2.5 mm penetration:** $$\text{CBR}_{2.5} = \frac{105.0}{1370} \times 100 = 7.66 \text{ \%}$$ **Step C — Compute CBR at 5.0 mm penetration:** $$\text{CBR}_{5.0} = \frac{212.5}{2055} \times 100 = 10.34 \text{ \%}$$ **Step D — Apply the Adoption Rule:** Since $\text{CBR}_{5.0} = 10.34\% > \text{CBR}_{2.5} = 7.66\%$, we must check the **convexity of the curve**. The 5 mm ordinate (212.5 kg) lies on the extension of the initial straight-line portion (slope ≈ 42.5 kg/mm) → No convexity correction needed here. So **CBR at 5 mm is adopted** (per repeated result rule): $$\boxed{\text{Soaked CBR} = 10.34\% \approx 10.3\%}$$ **Step E — Identify Soil Type:** From the indicative table in Section 1.3, a CBR of **10.3%** falls in the range of **sandy clay / silty clay (8–15%)** → **Medium-quality subgrade material**. **Step F — Compute Dry Density (additional marking opportunity):** $$\rho_b = \frac{5.20 \text{ kg}}{2251 \text{ cm}^3} = 2.31 \text{ g/cc (approx., after unit conversion)}$$ $$\rho_d = \frac{\rho_b}{1 + w} = \frac{2.31}{1 + 0.125} = 2.05 \text{ g/cc}$$ **Final Answer:** $$\boxed{\text{CBR} = 10.3\%, \quad \rho_d = 2.05 \text{ g/cc}, \quad \text{Soil} = \text{Sandy Clay / Medium Subgrade}}$$ ## 3.4 Soaking Concept & Swell Calculation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Soak?** The CBR test is conducted on soaked samples to simulate the **worst-case field condition** when the subgrade is fully saturated due to monsoon rainfall. A typical soaked CBR value is **30% to 50% lower** than the corresponding unsoaked CBR for the same soil. Swell measurement (using dial gauge over 4 days of soaking): $$\% \text{ Swell} = \frac{(\text{Final dial reading} - \text{Initial dial reading})}{\text{Original height of specimen}} \times 100$$ $$\% \text{ Swell} = \frac{\Delta h}{127.5 \text{ mm}} \times 100$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic Block Diagram — Complete CBR Test Apparatus (Functional Architecture) > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional architecture of the CBR test setup. It describes how each component interfaces with the loading system, mimicking the physical assembly in a KTU-approved transportation engineering laboratory. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MECH["MECHANICAL LOADING SYSTEM"] A1["Loading Frame
50 kN Capacity"] A2["Proving Ring
Calibrated Constant"] A3["Penetration Plunger
50 mm Diameter"] A4["Surcharge Weights
Simulated Overburden"] end subgraph SAMPLE["SOIL SPECIMEN ASSEMBLY"] B1["CBR Mould
150 mm dia x 175 mm height"] B2["Compacted Soil Specimen
Height 127.5 mm"] B3["Spacer Disc
61.4 mm height"] B4["Perforated Base Plate
and Filter Paper"] end subgraph DIAL["MEASUREMENT & DIAL GAUGE SYSTEM"] C1["Penetration Dial Gauge
0.01 mm Least Count"] C2["Swell Dial Gauge
Mounted on Tripod"] end subgraph PROC["DATA ACQUISITION & PROCESSING"] D1["Load-Penetration Table"] D2["CBR Calculation Engine"] D3["Result Output
CBR percent and Soil Class"] end A1 --> A2 --> A3 A4 -.-> B2 A3 -- penetrates --> B2 B2 -- sits on --> B3 --> B4 --> B1 C1 -- measures --> A3 C2 -- measures --> B2 A2 -- proving ring divisions --> D1 C1 -- penetration values --> D1 D1 --> D2 --> D3 ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Step-by-Step CBR Test Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["Step 1: Take Air-Dried Soil Sample
Pass 20 mm IS Sieve"] P2["Step 2: Determine OMC and MDD
via Proctor Compaction"] P3["Step 3: Mix Soil with OMC Water
Cure for 30 minutes"] P4["Step 4: Place Spacer Disc in Mould
Fix Base Plate and Filter Paper"] P5["Step 5: Compact Soil in 5 Layers
55 blows per layer with 4.89 kg rammer"] P6["Step 6: Remove Collar
Trim Top Surface"] P7["Step 7: Invert Mould
Place Surcharge Weights"] P8["Step 8: Immerse in Water Tank
Soak for 4 days 96 hours"] P9["Step 9: Measure Swell
Record Dial Gauge Readings"] P10["Step 10: Remove from Tank
Drain for 15 minutes"] P11["Step 11: Place on Loading Frame
Apply Surcharge Weights"] P12["Step 12: Seat Plunger
Apply 4 kg Initial Seating Load"] P13["Step 13: Penetration Test
1.25 mm/min rate"] P14["Step 14: Record Readings
0.5 to 10 mm penetration"] P15["Step 15: Plot Load-Penetration Curve
Compute CBR at 2.5 mm and 5 mm"] P16["Step 16: Apply Convexity Correction
Adopt Final CBR Value"] P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8 --> P9 --> P10 --> P11 --> P12 --> P13 --> P14 --> P15 --> P16 ``` ## 4.3 Apparatus Component Interaction Diagram (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TOP["UPPER ASSEMBLY"] T1["Plunger"] T2["Proving Ring"] T3["Cross-Head of Frame"] end subgraph MID["MIDDLE ASSEMBLY"] M1["Surcharge Discs"] M2["Soil Specimen"] M3["Mould Cylinder"] end subgraph BOT["LOWER ASSEMBLY"] B1["Base Plate"] B2["Filter Papers"] B3["Drainage Valve"] end T3 -- applies load --> T2 -- transmits --> T1 -- penetrates --> M2 M1 -- overburden --> M2 M2 -- contained by --> M3 M3 -- rests on --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper] **Define the California Bearing Ratio Test. Mention the IS code governing this test and list the two standard penetration depths at which CBR is calculated.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1.5 Marks):** The California Bearing Ratio (CBR) test is defined as the ratio, expressed as a percentage, of the unit load required to cause a standard plunger (50 mm diameter) to penetrate a soil specimen at a rate of 1.25 mm/min to the unit load required to produce the same depth of penetration in a standard sample of crushed stone. - **IS Code (0.5 Mark):** **IS 2720 (Part 16) – 1987** for laboratory CBR on soils. - **Standard Penetration Depths (1 Mark):** The CBR is calculated at penetration depths of **2.5 mm** (standard load 1370 kg) and **5.0 mm** (standard load 2055 kg). ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Sample Paper] **State the significance of soaking the CBR specimen for 4 days (96 hours). What is the role of surcharge weights during the test?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Significance of Soaking (1.5 Marks):** Soaking simulates the **worst-case field condition** during monsoon rainfall when the subgrade soil becomes fully saturated. Subgrade strength is minimum when saturated, and designing the pavement for this condition ensures long-term durability. - **Role of Surcharge Weights (1.5 Marks):** Surcharge weights (annular metal discs) simulate the **overburden pressure of the proposed pavement structure** above the subgrade. They prevent the soil from heaving laterally and upward during penetration, ensuring realistic confined loading conditions representative of the field. ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students must answer **ONE full question** of 14 marks from this module. The module provides **internal choice** between two full questions. --- ### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **(a)** Describe the apparatus used in the California Bearing Ratio test with a neat functional block diagram. State the dimensions of the mould and plunger as per IS specifications. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A CBR test was conducted on a soaked soil sample compacted at OMC. The proving ring constant was **3.0 kg/division**, and the readings obtained are tabulated below. Determine the CBR of the soil and classify the subgrade. | Penetration (mm) | Proving Ring Reading (div) | |---|---| | 0.5 | 6 | | 1.0 | 14 | | 1.5 | 26 | | 2.0 | 40 | | 2.5 | 58 | | 3.0 | 72 | | 4.0 | 92 | | 5.0 | 110 | | 7.5 | 128 | | 10.0 | 140 | **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Q.A(a): **Apparatus (4 Marks):** 1. **CBR Mould** — Cylindrical metal mould, **150 mm internal diameter, 175 mm height**, with detachable base plate and extension collar (50 mm height). 2. **Spacer Disc** — 150 mm diameter, 61.4 mm height, placed inside the mould at the base during compaction. 3. **Compaction Rammer** — 4.89 kg heavy rammer with 450 mm drop for Modified Proctor effort. 4. **Loading Machine** — Motorized compression testing frame of 50 kN capacity. 5. **Proving Ring** — Calibrated load-measuring device, capacity 5000 kg. 6. **Penetration Plunger** — Hardened steel, **50 mm diameter**, minimum 100 mm length. 7. **Surcharge Weights** — Annular discs, each 2.5 kg. 8. **Dial Gauges** — Two, 0.01 mm least count. 9. **Soaking Tank** — Water-tight tank with drainage valve. 10. **Filter Papers and Perforated Plates.** **Dimensions & IS Codes (3 Marks):** - Mould: D = 150 mm, H = 175 mm. - Effective Specimen Height: 127.5 mm. - Plunger Diameter: 50 mm (Area = 19.35 cm²). - Penetration Rate: 1.25 mm/min. - IS Code: IS 2720 (Part 16) – 1987. #### Model Solution for Q.A(b): **Step 1 — Convert divisions to load (PRC = 3.0 kg/div):** | Penetration (mm) | Load (kg) | |---|---| | 2.5 | $58 \times 3.0 = 174.0$ | | 5.0 | $110 \times 3.0 = 330.0$ | **Step 2 — Calculate CBR at 2.5 mm:** $$\text{CBR}_{2.5} = \frac{174.0}{1370} \times 100 = 12.70\%$$ **Step 3 — Calculate CBR at 5.0 mm:** $$\text{CBR}_{5.0} = \frac{330.0}{2055} \times 100 = 16.06\%$$ **Step 4 — Apply Adoption Rule:** Since $\text{CBR}_{5.0} = 16.06\% > \text{CBR}_{2.5} = 12.70\%$, **repeat the test**. If on repeat the result is consistent, **CBR at 5 mm is adopted** (per IS 2720 Part 16, Cl 5.4.2). **Step 5 — Final CBR:** $$\boxed{\text{Soaked CBR} = 16.1\% \text{ (adopted at 5 mm)}}$$ **Step 6 — Classification:** From standard IRC guidelines, CBR ≈ 16% corresponds to a **Good Subgrade** (sandy clay to well-graded sand range). The soil is suitable for a moderate pavement thickness. --- ### Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the step-by-step laboratory procedure for conducting the CBR test on a soaked soil sample as per IS 2720 (Part 16). Highlight the role of compaction and the significance of the loading rate. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** The following data were obtained for two compacted soil specimens prepared at different compactive efforts: | Specimen | Dry Density (g/cc) | CBR (%) | |---|---|---| | A (3 layers, 56 blows/layer) | 1.85 | 6.5 | | B (5 layers, 55 blows/layer) | 2.05 | 11.0 | Comment on the relationship between dry density and CBR. Why is the soaked CBR always lower than the unsoaked CBR for the same soil? **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Q.B(a): **Procedure (5 Marks):** 1. Take ~6 kg of air-dried soil passing 20 mm sieve. 2. Mix with OMC water and cure for 30 minutes. 3. Place spacer disc + filter paper at the bottom of the mould. 4. Compact soil in **5 equal layers** using the **4.89 kg rammer with 55 blows per layer** (Modified Proctor). 5. Remove collar, trim top surface flush with mould. 6. Invert the mould so the soil sits on the spacer disc; place filter paper on top. 7. Apply surcharge weights and immerse the specimen in water for **4 days (96 h)**. 8. Record swell using the dial gauge. 9. After soaking, place mould on the loading frame; seat plunger under a 4 kg initial seating load. 10. Apply load at **1.25 mm/min** and record proving ring readings at penetrations of 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 7.5, 10.0 mm. 11. Plot the load-penetration curve and compute CBR. **Role of Compaction (1 Mark):** Compaction densifies the soil, increases its shear strength, and reduces future settlement. It reproduces the field compaction state during construction. **Significance of Loading Rate (1 Mark):** The standard rate of **1.25 mm/min** ensures **undrained loading conditions** for cohesive soils, replicating the rapid stress application by moving traffic wheels. This yields the most conservative (lowest) strength estimate. #### Model Solution for Q.B(b): **Relationship between Dry Density and CBR (4 Marks):** $$\text{As } \rho_d \uparrow, \quad \text{CBR} \uparrow$$ - Specimen A: $\rho_d = 1.85$ g/cc → CBR = 6.5% - Specimen B: $\rho_d = 2.05$ g/cc → CBR = 11.0% A higher dry density implies: - Lower void ratio (e ↓). - Greater particle-to-particle contact. - Higher cohesion and angle of internal friction. - Hence, **higher penetration resistance** and **higher CBR**. This relationship is **non-linear**; CBR increases approximately with the **square** of the increase in dry density for many soils. Field engineers target ≥ 95% of MDD to ensure adequate subgrade CBR. **Why Soaked CBR < Unsoaked CBR (3 Marks):** 1. **Pore Water Pressure Effect:** On saturation, water fills the voids, creating **positive pore water pressure** under load, reducing effective stress ($\sigma' = \sigma - u$). Since shear strength $\tau = c' + \sigma' \tan \phi'$, a reduction in $\sigma'$ directly reduces shear strength. 2. **Lubrication Effect:** Water acts as a lubricant between soil particles, reducing inter-particle friction ($\phi$). 3. **Loss of Suction (Matric Suction):** In unsaturated soils, capillary tension contributes apparent cohesion. Saturation eliminates this suction, reducing total cohesion. 4. **Swelling of Clayey Soils:** Water causes clay particles to expand, reducing density and increasing compressibility. 5. **Reduced Penetration Resistance:** Lower shear strength directly translates to lower plunger load → lower CBR value. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks in the CBR Question:** > > 1. **Forgetting to multiply proving ring readings by PRC (Proving Ring Constant)** — Results in CBR values off by a factor of 2 to 5. (Loses 1–2 marks.) > 2. **Using wrong standard loads** — Some students memorize 70 and 105 (the *stresses* in kg/cm²) and use them as loads directly. The correct standard **loads** are **1370 kg and 2055 kg**. (Loses 2 marks.) > 3. **Ignoring the adoption rule** — Always state which CBR (2.5 mm or 5.0 mm) is adopted and the reason. Examiners specifically check for this. (Loses 1 mark.) > 4. **Not drawing the convexity correction explanation** — If your curve has 5 mm ordinate above the extended tangent, mention the correction procedure explicitly. (Loses 1 mark.) > 5. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — Always write **CBR = X.X %** and **CBR = X.X % (soaked)**. (Loses 0.5 mark.) > 6. **Confusing IS 2720 Part 16 vs Part 31** — Part 16 is **laboratory** CBR; Part 31 is **in-situ** CBR. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — California Bearing Ratio Test** > > ☐ **Definition:** CBR = (Test Load / Standard Load) × 100, expressed in %. > > ☐ **Standard Loads:** 1370 kg @ 2.5 mm; 2055 kg @ 5.0 mm. > > ☐ **IS Code:** IS 2720 (Part 16) – 1987 (Lab) | IS 2720 (Part 31) – 1990 (Field). > > ☐ **Plunger Diameter:** 50 mm (Area = 19.35 cm²). > > ☐ **Penetration Rate:** 1.25 mm/min (constant for all soils). > > ☐ **Mould Dimensions:** D = 150 mm, H = 175 mm, V = 2251 cm³. > > ☐ **Effective Specimen Height:** 127.5 mm (after spacer disc). > > ☐ **Compaction:** 5 layers × 55 blows/layer × 4.89 kg rammer (Modified Proctor). > > ☐ **Soaking Duration:** 4 days = 96 hours. > > ☐ **Surcharge Weight:** Simulates pavement overburden; ≥ 4.5 kg minimum recommended. > > ☐ **CBR Adoption Rule:** Adopt 2.5 mm value normally; adopt 5.0 mm only if $\text{CBR}_{5.0} > \text{CBR}_{2.5}$ on a convexity-corrected curve and persists on repeat. > > ☐ **Convexity Correction:** Mandatory if 5 mm ordinate lies above the extension of the steepest straight portion of the curve. > > ☐ **Typical CBR Values:** Clay = 2–8%, Sandy Clay = 8–15%, Sand = 15–30%, Crushed Stone = 100%. > > ☐ **Soaked CBR < Unsoaked CBR** — Always — because water reduces effective stress and shear strength. > > ☐ **Application:** Input parameter for **IRC:37-2018** design charts for flexible pavement thickness. > > ☐ **Plunger Area Formula:** $A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 5^2 = 19.635 \text{ cm}^2$ (≈ 19.35 cm² using practical diameter). > > ☐ **Unit Load:** $p = P_t / A$, where $P_t$ is plunger load in kg and $A = 19.35$ cm².

topics.title: Specific Gravity and Water Absorption Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL607 - TRANSPORTATION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Specific Gravity and Water Absorption Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Specific Gravity and Water Absorption Test** of coarse aggregates is a fundamental laboratory test conducted to determine the **specific gravity**, the **apparent specific gravity**, the **bulk specific gravity (saturated surface dry basis)**, and the **percentage of water absorption** of coarse aggregates used in road construction, as specified in **IS 2386 (Part III) - 1963**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:**
> **Specific Gravity** is defined as the ratio of the mass of a given volume of aggregate (at a stated temperature, conventionally **27 °C**) to the mass of an equal volume of distilled water (at the same temperature).
>
> **Water Absorption** is defined as the percentage of water absorbed by the aggregate when immersed in water for a specified period (24 hours) under prescribed conditions, expressed as a percentage of the oven-dry mass of the aggregate.

### 1.1.1 The Three Types of Specific Gravity

| Term | Academic Definition | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Apparent Specific Gravity ($G_a$)** | Ratio of the mass of oven-dry aggregate to the mass of water occupying a volume equal to that of the solid particles only (excluding permeable pores). | Density of the impermeable solid stone material itself. |
| **Bulk Specific Gravity ($G_b$)** | Ratio of the mass of oven-dry aggregate to the mass of water occupying a volume equal to the total volume of the aggregate particles (including impermeable and water-permeable pores). | Average density including all internal voids. |
| **Bulk Specific Gravity (SSD) ($G_{ssd}$)** | Ratio of the mass of saturated surface-dry aggregate to the mass of water occupying a volume equal to the total volume of the aggregate. | Density when internal pores are water-filled, surface dry. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Pearl:** $G_a > G_{ssd} > G_b$. Students often confuse these three. Memorize the order: **A**pparent is **A**lways the **A**lmost highest.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

### 🍞 The "Bread Slice" Analogy

Imagine a piece of **whole wheat bread** (the aggregate):
- The **solid floury part** inside the bread is like the **impermeable solid stone material** of the aggregate.
- The **tiny air pockets** inside the bread represent the **permeable voids** (pores that water can enter).
- The **crust (outer dry surface)** represents the **saturated surface dry (SSD)** condition — surface is dry, but inside is wet.

> 🍞 **Now the test becomes intuitive:**
> - **Apparent Specific Gravity** = weighing only the "flour" (solid mass, ignoring all air pockets).
> - **Bulk Specific Gravity** = weighing the entire bread (solid + all air pockets filled with water).
> - **SSD Specific Gravity** = weighing the bread whose surface is wiped dry, but insides are soaked.
> - **Water Absorption** = how thirsty the bread is! How much water it drinks when dunked for 24 hours.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this test matter in KTU B.Tech Civil Engineering?**
> 1. **Mix Design:** Specific gravity of aggregate is used in **Bituminous Mix Design (Marshall Method)** and **Concrete Mix Design (IS 10262)** to compute the **volumetric proportions** of aggregates.
> 2. **Quality Control:** A low specific gravity indicates **weak, porous, or weathered aggregate** which is unsuitable for road construction.
> 3. **Bitumen Absorption:** A highly porous aggregate (high water absorption) will absorb more **bitumen**, making the mix uneconomical.
> 4. **Durability:** Water absorption is an indirect measure of the **soundness and durability** of the aggregate.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Standard Range (As per IS 383 & MoRTH):**
> - **Specific Gravity** of sound aggregate: **2.6 to 2.9** (typically).
> - **Water Absorption:** Should be **less than 2%** for coarse aggregate used in **bituminous road construction**, and **less than 0.6%** for high-quality wearing course aggregates.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physics — Why Aggregates Absorb Water

Aggregates are not perfectly solid. They contain:
- **Impermeable Voids:** Closed pores that water **cannot** enter — these contribute to the **apparent volume**.
- **Permeable Voids:** Open, interconnected pores that water **can** enter under saturation — these contribute to the **bulk volume**.

When an oven-dry aggregate (mass $= A$) is immersed in water, the **permeable pores** gradually fill with water. This water-filled state changes the mass and the displaced water volume, allowing us to **mathematically separate** the three types of specific gravity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Core Concept:** The differences between $G_a$, $G_{ssd}$, and $G_b$ arise purely from **how we treat the permeable pores** — are they considered solid, ignored, or filled with water? The test mathematically quantifies this.

---

## 2.2 Standard Test Parameters (As per IS 2386 Part III - 1963)

| Parameter | Standard Specification | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Aggregate Size** | Passing 20 mm IS sieve, retained on 10 mm IS sieve | About **2 kg** of sample. |
| **Water Temperature** | **15.5 °C to 29.5 °C** (room temperature) | Preferable constant for accuracy. |
| **Saturation Period** | **24 hours** | Allows complete filling of permeable pores. |
| **Drying Method (Surface)** | Towel drying (SSD condition) | Surface dry but pores saturated. |
| **Drying Method (Oven)** | Oven at **100 °C to 110 °C** | Until constant mass achieved. |
| **Container** | Wire basket of **3.35 mm** or finer mesh | Suspended in water tank. |

---

## 2.3 Mass Variables Used in Formulas

The test uses **four key mass measurements** (recorded in grams):

| Symbol | Meaning | How Obtained |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $A$ | Mass of oven-dry aggregate in air | After oven drying to constant mass. |
| $B$ | Mass of saturated surface-dry (SSD) aggregate in air | After 24h immersion, towel dried. |
| $C$ | Mass of saturated aggregate in water (immersed weight) | Suspended in water basket. |
| $W$ | Water temperature correction (if needed) | Standard at 27 °C. |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these four formulas. They appear in EVERY KTU exam question on this topic.**

| Quantity | Formula | Standard Notation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Apparent Specific Gravity** | $G_a = \dfrac{A}{A - C}$ | Mass of dry aggregate / Mass of water equal to solid volume. |
| **Bulk Specific Gravity** | $G_b = \dfrac{A}{B - C}$ | Mass of dry aggregate / Mass of water equal to bulk volume. |
| **Bulk Specific Gravity (SSD basis)** | $G_{ssd} = \dfrac{B}{B - C}$ | Mass of SSD aggregate / Mass of water equal to bulk volume. |
| **Water Absorption (%)** | $\text{WA} = \dfrac{B - A}{A} \times 100$ | (SSD mass - Dry mass) / Dry mass $\times 100$. |
| **Volume of Permeable Voids** | $V_p = \dfrac{G_b \cdot (B - A)}{B - C} \times 100$ | KTU rare question, but included. |
| **Density (kg/m³)** | $\rho = G_b \times 1000$ | Conversion to SI density. |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL: Avoid using the vertical bar $\vert$ in exam answer sheets. Use the formula text in fraction form as shown above.**

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bituminous Mix Design (Marshall):** Specific gravity is used to calculate the **Effective Specific Gravity ($G_{se}$)** and **Maximum Theoretical Specific Gravity ($G_{mm}$)**, which determine the **air voids (Vv)** and **VMA** in bituminous mixes.
- **Concrete Mix Design (IS 10262):** Aggregate specific gravity is used in calculating the **absolute volume** of aggregates, directly affecting cement and water content.
- **Quality Grading:** Specific gravity is a quick field indicator of the **petrological type** of aggregate. For example, **trap/granite** has a higher specific gravity than **limestone**.
- **Hydraulic Design:** In **Porous Asphalt** and **Open-Graded Friction Course (OGFC)** design, high water absorption is acceptable. For **Dense Bituminous Macadam (DBM)**, low water absorption is mandatory.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Procedure with Theoretical Justification

### Step 1: Sample Preparation
- Take about **2 kg** of coarse aggregate.
- Wash thoroughly to remove dust, then **oven-dry at 100 °C to 110 °C** for 24 hours.
- Cool to room temperature (≈ 30 min in a desiccator).

> *Theoretical Justification:* A clean, dry sample ensures that subsequent mass readings are accurate and not influenced by surface contamination or retained moisture.

### Step 2: Initial Water Immersion
- Place the oven-dry sample in a wire basket and **submerge it in water at room temperature (15.5–29.5 °C)**.
- Maintain a **constant water level** such that the basket is covered by at least 50 mm of water.
- Leave the sample immersed for **24 ± 0.5 hours**.

> *Theoretical Justification:* This 24-hour period allows the **permeable pores** to become fully saturated with water through capillary action and diffusion.

### Step 3: Surface Drying (Achieving SSD Condition)
- Remove the sample from water and roll it gently on a **dry absorbent cloth** until the **visible film of water disappears**.
- For larger particles, dry each one individually with a **towel**.
- The sample is now in the **SSD state** — internal pores are saturated, but the surface is dry.

> *Theoretical Justification:* The SSD condition is the critical "intermediate state" that allows the calculation of $G_{ssd}$ and water absorption.

### Step 4: Mass Measurement in SSD State
- Immediately weigh the SSD aggregate in air.
- Record this mass as **$B$** (in grams).

> *Theoretical Justification:* Delaying this measurement will lead to water loss from the permeable pores, biasing $B$ low and reducing the accuracy of $G_{ssd}$.

### Step 5: Mass Measurement in Water (Immersed Weight)
- Place the SSD aggregate back into the **wire basket**.
- Completely immerse the basket in water at the same temperature.
- Ensure no air bubbles are trapped — shake or tap the basket gently to dislodge bubbles.
- Weigh the **basket + sample** in water. Then weigh the **empty basket** in water.
- $C = $ (Mass of basket + sample in water) $-$ (Mass of empty basket in water).

> *Theoretical Justification:* By Archimedes' principle, the loss in weight equals the weight of water displaced by the **bulk volume** of the aggregate (including all pores).

### Step 6: Final Oven Drying
- Remove the sample from the basket, drain water, and **oven-dry at 100 °C to 110 °C** for 24 hours.
- Cool and weigh to a **constant mass** $A$.

> *Theoretical Justification:* Constant mass confirms that all free and most of the chemically bound water has evaporated.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Formulas — From First Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Favorite Question:** "Derive the formula for bulk specific gravity of aggregate."

### 3.2.1 Derivation of Bulk Specific Gravity ($G_b$)

**Step 1:** By definition, specific gravity is the ratio of two masses (since the densities of water and the displaced water are at the same temperature, volumes cancel out).

$$G_b = \dfrac{\text{Mass of oven-dry aggregate}}{\text{Mass of water displaced by bulk volume of aggregate}}$$

**Step 2:** The **mass of oven-dry aggregate** is the experimentally measured quantity $A$.

$$G_b = \dfrac{A}{\text{Mass of water displaced}}$$

**Step 3:** By **Archimedes' principle**, when the aggregate is suspended in water, it loses weight equal to the weight of water displaced. Hence:

$$\text{Mass of water displaced} = B - C$$

where:
- $B$ is the apparent mass of the SSD aggregate in air (≈ its true mass in air).
- $C$ is the apparent mass when suspended in water (immersed weight).

**Step 4:** Substituting:

$$\boxed{G_b = \dfrac{A}{B - C}}$$

> *Interpretation:* The denominator $(B - C)$ represents the weight of water equal in volume to the **bulk volume** of the aggregate (solid + impermeable voids + permeable voids).

---

### 3.2.2 Derivation of Apparent Specific Gravity ($G_a$)

**Step 1:** By definition:

$$G_a = \dfrac{\text{Mass of oven-dry aggregate}}{\text{Mass of water equal in volume to impermeable solid only}}$$

**Step 2:** The mass of dry aggregate is $A$. Now, what volume of water equals only the **solid** (impermeable) volume?

- When the aggregate is immersed in water, the apparent mass loss ($B - C$) accounts for the water displaced by the **bulk volume** (solid + all voids).
- However, the water absorbed by the aggregate (in the permeable pores) has weight $(B - A)$ — this water is "inside" the aggregate, **not** displaced in the water tank.
- Therefore, the weight of water equal to the **solid volume only** is:

$$\text{Apparent water mass} = (B - C) - (B - A) = A - C$$

**Step 3:** Substituting:

$$\boxed{G_a = \dfrac{A}{A - C}}$$

> *Interpretation:* The denominator $(A - C)$ is the **buoyant loss** if the aggregate had no permeable pores — i.e., as if it were a fully solid, impermeable stone.

---

### 3.2.3 Derivation of SSD Specific Gravity ($G_{ssd}$)

**Step 1:** By definition:

$$G_{ssd} = \dfrac{\text{Mass of SSD aggregate}}{\text{Mass of water equal to bulk volume}}$$

**Step 2:** Mass of SSD aggregate is $B$, and the bulk water displacement mass is $B - C$ (same as for $G_b$).

**Step 3:** Therefore:

$$\boxed{G_{ssd} = \dfrac{B}{B - C}}$$

---

### 3.2.4 Derivation of Water Absorption (WA)

**Step 1:** Water absorption is the mass of water absorbed relative to the oven-dry mass.

**Step 2:** The mass of water absorbed into the aggregate pores is:

$$\text{Water absorbed} = B - A$$

**Step 3:** Expressed as a percentage of the dry mass:

$$\boxed{\text{WA (\%)} = \dfrac{B - A}{A} \times 100}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern)

### **Problem Statement (Typical 7-Mark KTU Style):**

In a laboratory test, the following observations were recorded for a sample of coarse aggregate:

- Mass of oven-dry aggregate in air ($A$) = **1250 g**
- Mass of saturated surface-dry aggregate in air ($B$) = **1290 g**
- Mass of saturated aggregate immersed in water ($C$) = **785 g**

Calculate the **bulk specific gravity**, **apparent specific gravity**, **SSD specific gravity**, **water absorption**, and comment on the suitability of the aggregate for road construction.

---

### **Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

#### (i) Bulk Specific Gravity ($G_b$)

$$G_b = \dfrac{A}{B - C}$$

$$G_b = \dfrac{1250}{1290 - 785} = \dfrac{1250}{505}$$

$$\boxed{G_b = 2.475}$$

#### (ii) Apparent Specific Gravity ($G_a$)

$$G_a = \dfrac{A}{A - C}$$

$$G_a = \dfrac{1250}{1250 - 785} = \dfrac{1250}{465}$$

$$\boxed{G_a = 2.688}$$

#### (iii) SSD Specific Gravity ($G_{ssd}$)

$$G_{ssd} = \dfrac{B}{B - C}$$

$$G_{ssd} = \dfrac{1290}{1290 - 785} = \dfrac{1290}{505}$$

$$\boxed{G_{ssd} = 2.554}$$

> **Verification of order:** $G_a \; (2.688) > G_{ssd} \; (2.554) > G_b \; (2.475)$ ✓

#### (iv) Water Absorption (WA)

$$\text{WA} = \dfrac{B - A}{A} \times 100$$

$$\text{WA} = \dfrac{1290 - 1250}{1250} \times 100 = \dfrac{40}{1250} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{WA} = 3.20 \; \%}$$

#### (v) Comment on Suitability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - The **bulk specific gravity of 2.475** is **slightly lower than the typical range of 2.6 to 2.9** for sound aggregate. This suggests the aggregate is moderately porous.
> - The **water absorption of 3.20%** is **above the permissible limit of 2%** for aggregates used in bituminous road construction (as per MoRTH 5th Rev., Table 4.3).
> - **Conclusion:** The aggregate is **not suitable for use in the wearing course of bituminous pavements** but may be used in lower layers (base course) where strict limits are not enforced.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Complete Test Procedure

The following flowchart maps the **chronological sequence of operations** for the Specific Gravity and Water Absorption Test. It illustrates the **interconnection between mass measurements ($A$, $B$, $C$)** and the **logical decision pathways** (e.g., repetition of oven drying until constant mass is achieved).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Take 2 kg of coarse aggregate] --> A2[Wash and remove dust]
    A2 --> A3[Oven dry at 100 to 110 degrees C for 24 hours]
    A3 --> A4{Cool and weigh to constant mass A}
    A4 --> A5[Submerge in water at 15.5 to 29.5 degrees C for 24 hours]
    A5 --> A6[Remove and towel dry to SSD condition]
    A6 --> A7[Weigh SSD sample in air: record mass B]
    A7 --> A8[Suspend sample in wire basket in water]
    A8 --> A9[Tap basket to remove air bubbles]
    A9 --> A10[Weigh immersed sample: record mass C]
    A10 --> A11[Oven dry again at 100 to 110 degrees C]
    A11 --> A12{Cool and weigh to constant mass A}
    A12 --> A13[Compute Gb, Ga, Gssd, and WA using formulas]
    A13 --> A14[End: Record results and comment on suitability]

    style A1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A7 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A10 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A12 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A13 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A14 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> *Legend:* **Yellow** = Start, **Sky Blue** = Critical Mass Measurement Points, **Light Green** = Computation Step, **Red** = End.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Apparatus & Functional Architecture

Since a complex 3D laboratory apparatus cannot be easily rendered, the following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** maps the **interactions between the equipment pieces** and the **data flow of mass measurements**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Lab_Equipment [Laboratory Equipment Set]
        E1[Oven 100 to 110 degrees C] 
        E2[Wire Basket 3.35 mm mesh] 
        E3[Water Tank with constant level] 
        E4[Weighing Balance 0.1 g accuracy] 
        E5[Absorbent Cloth for SSD drying] 
    end

    subgraph Sample_States [Aggregate Sample States]
        S1[State 1: Oven Dry Aggregate] 
        S2[State 2: Saturated Sample in Water] 
        S3[State 3: SSD Aggregate] 
    end

    subgraph Mass_Data [Recorded Mass Values]
        M1[Mass A: Oven Dry in Air] 
        M2[Mass B: SSD in Air] 
        M3[Mass C: Saturated Immersed in Water] 
    end

    subgraph Output_Stage [Computed Output]
        O1[Specific Gravity Values Ga, Gb, Gssd] 
        O2[Water Absorption Percentage] 
        O3[Suitability Comment] 
    end

    E1 --> S1
    S1 --> M1
    E2 --> S2
    E3 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    E5 --> S3
    S3 --> M2
    S2 --> M3
    E4 --> M1
    E4 --> M2
    E4 --> M3
    M1 --> O1
    M2 --> O1
    M3 --> O1
    M1 --> O2
    M2 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
    O2 --> O3

    style Lab_Equipment fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#000
    style Sample_States fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000
    style Mass_Data fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#000
    style Output_Stage fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000
```

> *Interpretation:* This block diagram clarifies that **all three mass measurements ($A$, $B$, $C$) are essential inputs** to compute **all four output parameters**. Missing even one measurement renders the entire test invalid.

---

## 4.3 Concept Map — Interrelationship of the Three Specific Gravities

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P0[Aggregate Particle] --> P1[Impermeable Solid Volume Vs]
    P0 --> P2[Permeable Pore Volume Vp]
    P1 --> P3[Apparent SG: Ga = A divided by Vs in water]
    P1 --> P4[Bulk SG: Gb = A divided by Vb in water]
    P2 --> P4
    P1 --> P5[SSD SG: Gssd = B divided by Vb in water]
    P2 --> P5
    P4 --> P6[Always: Ga greater than Gssd greater than Gb]
    P3 --> P6
    P5 --> P6

    style P0 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000
    style P6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Differentiate between bulk specific gravity and apparent specific gravity of aggregate. State the conditions under which each is used.**

#### **Model Answer:**

| Property | Bulk Specific Gravity ($G_b$) | Apparent Specific Gravity ($G_a$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Ratio of the mass of oven-dry aggregate to the mass of water equal in volume to the **bulk volume** (solid + all pores). | Ratio of the mass of oven-dry aggregate to the mass of water equal in volume to the **impermeable solid** only. |
| **Voids considered** | Includes both impermeable and permeable voids. | Excludes permeable pores (assumes them to be solid). |
| **Formula** | $G_b = A / (B - C)$ | $G_a = A / (A - C)$ |
| **Used in** | General concrete and bituminous mix design. | Calculating the volume of **solid stone material**, used in porosity calculations. |
| **Magnitude** | Lower (since denominator is larger). | Higher (since denominator is smaller). |

> **Statement of Use:** $G_b$ is used in **Marshall Mix Design** for computing air voids (Vv), VMA, and VFB. $G_a$ is used when the **absolute volume of the solid stone** must be known (e.g., in advanced petrographic studies).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**What is the significance of the water absorption test of aggregate in road construction? What is the permissible limit as per MoRTH specifications?**

#### **Model Answer:**

**Significance:**
1. **Indicator of Porosity:** A high water absorption value indicates that the aggregate has a **high proportion of permeable voids**, making it weak and prone to disintegration under traffic loads.
2. **Bitumen Absorption:** Highly absorptive aggregates will **absorb more bitumen** from the mix, leading to a **hungry mix** and increased construction cost.
3. **Frost Susceptibility:** Aggregates with high water absorption are more prone to **frost heave** and **freeze-thaw damage** in cold climates.
4. **Durability:** Water absorption is an indirect measure of the **soundness and weathering resistance** of the aggregate.

**Permissible Limits (As per MoRTH 5th Revision, Table 4.3):**

| Application | Maximum Water Absorption |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Wearing Course (Bituminous)** | **0.6 %** (for surface dressing) |
| **Bituminous Macadam / DBM** | **2.0 %** |
| **WBM Base Course** | **2.0 %** (with upper limit relaxed) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)**, carrying **7 marks each**. Choice must be made between two full questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Define **apparent specific gravity**, **bulk specific gravity**, and **bulk specific gravity (SSD basis)**. Explain the procedure to determine these in the laboratory. Derive the corresponding expressions. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions:**
- **Apparent Specific Gravity ($G_a$):** Ratio of the mass of oven-dry aggregate to the mass of water equal in volume to the **impermeable solid** portion of the aggregate. (Ref: IS 2386 Part III)
- **Bulk Specific Gravity ($G_b$):** Ratio of the mass of oven-dry aggregate to the mass of water equal in volume to the **bulk volume** (solid + impermeable pores + permeable pores).
- **Bulk Specific Gravity (SSD basis) ($G_{ssd}$):** Ratio of the mass of **saturated surface-dry (SSD)** aggregate to the mass of water equal in volume to the **bulk volume**.

**(b) Laboratory Procedure (As per IS 2386 Part III - 1963):**
1. Take about **2 kg** of clean coarse aggregate, oven-dry to constant mass $A$.
2. Immerse in water at 15.5–29.5 °C for **24 ± 0.5 hours**.
3. Remove and towel-dry to SSD condition; weigh in air to get mass $B$.
4. Suspend in wire basket, immerse in water, and weigh to get immersed mass $C$.
5. Oven-dry again to confirm constant mass $A$.

**(c) Derivation:**

For $G_b$, the mass of water displaced by the **bulk volume** of the aggregate is $(B - C)$ (by Archimedes' principle, apparent loss in weight). Hence:

$$G_b = \dfrac{A}{B - C}$$

For $G_a$, the water equal to the **solid volume only** excludes the water absorbed in the pores. The water absorbed is $(B - A)$. So the water displaced by the solid is $(B - C) - (B - A) = (A - C)$. Hence:

$$G_a = \dfrac{A}{A - C}$$

For $G_{ssd}$, the mass is $B$ and the bulk water displacement is $(B - C)$. Hence:

$$G_{ssd} = \dfrac{B}{B - C}$$

---

**(b)** The following observations were recorded during the specific gravity test of a coarse aggregate sample: $A = 980$ g, $B = 1020$ g, $C = 615$ g. Calculate the bulk specific gravity, apparent specific gravity, SSD specific gravity, and water absorption. Comment on the suitability of the aggregate for use in a bituminous wearing course. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Given:** $A = 980 \; g$, $B = 1020 \; g$, $C = 615 \; g$.

**(i) Bulk Specific Gravity:**

$$G_b = \dfrac{A}{B - C} = \dfrac{980}{1020 - 615} = \dfrac{980}{405}$$

$$\boxed{G_b = 2.420}$$

**(ii) Apparent Specific Gravity:**

$$G_a = \dfrac{A}{A - C} = \dfrac{980}{980 - 615} = \dfrac{980}{365}$$

$$\boxed{G_a = 2.685}$$

**(iii) SSD Specific Gravity:**

$$G_{ssd} = \dfrac{B}{B - C} = \dfrac{1020}{1020 - 615} = \dfrac{1020}{405}$$

$$\boxed{G_{ssd} = 2.519}$$

**Verification:** $G_a \; (2.685) > G_{ssd} \; (2.519) > G_b \; (2.420)$ ✓

**(iv) Water Absorption:**

$$\text{WA} = \dfrac{B - A}{A} \times 100 = \dfrac{1020 - 980}{980} \times 100 = \dfrac{40}{980} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{WA} = 4.08 \; \%}$$

**(v) Comment on Suitability:**

- $G_b = 2.42$ — **below the typical range of 2.6–2.9** for sound aggregate, indicating a **weak, porous material**.
- $\text{WA} = 4.08\%$ — **far above the permissible limit of 0.6%** for bituminous wearing course (MoRTH 5th Rev.).
- **Conclusion:** The aggregate is **NOT suitable** for use in a bituminous wearing course. It can only be considered for **sub-base or non-critical applications**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **physical significance** of the three types of specific gravity with a neat diagram. State the relevant IS code and the conditions of aggregate for which each is applied in design. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**IS Code:** **IS 2386 (Part III) - 1963** — "Methods of Test for Aggregates for Concrete: Specific Gravity, Density, Voids, Absorption and Bulking."

**Physical Significance:**

- **Apparent Specific Gravity ($G_a$):** Represents the **true density of the parent rock** material, excluding any pore spaces. It is useful when the engineer is interested in the **mineralogical quality** of the stone, not its voids.

- **Bulk Specific Gravity ($G_b$):** Represents the **effective density** of the aggregate as it exists in the real world — solid material plus all its internal pores. It is the **most commonly used value** in mix design.

- **SSD Specific Gravity ($G_{ssd}$):** Represents the density when the aggregate pores are **saturated with water** (a conservative condition, as it may happen in service due to rain or moisture migration from the subgrade).

**Conditions of Application:**

| Specific Gravity | Used When |
| :--- | :--- |
| $G_b$ | Aggregate is in **oven-dry** state (e.g., stored in covered sheds). |
| $G_{ssd}$ | Aggregate is exposed to moisture in service (e.g., road pavement, outdoor structures). |
| $G_a$ | Calculating **absolute volume of the solid** (e.g., determining the porosity of the aggregate). |

**Neat Diagram Description (with sketch):**

```
   ┌──────────────────┐
   │ ⊕ ⊕ ⊕ ⊕ ⊕ ⊕ ⊕  │  ← Outer surface
   │  ┌──┐    ⊕ ⊕ ⊕  │     (dry in SSD)
   │  │[]│  ┌──┐  ⊕  │     
   │  │[]│  │[]│ ┌─┐ │  [] = impermeable void (counted in solid)
   │  └──┘  └──┘ │·│ │  ·  = water-filled permeable void
   │              └─┘ │  ⊕  = solid stone matrix
   └──────────────────┘
       ↑ Outer Boundary
   (Bulk Volume = Solid + All Voids)
   (Apparent Volume = Solid + Impermeable Voids)
```

- **Bulk Volume** = Outer boundary encloses everything.
- **Apparent Volume** = Outer boundary encloses solid + impermeable voids.
- **Permeable voids** = The extra space between apparent and bulk.

---

**(b)** A coarse aggregate sample weighing 1500 g in oven-dry condition was found to weigh 1560 g in saturated surface-dry condition and 945 g when suspended in water. Calculate the **percentage of water absorption** and **all three specific gravities**. If the aggregate is to be used in a **wet-freeze zone**, what additional precautions are recommended? **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Given:** $A = 1500 \; g$, $B = 1560 \; g$, $C = 945 \; g$.

**(i) Bulk Specific Gravity:**

$$G_b = \dfrac{A}{B - C} = \dfrac{1500}{1560 - 945} = \dfrac{1500}{615}$$

$$\boxed{G_b = 2.439}$$

**(ii) Apparent Specific Gravity:**

$$G_a = \dfrac{A}{A - C} = \dfrac{1500}{1500 - 945} = \dfrac{1500}{555}$$

$$\boxed{G_a = 2.703}$$

**(iii) SSD Specific Gravity:**

$$G_{ssd} = \dfrac{B}{B - C} = \dfrac{1560}{1560 - 945} = \dfrac{1560}{615}$$

$$\boxed{G_{ssd} = 2.537}$$

**Verification:** $G_a > G_{ssd} > G_b$ ✓

**(iv) Water Absorption:**

$$\text{WA} = \dfrac{B - A}{A} \times 100 = \dfrac{1560 - 1500}{1500} \times 100 = \dfrac{60}{1500} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{WA} = 4.00 \; \%}$$

**(v) Precautions for Wet-Freeze Zone:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Use aggregates with WA < 1%** to minimize freeze-thaw damage.
> 2. Conduct the **Soundness Test (IS 2386 Part V)** using sodium or magnesium sulphate to simulate freeze-thaw cycles.
> 3. **Drainage layer:** Provide a proper **sub-surface drainage system** to prevent water accumulation in the pavement.
> 4. **Air-entraining agents:** Use **air-entrained concrete** (if concrete pavement) to provide space for ice expansion.
> 5. **Avoid lightweight aggregate** in such zones; prefer **dense, low-porosity aggregates** (basalt, granite, trap rock).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the denominator in $G_a$ and $G_b$:** Many students write $G_b = A / (A - C)$ — this is **WRONG**. It is the formula for $G_a$.
> 2. **Using SI units incorrectly:** Masses must be in **grams (g)** consistently, or all converted to kg. Mixing units causes a 1000× error.
> 3. **Forgetting the × 100 in water absorption:** Water absorption is a **percentage**. Forgetting this is a 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Not mentioning IS 2386 Part III - 1963:** Examiners allot **dedicated marks** for citing the IS code. Always mention it.
> 5. **Not commenting on suitability:** In numerical questions, the final "comment on suitability" carries **2 marks** specifically.
> 6. **Misinterpreting SSD condition:** SSD means **surface dry but pores saturated** — not "fully dry" and not "fully soaked".
> 7. **Order of magnitudes:** Forgetting that $G_a > G_{ssd} > G_b$ — a quick numerical check that earns easy marks if remembered.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):**

- ✅ **IS Code:** **IS 2386 (Part III) - 1963** — cite it in every answer.
- ✅ **Saturation period:** **24 hours** at room temperature (15.5–29.5 °C).
- ✅ **Drying temperature:** **100 °C to 110 °C** in oven.
- ✅ **Aggregate size:** Passing **20 mm**, retained on **10 mm** IS sieve.
- ✅ **Three Specific Gravities (Magnitude Order):** $G_a > G_{ssd} > G_b$.
- ✅ **Four Formulas — Memorize Cold:**
    - $G_a = A / (A - C)$
    - $G_b = A / (B - C)$
    - $G_{ssd} = B / (B - C)$
    - $\text{WA (\%)} = [(B - A) / A] \times 100$
- ✅ **Standard SG range:** **2.6 to 2.9** for sound coarse aggregate.
- ✅ **Permissible WA limits (MoRTH):**
    - **Wearing course** → ≤ **0.6 %**
    - **Bituminous macadam/DBM** → ≤ **2.0 %**
- ✅ **Practical significance:** Low SG + High WA = **weak, porous, unsuitable aggregate**.
- ✅ **Apparent SG ignores permeable pores**; Bulk SG includes all pores; SSD SG assumes pores are water-filled.
- ✅ **SSD condition = surface dry, pores saturated** — easy to confuse, frequently tested.
- ✅ **Archimedes' principle** underpins the test: apparent loss in water = weight of displaced water.
- ✅ **Volume of permeable voids formula** (advanced): $V_p = G_b \cdot (B - A) / (B - C) \times 100$ — useful for high-marks derivation questions.
- ✅ **Applications:** Marshall Mix Design, Concrete Mix Design (IS 10262), Aggregate Quality Grading.
- ✅ **Real-world analogy:** The "bread slice" — solid flour, air pockets, dry crust = Aggregate solid, pores, SSD surface.

> 🎯 **Final KTU Tip:** When asked a numerical problem, **always compute all four quantities** ($G_a$, $G_b$, $G_{ssd}$, WA) and **end with a suitability comment**. This habit alone can boost your score by 2–3 marks per question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aggregate Impact Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL607 - TRANSPORTATION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Aggregate Impact Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: AGGREGATE IMPACT TEST

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU / IS 2386 Part IV – 1963):**
> The **Aggregate Impact Test** is a standard laboratory test conducted to determine the **Aggregate Impact Value (AIV)** of coarse aggregates, which is a measure of the resistance of the aggregate to sudden impact or shock loading. The test simulates the effect of impact or sudden loads that aggregates endure in road construction under the action of moving traffic, rollers, or other dynamic forces.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you drop a ripe coconut onto a hard floor — it cracks easily. Now drop a small stone on the same floor — it barely chips. That difference in resistance to sudden impact is exactly what the Aggregate Impact Test quantifies. The test essentially asks the question: *"If a standard hammer strikes an aggregate sample 15 times, how much of it breaks down into fines?"* The smaller the percentage that breaks, the tougher the aggregate, and the better it is for use in road pavement layers.

### 1.2 Why is the Impact Test Needed?

Aggregates used in road construction, particularly in **bituminous layers (wearing course, base course)** and **water-bound macadam**, are subjected to:
- **Dynamic (impact) loads** from fast-moving vehicle tyres.
- **Vibratory loads** during construction compaction.
- **Repeated shock** from braking and accelerating vehicles.

An aggregate that shatters under these conditions will cause **rutting, potholes, and surface failures**. Hence, evaluating its impact resistance is a mandatory quality control step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Reference:** IS 2386 (Part IV) – 1963 — *Methods of Test for Aggregates for Concrete — Mechanical Properties.*
> The impact value is also a general index of the **toughness** of an aggregate and is broadly related to its **compressive strength**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standards in Bold

- **Standard hammer mass:** **13.5 kg to 14.0 kg** (typically 14 kg).
- **Drop height of hammer:** **380 mm ± 5 mm**.
- **Number of blows:** **15 blows** (applied at an interval of not less than 1 second).
- **Sieve used for fines:** **2.36 mm IS Sieve**.
- **Aggregate size for test:** **12.5 mm passing and 10 mm retained** (passing 12.5 mm and retained on 10 mm IS Sieve).
- **Sample weight (W1):** Aggregate filling the cylindrical measure in **3 equal layers**, each tamped **25 times** with the tamping rod.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle

The test works on a simple **"impact and sieve"** principle:
1. A known mass of dry aggregate is placed inside a cylindrical cup mounted on a rigid base.
2. A metal hammer of standard weight is dropped from a fixed height onto the aggregate, **15 times**.
3. The crushed material is then sieved through a **2.36 mm sieve**.
4. The fraction passing the 2.36 mm sieve is weighed and expressed as a percentage of the original sample weight. This percentage is the **Aggregate Impact Value (AIV)**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Test

- **Step 1 — Sample Preparation:** The aggregate is oven-dried at **100 °C to 110 °C** for 4 hours and cooled to room temperature.
- **Step 2 — Filling the Cylinder:** The dried aggregate is filled into a cylindrical steel cup (of internal diameter 102 mm and depth 50 mm) in **three layers**, each compacted with **25 strokes** of a tamping rod (12 mm dia, 800 mm long, rounded at one end).
- **Step 3 — Impact Loading:** The cup is fixed firmly in position on the base of the testing machine. The hammer is raised to a height of **380 mm** and allowed to fall freely onto the aggregate, delivering an impact blow. This is repeated **15 times** at uniform intervals (≥ 1 second between blows).
- **Step 4 — Sieving:** The entire crushed aggregate is transferred to a **2.36 mm IS sieve** and sieved for a minimum of 1 minute. The fraction **passing** the 2.36 mm sieve is weighed to the nearest **0.1 g** (W2).
- **Step 5 — Calculation:** The original weight of the sample (W1) is also noted, and AIV is computed.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aggregate Impact Value | $\text{AIV}$ | $\dfrac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ | % |
| Weight of original sample | $W_1$ | Measured directly | g |
| Weight of fraction passing 2.36 mm sieve | $W_2$ | Measured after sieving | g |
| Hammer mass | — | **13.5 – 14.0** | kg |
| Drop height | $h$ | **380 ± 5** | mm |
| Number of blows | $n$ | **15** | — |
| Cylinder internal diameter | $D$ | **102** | mm |
| Cylinder depth | $d$ | **50** | mm |
| Sieve size for fines | — | **2.36 mm IS Sieve** | mm |
| Test aggregate size | — | **Passing 12.5 mm & Retained on 10 mm** | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EQUATION TO MEMORIZE:**
> $$\boxed{\text{AIV} = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100 \%}$$
> where $W_1$ = total weight of dry aggregate sample, and $W_2$ = weight of aggregate fraction passing through the **2.36 mm IS sieve** after impact.

## 2.4 Engineering Interpretation of AIV

The Aggregate Impact Value is a direct indicator of the **toughness** of the aggregate. Lower AIV = tougher, more impact-resistant aggregate.

| AIV Range (%) | Aggregate Quality | Recommended Use |
|---|---|---|
| $\lt 10$ | Exceptionally strong | Heavy-duty concrete, runways |
| $10$ – $20$ | Strong | **Wearing course** (bituminous surface) |
| $20$ – $30$ | Moderately strong | **Base course** (lower layers) |
| $\gt 30$ | Weak | **NOT suitable** for road construction |

> [!NOTE]
> As per **IRC / IS specifications**, aggregates with AIV ≤ **30%** are generally acceptable for **bituminous road construction**. For **WBM (Water Bound Macadam)**, AIV ≤ **40%** is acceptable for the base course.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Pavement Design:** Engineers use AIV to decide whether a given quarry aggregate can be used in upper (high-stress) pavement layers or only in lower (sub-base) layers.
- **Quality Assurance:** Helps reject substandard aggregates at the quarry site before they are transported to the project.
- **Comparative Studies:** Allows comparison between different aggregate sources (granite, basalt, limestone, gravel) for a specific project.
- **Correlations:** AIV is often empirically correlated with other mechanical properties such as **Los Angeles Abrasion Value**, **Aggregate Crushing Value**, and **10% Fines Value**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the AIV Formula

The Aggregate Impact Value is fundamentally a **mass ratio expressed as a percentage**. Let us derive it from first principles.

### 3.1.1 Defining the Variables

Let:
- $W_1$ = Mass of the **original dry aggregate sample** placed in the cylindrical cup (in grams).
- $W_2$ = Mass of the **aggregate fraction that passes** through the 2.36 mm sieve **after** the 15 impact blows (in grams).

### 3.1.2 Physical Interpretation

When the hammer strikes the aggregate 15 times, the larger particles fracture and break down into smaller fragments. A portion of this broken material becomes small enough to pass through the 2.36 mm sieve. The proportion of this fine fraction, relative to the original sample, indicates the **damage** caused by the impact.

Mathematically, the **fractional damage** is:

$$
\text{Fractional Damage} = \frac{W_2}{W_1}
$$

### 3.1.3 Converting to a Percentage

To express this ratio as a standard percentage (which is the convention in IS 2386 Part IV):

$$
\text{AIV (\%)} = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

### 3.1.4 Unit Check

- Numerator: $W_2$ has units of grams (g).
- Denominator: $W_1$ has units of grams (g).
- Ratio: $\dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{g}}$ = dimensionless.
- Multiplied by 100: still dimensionless, expressed as a **%**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **lower the AIV, the tougher the aggregate**, because less material was pulverized into fines by the impact blows.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Typical Problem)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model]:**
> In an Aggregate Impact Test conducted on a coarse aggregate sample, the following observations were recorded:
> - Original dry weight of aggregate sample, $W_1 = 324.5$ g
> - Weight of aggregate passing 2.36 mm sieve after 15 blows, $W_2 = 78.2$ g
>
> **Calculate the Aggregate Impact Value (AIV) and comment on the suitability of the aggregate for use in a bituminous wearing course.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the given values.**

$$
W_1 = 324.5 \text{ g}, \quad W_2 = 78.2 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the AIV formula.**

$$
\text{AIV} = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\text{AIV} = \frac{78.2}{324.5} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the division.**

$$
\frac{78.2}{324.5} = 0.24099...
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by 100.**

$$
\text{AIV} = 0.24099 \times 100 = 24.10\%
$$

**Step 6 — Round to appropriate significant figures.**

$$
\boxed{\text{AIV} \approx 24.1\%}
$$

**Step 7 — Comment on suitability.**

Since $\text{AIV} = 24.1\%$, which lies in the range of **20% – 30%**, the aggregate is classified as **moderately strong**. 

> **Conclusion:** The aggregate is **suitable for the base course** of a bituminous pavement, but **NOT recommended for the wearing course** (where AIV should ideally be ≤ 20%).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Exam Traps

| Common Mistake | Correct Approach | Marks Lost (Typical) |
|---|---|---|
| Using sieve size 4.75 mm instead of 2.36 mm | Always sieve through **2.36 mm IS Sieve** for the impact test. | 1–2 marks |
| Forgetting to multiply by 100 | Always express AIV as a **percentage** | 1 mark |
| Using wrong aggregate size | Test uses **passing 12.5 mm and retained on 10 mm** | 2 marks |
| Conflating with LA Abrasion Test | Impact test uses **hammer drop**, not rotating drum | 2–3 marks |
| Reporting AIV with units like "g" | AIV is **dimensionless (%)** | 1 mark |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Apparatus Block Diagram

The following block diagram shows the principal components of the Aggregate Impact Testing Machine.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Base Plate with Rigid Support] --> B[Cylindrical Steel Cup 102 mm dia]
    B --> C[Aggregate Sample in 3 Layers]
    C --> D[Tamping Rod 25 strokes per layer]
    D --> E[Vertical Guide Cylinder]
    E --> F[Hammer 13.5 to 14.0 kg]
    F --> G[Drop Height 380 mm]
    G --> H[Impact 15 Blows]
    H --> I[2.36 mm IS Sieve]
    I --> J[Weighing Balance 0.1 g accuracy]
    J --> K[Calculate AIV = W2/W1 x 100]
```

## 4.2 Test Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Step 1: Oven dry aggregate 100 to 110 deg C] --> P2[Step 2: Sieve to obtain 12.5 mm passing and 10 mm retained]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Fill cylindrical cup in 3 layers]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Tamp each layer 25 times]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Weigh sample as W1]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Fix cup on base of machine]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Drop hammer 15 times from 380 mm]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Transfer crushed aggregate to 2.36 mm sieve]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Sieve for 1 minute and weigh fines as W2]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Calculate AIV percent]
    P10 --> P11[Step 11: Compare with IRC standards and conclude]
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix for Aggregate Suitability

```mermaid
graph TD
    R1[Calculate AIV percent] --> R2{AIV less than 10 percent}
    R2 -- Yes --> R3[Exceptionally strong: Use in runways and high stress concrete]
    R2 -- No --> R4{AIV 10 to 20 percent}
    R4 -- Yes --> R5[Strong: Suitable for wearing course]
    R4 -- No --> R6{AIV 20 to 30 percent}
    R6 -- Yes --> R7[Moderately strong: Suitable for base course only]
    R6 -- No --> R8{AIV greater than 30 percent}
    R8 -- Yes --> R9[Weak: Reject for road construction]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Schematic of the Impact Action

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Hammer 14 kg] -->|Drop 380 mm| B[Aggregate Bed]
    B -->|Fracture| C[Particles greater than 2.36 mm]
    B -->|Fracture| D[Fines less than 2.36 mm]
    D -->|Weigh as W2| E[AIV Calculation]
    C -->|Discard| F[Retained on Sieve]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Aggregate Impact Value. State its significance in road construction. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Aggregate Impact Value (AIV)** is defined as the percentage of the fine material (passing the 2.36 mm IS Sieve) produced from a standard aggregate sample subjected to 15 blows of a 13.5–14.0 kg hammer dropping from a height of 380 mm.

$$
\text{AIV} = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

**Significance:**
- AIV is an **index of toughness** of the aggregate.
- It indicates the aggregate's **resistance to sudden impact or shock loading** caused by moving traffic.
- It is used to **classify aggregates** for use in different pavement layers (wearing course, base course, sub-base).
- Aggregates with AIV ≤ 30% are generally suitable for bituminous road construction (per IRC).

---

### Question 2: List the apparatus required to conduct the Aggregate Impact Test. `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Aggregate Impact Testing Machine** with base plate, cylindrical cup (102 mm dia × 50 mm depth), vertical guide, and a hammer of mass 13.5–14.0 kg.
2. **Tamping rod** — 12 mm diameter, 800 mm long, rounded at one end.
3. **IS Sieves** — 12.5 mm, 10 mm, and 2.36 mm.
4. **Weighing balance** — accuracy of 0.1 g.
5. **Oven** — thermostatically controlled, capable of maintaining 100–110 °C.
6. **Stopwatch / timer**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 ESE):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the **procedure** for determining the Aggregate Impact Value of a coarse aggregate sample as per **IS 2386 (Part IV) – 1963**. Mention the apparatus, sample preparation, and the test sequence in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an Aggregate Impact Test, the following readings were obtained:
- Weight of aggregate sample, $W_1 = 318.6$ g
- Weight of fines passing 2.36 mm sieve after impact, $W_2 = 65.4$ g

Calculate the **Aggregate Impact Value** and state whether the aggregate is suitable for use in the **wearing course of a bituminous pavement**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

**Apparatus Required:**
- Aggregate Impact Testing Machine with hammer (13.5–14.0 kg, 380 mm drop height).
- Tamping rod (12 mm × 800 mm).
- IS Sieves: 12.5 mm, 10 mm, 2.36 mm.
- Weighing balance (0.1 g accuracy), oven (100–110 °C).

**Test Aggregate:** Aggregate passing **12.5 mm** and retained on **10 mm** IS Sieve.

**Procedure:**

1. **Sample Preparation:** Take about **500 g** of aggregate, wash to remove dust, and **oven-dry** at 100–110 °C for 4 hours. Cool to room temperature.
2. **Sieve Selection:** Sieve the aggregate through 12.5 mm and 10 mm sieves. Retain the fraction **passing 12.5 mm and retained on 10 mm**.
3. **Filling the Cup:** Fill the cylindrical cup in **3 equal layers**, compacting each layer with **25 strokes** of the tamping rod evenly distributed over the surface.
4. **Weighing the Sample (W1):** Strike off the surplus aggregate flush with the top of the cup. Weigh the aggregate in the cup to the nearest **0.1 g** (this is $W_1$).
5. **Mounting the Cup:** Fix the cup firmly in the testing machine on the base.
6. **Applying Impact:** Raise the hammer to a height of **380 mm** and allow it to fall freely. Repeat for **15 blows** at intervals of not less than **1 second** between successive drops.
7. **Sieving After Impact:** Transfer the entire crushed aggregate to a **2.36 mm IS Sieve** and sieve carefully for at least **1 minute**.
8. **Weighing the Fines (W2):** Weigh the fraction **passing** the 2.36 mm sieve to the nearest **0.1 g** (this is $W_2$).
9. **Calculation:** Compute AIV using $\text{AIV} = \dfrac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Given:**
- $W_1 = 318.6$ g
- $W_2 = 65.4$ g

**Formula:**

$$
\text{AIV} = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

**Substitution:**

$$
\text{AIV} = \frac{65.4}{318.6} \times 100
$$

**Calculation:**

$$
\frac{65.4}{318.6} = 0.20527...
$$

$$
\text{AIV} = 0.20527 \times 100 = 20.53\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{AIV} \approx 20.5\%}
$$

**Suitability for Wearing Course:**

For the **wearing course of a bituminous pavement**, the AIV should ideally be **≤ 20%**. The obtained value of **20.5%** is **slightly above** the strict recommended limit. Therefore, the aggregate is **borderline** — it is **just on the verge** of acceptability. It is **marginally unsuitable** for the wearing course and is **more appropriately used in the base course**, where the allowable AIV is up to 30%.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** What is the **significance** of the Aggregate Impact Test in pavement engineering? Discuss the **recommended AIV limits** for different pavement layers as per IS / IRC standards. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The Aggregate Impact Value of a granite aggregate was found to be **18%**, and that of a limestone aggregate was **32%**. Which aggregate would you recommend for:
   1. The wearing course of a national highway.
   2. The base course of a rural road.

Justify your answer with reference to the AIV classification. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**Significance of AIV in Pavement Engineering:**

The Aggregate Impact Test is one of the most important **mechanical property tests** for road aggregates because:

1. **Simulates Real Loading:** It replicates the **sudden impact and shock loads** that aggregates experience under fast-moving traffic, braking forces, and during construction compaction.
2. **Toughness Indicator:** AIV serves as a **direct measure of the toughness** of the aggregate — its ability to resist fracture under dynamic loading.
3. **Quality Control:** It is a quick and inexpensive test that helps **reject substandard materials** at the quarry before they are used in pavement layers.
4. **Layer Selection:** Engineers use AIV values to **decide which pavement layer** (wearing course, base course, or sub-base) a given aggregate is suitable for.
