5. **Correlation with Other Properties:** AIV correlates broadly with **compressive strength, LA abrasion value, and 10% fines value**, making it a useful preliminary screening tool.

**Recommended AIV Limits (IS 2386 / IRC):**

| Pavement Layer | Maximum Permissible AIV |
|---|---|
| **Wearing course (Bituminous)** | ≤ 20% |
| **Base course (Bituminous/WBM)** | ≤ 30% (WBM allows up to 40%) |
| **Sub-base course** | ≤ 40–50% (no strict upper limit) |

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**Given:**
- Granite: AIV = 18%
- Limestone: AIV = 32%

**1. Wearing Course of a National Highway:**

A national highway's wearing course is subjected to the **most severe impact and abrasion** from heavy, high-speed traffic. The recommended AIV for the wearing course is **≤ 20%**.

- **Granite (AIV = 18%)** → falls within the acceptable range (≤ 20%). ✅
- **Limestone (AIV = 32%)** → exceeds the wearing course limit. ❌

> **Recommendation:** **Granite aggregate** should be used for the wearing course of the national highway.

**2. Base Course of a Rural Road:**

A rural road's base course experiences moderate traffic loads. The recommended AIV for the base course is **≤ 30%** (WBM allows up to 40%).

- **Granite (AIV = 18%)** → well within the limit. ✅
- **Limestone (AIV = 32%)** → marginally exceeds the 30% limit but is acceptable for WBM (≤ 40%). ✅ (conditionally)

> **Recommendation:** Either aggregate is acceptable, but **Granite (AIV = 18%)** is still the **superior choice** due to its significantly higher toughness and durability. However, if **economic considerations** (limestone is typically cheaper and more locally available) prevail, **Limestone (AIV = 32%)** is also **acceptable for the base course** under WBM specifications.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Wrong Sieve Size:** Many students write **4.75 mm** instead of **2.36 mm** for the fines sieve. This is a **fatal error** — 2 marks lost immediately.
> 2. **Confusion with Los Angeles Abrasion Test:** The LA test uses a **rotating drum with steel balls**, not a falling hammer. Do not mix up the two.
> 3. **Forgetting the 100 multiplier:** Reporting $\dfrac{W_2}{W_1} = 0.205$ without multiplying by 100 — partial marks only.
> 4. **No Layer Classification in Conclusion:** Simply stating "the aggregate is suitable" without specifying **which layer** loses 1–2 marks. Always link AIV to the **specific pavement layer**.
> 5. **Wrong Aggregate Gradation:** The test uses material **passing 12.5 mm and retained on 10 mm**, not 20 mm or any other size.
> 6. **Unit Errors:** AIV is a **percentage (%)** — writing it as grams or as a ratio is incorrect.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Test Standard:** IS 2386 (Part IV) – 1963.
- ✅ **AIV Formula:** $\text{AIV} = \dfrac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$, where $W_2$ = weight passing 2.36 mm sieve, $W_1$ = original weight.
- ✅ **Hammer:** 13.5–14.0 kg, drop height = 380 mm, **15 blows**.
- ✅ **Sieve for fines:** **2.36 mm IS Sieve** (NOT 4.75 mm).
- ✅ **Aggregate size:** Passing 12.5 mm, retained on 10 mm.
- ✅ **Compaction:** 3 layers × 25 tamping strokes each.
- ✅ **Drying:** Oven-dry at 100–110 °C for 4 hours.
- ✅ **Lower AIV = Tougher aggregate** (inverse relationship).
- ✅ **Wearing course limit:** AIV ≤ 20% (bituminous).
- ✅ **Base course limit:** AIV ≤ 30% (bituminous); ≤ 40% (WBM).
- ✅ **Sub-base course:** No strict limit; AIV up to 40–50% acceptable.
- ✅ **AIV > 30% → REJECT** for road construction.
- ✅ **Unit of AIV:** Dimensionless, expressed as a **percentage (%)**.
- ✅ AIV is an **index of toughness**, related to but not identical to compressive strength.
- ✅ The test does **NOT** measure abrasion resistance — that is the **Los Angeles Abrasion Test**.
- ✅ Always report AIV to **one decimal place** (e.g., 24.1%, not 24%).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Los Angeles Abrasion Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL607 - TRANSPORTATION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Los Angeles Abrasion Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Los Angeles (LA) Abrasion Test** is a standard laboratory test conducted to determine the **resistance of coarse aggregates to abrasion (wearing), impact, and grinding** under a combined action of steel balls and rotating drum, as per **IS:2386 (Part IV) – 1963**, with parallel equivalence in **ASTM C131 / AASHTO T96**.

The test quantifies the **degradation of coarse aggregate** when subjected to a combination of **abrasion, impact, and pulverization** inside a rotating steel drum charged with a specified number of abrasive steel spheres.

The result is expressed as the **Los Angeles Abrasion Value (%)**, which represents the percentage loss in mass of the aggregate after a defined number of revolutions.

$$
\text{LA Abrasion Value (\%)} = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

Where:
- $W_1$ = Initial weight of oven-dry aggregate sample (g)
- $W_2$ = Final weight of aggregate retained on the **1.70 mm (No. 12) IS sieve** after the test (g)
- $W_1 - W_2$ = Loss in weight (material passing 1.70 mm sieve)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The LA Abrasion Test is the **single most important aggregate toughness test** for pavement design. It is mandatory in the mix design of both **flexible (bituminous) and rigid (cement concrete) pavements** as per **IRC:44, MORTH (5th Revision), and IS:383**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Tumble-Dryer Test for Rocks"**

Imagine putting a handful of gravel into a heavy-duty **clothes tumble dryer**, but instead of soft clothes, you also drop in **12 solid steel bowling balls**. You switch it on, and the drum spins about **30 times every minute**. Over time, the rocks bash against each other, the steel balls hammer them, and the wall of the drum grinds them down. The weaker, flaky, or cracked rocks break into dust and tiny chips, while the strong, tough, well-shaped rocks survive almost intact.

At the end of the test, you sieve everything through a fine mesh (the 1.7 mm sieve). The amount of fine dust that **passes through** is the "damage" — and the percentage of original weight lost as dust is the **Abrasion Value**.

- **Low abrasion value** → Rocks survived → **Tough, durable aggregate** → Suitable for high-traffic roads.
- **High abrasion value** → Rocks pulverized → **Weak, soft, or weathered aggregate** → Unsuitable for surface courses.

This directly mirrors what happens in real roads: vehicle tyres (impact) and inter-particle friction (abrasion) continuously hammer and grind the aggregate. The LA test **simulates this destructive action in a few hours** so engineers can predict how the aggregate will behave over years of service.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Standards & Equipment Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Drum internal diameter** | **711 mm ± 5 mm** | IS:2386 Part IV |
| **Drum internal length** | **508 mm ± 5 mm** | IS:2386 Part IV |
| **Drum rotational speed** | **30 – 33 rpm** | IS:2386 Part IV |
| **Standard revolution count** | **500** (Grading A–D) / **1000** (Grading E–G) | IS:2386 Part IV |
| **Steel ball diameter** | **46.0 – 47.6 mm (≈ 48 mm)** | IS:2386 Part IV |
| **Weight of each steel ball** | **390 – 445 g** | IS:2386 Part IV |
| **Charge (number of balls)** | **6, 8, 11, or 12** (depends on grading) | IS:2386 Part IV |
| **Sieve for measuring loss** | **1.70 mm IS Sieve (No. 12)** | IS:2386 Part IV |
| **Total test sample weight** | **5000 g (5 kg)** | IS:2386 Part IV |
| **Total weight of abrasive charge** | **3330 – 5340 g** (depends on grading) | IS:2386 Part IV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** The LA Abrasion Value is a direct indicator of **aggregate hardness, toughness, and resistance to disintegration**. It is a controlling property for aggregate acceptance in:
> - **Surface course (bituminous concrete):** LA Value < **30%** (MORTH)
> - **Wearing course / surface dressing:** LA Value < **30%**
> - **Granular base course (WBM):** LA Value < **40%**
> - **Cement concrete pavement (coarse aggregate):** LA Value < **30% – 35%** (IS:383)
> - **Railway ballast:** LA Value < **25% – 30%**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The test is built on a **deceptively simple physical principle**: combine three destructive forces — **impact**, **abrasion**, and **grinding** — simultaneously on a known mass of aggregate, then measure how much material disintegrates.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Sample Preparation (Pre-Test):**
   - Oven-dry the aggregate at **100 °C – 110 °C** until constant mass (typically 4–6 hours).
   - Select a grading (A, B, C, D, E, F, or G) based on **maximum nominal size** of aggregate.
   - Weigh **5000 g ± 10 g** of graded aggregate.

2. **Charging the Drum:**
   - Place the **oven-dry aggregate sample** inside the LA drum.
   - Add the **correct number and total mass of steel abrasive charge** for the chosen grading.

3. **Rotation Phase (The Test):**
   - Rotate the drum at **30 – 33 rpm** for the specified number of revolutions (**500 or 1000**).
   - During rotation, the **lifting shelf (internal baffle)** carries the charge up to a certain height, then drops it. This produces:
     - **Impact force** (falling steel balls + aggregate)
     - **Abrasion force** (sliding/pulverizing action between particles and drum wall)
     - **Grinding force** (comminution between particles)

4. **Post-Test Sieving:**
   - Discharge the contents carefully.
   - Sieve the entire material through the **1.70 mm IS sieve** using a mechanical sieve shaker.
   - Wash the fines if necessary and re-oven-dry.
   - Weigh the **material retained on 1.70 mm sieve** ($W_2$).

5. **Calculation:**
   - Apply the LA Abrasion formula to obtain percentage loss.

---

### 2.2 Grading System — Selection Logic

The IS code specifies **7 different gradings** depending on the maximum size of aggregate. The choice of grading controls **how many balls** and **how many revolutions** are used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule (KTU & IS:2386):**
> Choose the grading that matches the **nominal maximum size of aggregate** and the **pavement layer** in which it will be used. Most undergraduate labs and field mix designs use **Grading A, B, or C** (500 revolutions).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Form | Units | Application / Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LA Abrasion Value | $\%L = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ | % | Main result; **lower is better** |
| 2 | Weight loss | $\Delta W = W_1 - W_2$ | g | Material passing 1.7 mm sieve |
| 3 | Weight retained | $W_2 = W_1 - \dfrac{\%L \times W_1}{100}$ | g | Used for back-calculation |
| 4 | Drum speed | $N = 30 \text{ to } 33 \text{ rpm}$ | rev/min | Constant per IS code |
| 5 | Drum diameter | $D = 711 \pm 5$ | mm | Standardised |
| 6 | Drum length | $L = 508 \pm 5$ | mm | Standardised |
| 7 | Total revolutions (A–D) | $R = 500$ | rev | For standard gradings |
| 8 | Total revolutions (E–G) | $R = 1000$ | rev | For harder, larger aggregates |
| 9 | Time of test ($R=500$) | $t = 500 / 30 \approx 16.67$ | min | At 30 rpm |
| 10 | Time of test ($R=1000$) | $t = 1000 / 30 \approx 33.33$ | min | At 30 rpm |
| 11 | Steel ball mass (each) | $m_b = 390 \text{ to } 445$ | g | Per ball |
| 12 | Total ball mass | $M_b = n_b \times m_b$ | g | $n_b$ = number of balls |
| 13 | Acceptability (bituminous) | $\%L < 30$ | % | MORTH (surface course) |
| 14 | Acceptability (WBM) | $\%L < 40$ | % | Granular base course |
| 15 | Acceptability (PCC) | $\%L < 30 \text{ to } 35$ | % | IS:383 (coarse aggregate) |
| 16 | Acceptability (rail ballast) | $\%L < 25$ | % | IRS-GE-1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Safeguard Note:** All absolute-value / magnitude symbols in the formulas above are rendered as plain text or inline expressions to avoid breaking the markdown table layout. For example, the "$\pm 5$ mm" tolerance is written as $\pm 5$ with a backslash-escaped `±` symbol — never as a raw pipe symbol.

---

### 2.4 Grading Reference Table (IS:2386 Part IV)

| Grading | Sieve Range (mm) | Sample Mass (g) | No. of Balls | Total Ball Mass (g) | Revolutions |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 12.5 – 10 (2500 g) + 10 – 6.3 (2500 g) | 5000 | 12 | 5000 ± 25 | 500 |
| **B** | 19 – 12.5 (2500 g) + 12.5 – 9.5 (2500 g) | 5000 | 11 | 4584 ± 25 | 500 |
| **C** | 12.5 – 9.5 (2500 g) + 9.5 – 6.3 (2500 g) | 5000 | 8 | 3330 ± 20 | 500 |
| **D** | 6.3 – 4.75 (5000 g) | 5000 | 6 | 2500 ± 15 | 500 |
| **E** | 25 – 19 (2500 g) + 19 – 12.5 (2500 g) | 5000 | 12 | 5000 ± 25 | 1000 |
| **F** | 19 – 12.5 (5000 g) | 5000 | 12 | 5000 ± 25 | 1000 |
| **G** | 12.5 – 9.5 (2500 g) + 9.5 – 6.3 (2500 g) | 5000 | 12 | 5000 ± 25 | 1000 |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The LA Abrasion Test is not an academic exercise — it is a **gate-keeper test** that determines whether a quarry can supply aggregate for a road project. Its outcomes drive:

1. **Pavement Mix Design** — aggregate with LA > 35% is rejected outright for surface courses.
2. **Cost Engineering** — softer aggregates may be abundant locally but require stabilization, increasing project cost.
3. **Service Life Prediction** — empirical correlations link LA Abrasion Value to **rutting, ravelling, and surface degradation** rates.
4. **International Trade Specification** — most national highway codes (AASHTO, ASTM, IS, BS EN 1097-2) require LA testing for **every aggregate source** before approval.
5. **Railway Engineering** — ballast degradation is heavily correlated with LA Value, affecting tamping cycles and track geometry retention.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the LA Abrasion Value Formula

The percentage abrasion loss is fundamentally a **mass ratio scaled to 100%**. Below is the complete derivation with every step explicitly shown.

**Given:**

Let $M_i$ = initial oven-dry mass of aggregate sample (g), placed inside the LA drum.

$$
M_i = W_1 = 5000 \text{ g (standard)}
$$

**Step 1 — Physical Setup After Test:**

After $N$ revolutions, the aggregate has been pulverized. The drum content is discharged. Sieving through the **1.70 mm IS sieve** yields two distinct fractions:

- **Coarse fraction (retained on 1.70 mm sieve):** $W_2$ (g) — the surviving aggregate
- **Fines fraction (passing 1.70 mm sieve):** $W_f$ (g) — the abraded material

**Step 2 — Conservation of Mass:**

By conservation of mass (no aggregate is added or removed other than the fines that pass through the sieve; assuming negligible dust loss to the environment during the test), the mass balance is:

$$
W_1 = W_2 + W_f
$$

**Step 3 — Define Abrasion Loss as a Mass Ratio:**

The "abrasion loss" is the proportion of material that has disintegrated into fines:

$$
\text{Loss ratio} = \frac{W_f}{W_1}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $W_f$ Using the Mass Balance:**

From Step 2:

$$
W_f = W_1 - W_2
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{Loss ratio} = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Percentage:**

By definition, percentage is the ratio multiplied by 100:

$$
\text{LA Abrasion Value (\%)} = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

**Final Form:**

$$
\boxed{\;\%L = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100\;}
$$

**Units Check:**

- Numerator: $W_1 - W_2$ is in **grams (g)**
- Denominator: $W_1$ is in **grams (g)**
- Ratio is **dimensionless** → multiplied by 100 → expressed as **%**

✓ Units are consistent.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 1 (Grading A — 5000 g Sample)

> **Problem:** A 5000 g oven-dry sample of crushed granite aggregate (Grading A) is subjected to the LA Abrasion Test. After 500 revolutions, the material retained on the 1.70 mm sieve weighs 3760 g. Calculate the LA Abrasion Value and comment on the suitability of the aggregate for a bituminous wearing course.

**Given Data:**

$$
W_1 = 5000 \text{ g}, \quad W_2 = 3760 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the weight loss:**

$$
\Delta W = W_1 - W_2 = 5000 - 3760 = 1240 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the LA Abrasion Value formula:**

$$
\%L = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100 = \frac{1240}{5000} \times 100
$$

$$
\%L = 0.248 \times 100 = 24.80\%
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

- **LA Abrasion Value = 24.80%**
- MORTH acceptance limit for bituminous wearing course: **< 30%**
- Since **24.80% < 30%**, the aggregate **IS ACCEPTABLE** for use in a bituminous wearing course.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The aggregate is **suitable** with a comfortable safety margin of 5.2%.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 2 (Grading B — Back-Calculation)

> **Problem:** A bituminous surface dressing project specifies that the LA Abrasion Value of the aggregate must be **less than 28%**. If the initial sample mass is 5000 g and the final retained mass on 1.70 mm sieve is 3650 g, determine whether the supplied aggregate meets the specification.

**Given Data:**

$$
W_1 = 5000 \text{ g}, \quad W_2 = 3650 \text{ g}, \quad \%L_{\text{allowable}} < 28\%
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the percentage loss:**

$$
\%L = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100 = \frac{5000 - 3650}{5000} \times 100
$$

$$
\%L = \frac{1350}{5000} \times 100 = 27.00\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with specification:**

Since **27.00% < 28%**, the aggregate **MEETS** the specification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Decision:** Aggregate is **ACCEPTED** for surface dressing. However, the margin is only 1.0% — borderline. KTU examiners often test whether students notice this narrow margin.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem 3 (Time Calculation for 1000 Revolutions at 33 rpm)

> **Problem:** For a Grading E aggregate test, calculate the total test duration (in minutes) if the drum rotates at 33 rpm for 1000 revolutions. Also, compute the total mass of the abrasive charge (in grams) if 12 steel balls of 420 g each are used.

**Step 1 — Time calculation:**

$$
t = \frac{R}{N} = \frac{1000 \text{ rev}}{33 \text{ rev/min}}
$$

$$
t = 30.30 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 2 — Total abrasive charge mass:**

$$
M_b = n_b \times m_b = 12 \times 420 = 5040 \text{ g}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Check:** The total ball mass (5040 g) is within the IS:2386 tolerance of **5000 g ± 25 g** for Grading E/F/G — ✓ acceptable.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Aggregate Acceptance

| LA Abrasion Value (%) | Suitability Rating | Recommended Application |
|---|---|---|
| **< 10** | **Excellent** | Airport runways, premium highways, high-speed corridors |
| **10 – 20** | **Very Good** | National highways, expressways, major arterial roads |
| **20 – 30** | **Good** | Bituminous surface course, dense graded bituminous macadam (DBM/BC) |
| **30 – 35** | **Fair** | Bituminous base course only, NOT recommended for surface |
| **35 – 40** | **Marginal** | Granular base course (WBM), low-volume rural roads |
| **40 – 50** | **Poor** | Subbase course, fill material, NOT for bound layers |
| **> 50** | **Reject** | Aggregate source disqualified for any pavement layer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** Students often confuse the boundaries (30% vs 35%). Memorize the three magic numbers — **30, 35, 40** — for surface, base, and subbase respectively.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid node IDs below are **purely alphanumeric** (e.g., `n1`, `n2`, `compA`, `stepA`) and prefixed with letters to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts. All node labels use **clean uppercase alphanumeric text** (no bold, italics, or HTML inside labels).

---

### 4.2 Figure A — Cross-Sectional Schematic of the LA Abrasion Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stepA["OVEN DRY AGGREGATE SAMPLE 5000 G"] --> stepB["PLACE SAMPLE INSIDE HOLLOW STEEL DRUM 711 MM X 508 MM"]
    stepB --> stepC["ADD SPECIFIED STEEL BALL CHARGE 6 TO 12 BALLS 390 TO 445 G EACH"]
    stepC --> stepD["ROTATE DRUM AT 30 TO 33 RPM VIA HORIZONTAL SHAFT"]
    stepD --> stepE{"REVOLUTION COUNT"}
    stepE --> stepF["500 REVOLUTIONS FOR GRADING A B C D"]
    stepE --> stepG["1000 REVOLUTIONS FOR GRADING E F G"]
    stepF --> stepH["DISCHARGE DRUM CONTENTS"]
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI["SIEVE THROUGH 1.70 MM IS SIEVE NO 12"]
    stepI --> stepJ["WEIGH RETAINED MATERIAL W2"]
    stepJ --> stepK["COMPUTE PERCENT LOSS W1 MINUS W2 DIVIDED BY W1 TIMES 100"]
    stepK --> stepL["LA ABRASION VALUE PERCENT"]
```

---

### 4.3 Figure B — Functional Block Diagram of Test Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    compA["HOLLOW STEEL DRUM DIAMETER 711 MM"] --> compB["INTERNAL LIFTING SHELF BAFFLE"]
    compA --> compC["HORIZONTAL ROTATION SHAFT"]
    compC --> compD["GEAR MOTOR DRIVE 30 TO 33 RPM"]
    compA --> compE["STEEL BALLS 46 TO 47.6 MM DIAMETER"]
    compA --> compF["AGGREGATE SAMPLE 5000 G GRADING A B C D E F G"]
    compB --> compG["IMPACT FORCE BALL DROP FROM LIFTING SHELF"]
    compE --> compG
    compF --> compG
    compG --> compH["ABRASION AND GRINDING ACTION INSIDE DRUM"]
    compH --> compI["DISCHARGE PORT WITH COVER"]
    compI --> compJ["FINES PASSING 1.70 MM SIEVE WEIGHT W1 MINUS W2"]
    compI --> compK["COARSE RETAINED ON 1.70 MM SIEVE WEIGHT W2"]
```

---

### 4.4 Figure C — Test Workflow Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{"WHAT IS THE MAX NOMINAL SIZE OF AGGREGATE?"}
    q1 --> r1["20 TO 25 MM CHOOSE GRADING E OR F"]
    q1 --> r2["10 TO 20 MM CHOOSE GRADING A OR B OR C"]
    q1 --> r3["BELOW 10 MM CHOOSE GRADING D OR C"]
    r1 --> s1["USE 12 BALLS 1000 REVOLUTIONS"]
    r2 --> s2["USE 8 TO 12 BALLS 500 REVOLUTIONS"]
    r3 --> s3["USE 6 TO 8 BALLS 500 REVOLUTIONS"]
    s1 --> t1["ROTATE TEST AND SIEVE"]
    s2 --> t1
    s3 --> t1
    t1 --> u1{"LA VALUE COMPARED WITH MORTH LIMITS"}
    u1 --> v1["BELOW 30 PERCENT ACCEPTED FOR SURFACE COURSE"]
    u1 --> v2["30 TO 40 PERCENT SUITABLE FOR BASE COURSE ONLY"]
    u1 --> v3["ABOVE 40 PERCENT REJECTED OR SUITABLE FOR SUBBASE"]
```

---

### 4.5 Figure D — Pictorial Representation (ASCII Schematic)

```
   +--------------------------------------------------------+
   |               LOS ANGELES ABRASION MACHINE            |
   |                                                        |
   |        0000    0000    0000    0000                    |
   |       0    0  0    0  0    0  0    0     <-- Steel Balls
   |      0  *  0 0  *  0 0  *  0 0  *  0    <-- Aggregate  |
   |       0    0  0    0  0    0  0    0                   |
   |        0000    0000    0000    0000                    |
   |             ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~                            |
   |            ~~~~~LIFTING~~~~~~      <-- Internal Shelf  |
   |           ~~~~~SHELF~~~~~~~~~~                        |
   |                                                        |
   |  <==== HORIZONTAL ROTATION SHAFT (30-33 RPM) =======>  |
   +--------------------------------------------------------+
         |                                                |
         |         Drum: 711 mm dia x 508 mm length        |
         +------------------------------------------------+
                          |
                          v
                 SIEVE THROUGH 1.70 mm
                 /                \
        COARSE (W2)            FINES (W1 - W2)
        Retained               Passing
```

---

### 4.6 Equipment Photograph Reference (Lab Identification Guide)

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical University Lab Setup (KTU Affiliated Colleges):**
> - The machine is a **horizontal cylindrical drum** mounted on a robust steel frame.
> - A **sliding cover** with a quick-release clamp seals the opening during testing.
> - A **digital revolution counter** is mounted on the front panel.
> - The drum is supported by **two trunnion rollers** and rotated by a **gear-reduced electric motor** (typically 1 HP).
> - A **hand hole cover** allows safe charging and discharging of aggregate and steel balls.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, S3 Civil]**
> **Define the Los Angeles Abrasion Test. State the standard code, the diameter and length of the testing drum, and the number of revolutions for Grading A.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Los Angeles Abrasion Test is a laboratory test conducted to determine the **resistance of coarse aggregates to abrasion, impact, and grinding** when subjected to a rotating steel drum loaded with abrasive steel balls. It is standardized under **IS:2386 (Part IV) – 1963**.

- **Drum internal diameter:** **711 mm** (tolerance ± 5 mm)
- **Drum internal length:** **508 mm** (tolerance ± 5 mm)
- **Number of revolutions for Grading A:** **500 revolutions**
- **Drum speed:** 30 – 33 rpm

[1 mark for definition, 1 mark for standard code, 1 mark for dimensions and rpm]

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, S3 Civil]**
> **State the formula for Los Angeles Abrasion Value. List the maximum permissible LA Abrasion Values for (i) bituminous wearing course and (ii) granular subbase course as per MORTH specifications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Formula:**

$$
\text{LA Abrasion Value (\%)} = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100
$$

**MORTH Permissible Limits:**

- **(i) Bituminous wearing course:** LA Abrasion Value **< 30%**
- **(ii) Granular subbase course:** LA Abrasion Value **≤ 60%** (typically, with relaxed criteria for lower layers)

> [1 mark for formula, 1 mark for wearing course value, 1 mark for subbase value]

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer **either Option A or Option B** (full 14 marks).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, S5 Civil]**
> **(a)** Describe the **procedure for determining the Los Angeles Abrasion Value** of a coarse aggregate sample, including sample preparation, equipment, and grading selection. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** In a Los Angeles Abrasion Test conducted on a 5000 g sample of crushed stone aggregate (Grading B), the material retained on the 1.70 mm IS sieve after the test weighed **3540 g**. Calculate the LA Abrasion Value and **comment on the suitability** of the aggregate for use in (i) a bituminous wearing course, and (ii) a water-bound macadam (WBM) base course. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Sample Collection:** Obtain a representative sample of coarse aggregate weighing at least 15 kg. **[0.5 marks]**
2. **Drying:** Oven-dry the sample at 100 °C – 110 °C to constant mass. **[0.5 marks]**
3. **Sieve Analysis & Grading Selection:** Sieve the aggregate and select the appropriate grading (A, B, C, D, E, F, or G) based on the **nominal maximum size** of the aggregate. For example, for 20 mm aggregate, Grading B is commonly used. **[1 mark]**
4. **Sample Weighing:** Weigh **5000 g ± 10 g** of the graded aggregate. **[0.5 marks]**
5. **Charging:** Place the aggregate inside the clean LA drum. Add the **abrasive charge** of steel balls as per the chosen grading (e.g., 11 balls of ~417 g each for Grading B, total ≈ 4584 g). **[1 mark]**
6. **Rotation:** Close the drum cover securely. Rotate the drum at **30 – 33 rpm** for the specified number of revolutions (**500 for Grading B**). **[1 mark]**
7. **Discharge:** After the revolutions are complete, stop the machine. Discharge the entire contents (aggregate + balls) onto a clean tray. **[0.5 marks]**
8. **Sieving:** Separate the balls using a magnet or manual picking. Sieve the aggregate through the **1.70 mm IS sieve** using a mechanical sieve shaker for at least 2 minutes. **[1 mark]**
9. **Weighing:** Weigh the material **retained on the 1.70 mm sieve** to the nearest 1 g. This is $W_2$. **[0.5 marks]**
10. **Calculation & Reporting:** Apply the LA Abrasion formula and report the result as a percentage. **[0.5 marks]**

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**

$$
W_1 = 5000 \text{ g}, \quad W_2 = 3540 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 1 — Weight loss:**

$$
\Delta W = W_1 - W_2 = 5000 - 3540 = 1460 \text{ g} \quad \text{[\textbf{1 mark}]}
$$

**Step 2 — LA Abrasion Value:**

$$
\%L = \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100 = \frac{1460}{5000} \times 100
$$

$$
\%L = 0.292 \times 100 = 29.20\% \quad \text{[\textbf{2 marks}]}
$$

**Step 3 — Suitability Check:**

- **(i) Bituminous wearing course (MORTH limit: < 30%):** 29.20% < 30% → **ACCEPTABLE** for wearing course (margin = 0.80%). **[1.5 marks]**
- **(ii) WBM base course (MORTH limit: < 40%):** 29.20% < 40% → **ACCEPTABLE** for WBM base. **[1 mark]**

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners' comment:** The aggregate is technically acceptable for both layers, but the safety margin is **very narrow for the wearing course**. KTU evaluators often appreciate a remark about this borderline nature. **[Bonus 0.5 marks]**

**Final Answer:** LA Abrasion Value = **29.20%**; suitable for both applications (with a cautionary note on the narrow margin).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, S5 Civil]**
> **(a)** Explain the **significance of the Los Angeles Abrasion Test** in pavement engineering. List the factors that affect the test result and discuss how grading selection influences the outcome. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** During a quality check, two aggregate samples from different quarries are tested:
> - **Sample X:** Initial weight = 5000 g, Retained weight on 1.70 mm sieve = 3850 g
> - **Sample Y:** Initial weight = 5000 g, Retained weight on 1.70 mm sieve = 4100 g
>
> Compute the LA Abrasion Values and identify which aggregate is **more suitable for a national highway wearing course** (limit < 30%). Justify your answer with engineering reasoning. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Significance & Factors (7 marks):**

**Significance of LA Abrasion Test:**

1. **Aggregate Quality Assessment:** It is the most widely used test to evaluate the **toughness and abrasion resistance** of coarse aggregates used in road construction. **[1 mark]**
2. **Pavement Layer Selection:** The LA value directly determines the **layer of pavement** in which an aggregate can be used (surface, base, or subbase). **[1 mark]**
3. **Service Life Prediction:** Aggregates with low LA values resist polishing, abrasion, and impact, leading to **longer pavement life** and reduced maintenance. **[1 mark]**
4. **Cost-Effectiveness:** Rejecting poor aggregates at the source saves **massive rehabilitation costs** later. **[0.5 marks]**
5. **Standardization:** It is mandatory under **IS:2386, MORTH, IRC, and AASHTO** specifications for all major highway projects. **[0.5 marks]**

**Factors Affecting the Test Result:**

1. **Grading of aggregate** (A, B, C, etc.) — different gradings use different ball charges and revolutions. **[0.5 marks]**
2. **Number of steel balls and their total mass** — more balls → more impact → higher abrasion. **[0.5 marks]**
3. **Number of revolutions** — 1000 revs is more severe than 500 revs. **[0.5 marks]**
4. **Hardness and mineralogy of aggregate** — limestone abrades more than granite. **[0.5 marks]**
5. **Particle shape (flaky/elongated)** — flaky particles break faster. **[0.5 marks]**
6. **Moisture content at the time of test** — wet samples give slightly different results. **[0.5 marks]**

**Part (b) — Comparative Numerical Analysis (7 marks):**

**Sample X:**

$$
\%L_X = \frac{5000 - 3850}{5000} \times 100 = \frac{1150}{5000} \times 100 = 23.00\% \quad \text{[\textbf{1.5 marks}]}
$$

**Sample Y:**

$$
\%L_Y = \frac{5000 - 4100}{5000} \times 100 = \frac{900}{5000} \times 100 = 18.00\% \quad \text{[\textbf{1.5 marks}]}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Sample X | Sample Y | Acceptance Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial weight $W_1$ | 5000 g | 5000 g | — |
| Retained weight $W_2$ | 3850 g | 4100 g | — |
| Weight loss $\Delta W$ | 1150 g | 900 g | — |
| **LA Abrasion Value** | **23.00%** | **18.00%** | < 30% |

**Suitability for National Highway Wearing Course:**

- Both samples satisfy the MORTH limit of **< 30%**.
- **Sample Y is more suitable** because:
  - Lower LA value (18.00% vs 23.00%) → **better toughness and durability**. **[1 mark]**
  - Higher safety margin (30 - 18 = 12% vs 30 - 23 = 7%) → **better for high-traffic corridors**. **[1 mark]**
  - Expected to provide **longer service life** with lower maintenance. **[0.5 marks]**
  - **Engineering reasoning:** National highways carry heavy commercial traffic with high tyre pressures; aggregate with the lower abrasion value will better resist tyre-induced polishing and disintegration. **[0.5 marks]**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** **Sample Y (LA = 18.00%) is the preferred aggregate** for the national highway wearing course, with Sample X (LA = 23.00%) as an acceptable but inferior alternative. **[1 mark]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU Board Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion in Formula:** Writing $(W_1 - W_2) \div W_1 \times 100$ without the % symbol in the final answer → **0.5 mark cut**. Always state "**LA Abrasion Value = X.XX %**".
> 2. **Wrong Grading-Revolution Pairing:** Some students apply 1000 revolutions to Grading A by mistake. **Memorize:** A–D = 500 rev; E–G = 1000 rev. → **1 mark cut** if mis-stated.
> 3. **Confusing the Sieve Size:** Students often write "1.75 mm" or "1.18 mm" sieve. **Correct answer: 1.70 mm IS Sieve (No. 12)**. → **0.5 mark cut** for wrong sieve.
> 4. **Forgetting to Oven-Dry:** Skipping the drying step loses **0.5 mark** in procedure questions.
> 5. **Ignoring MORTH Limits:** Computing the LA value but failing to comment on acceptability → **up to 2 marks lost** in 7-mark problems.
> 6. **Sign Convention Errors:** Writing negative values or values > 100% due to sign mix-up in the formula → **full 0 on the numerical part**.
> 7. **No Internal Choice Justification:** In 14-mark questions, examiners expect explicit **"A is preferable because..."** or **"B is acceptable because..."** — not just a number dump.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ **Full name:** Los Angeles Abrasion Test (often abbreviated as **LA Abrasion Test** or **LAAV**).
- ✅ **Standard:** **IS:2386 (Part IV) – 1963**; also ASTM C131, AASHTO T96, BS EN 1097-2.
- ✅ **Purpose:** Determines **resistance of coarse aggregate to abrasion, impact, and grinding**.
- ✅ **Drum dimensions:** 711 mm diameter × 508 mm length (think **"71 cm × 51 cm"**).
- ✅ **Drum speed:** 30 – 33 rpm.
- ✅ **Standard sample weight:** **5000 g** (5 kg).
- ✅ **Steel balls:** 46.0 – 47.6 mm diameter, 390 – 445 g each, 6 to 12 balls.
- ✅ **Number of revolutions:** 500 (Grading A–D) or 1000 (Grading E–G).
- ✅ **Sieve for measuring loss:** **1.70 mm IS Sieve (No. 12)** — the gold standard.
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $\%L = \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1} \times 100$.
- ✅ **MORTH Acceptance Limits (memorize!):**
  - Bituminous surface course: **< 30%**
  - WBM/Granular base: **< 40%**
  - Subbase: **≤ 60%** (or as specified)
- ✅ **Lower LA value = tougher aggregate = better quality**.
- ✅ **Grading A** = 12 balls + 500 revs (most common in labs).
- ✅ **Grading D** = 6 balls + 500 revs (for aggregates < 6.3 mm).
- ✅ **Grading E, F, G** = 12 balls + 1000 revs (for harder, larger stones).
- ✅ **Test duration at 30 rpm:** ~16.67 min (500 revs) or ~33.33 min (1000 revs).
- ✅ **Time saving tip in exam:** always mention the **MORTH limit comparison** for full marks in suitability questions.
- ✅ **Mnemonic to remember limits:** **"Surface 30, Base 40, Subbase 60"** = **SBS (S-Base-Sub)**.
- ✅ **Common mistake to avoid:** NEVER forget to oven-dry the sample before initial weighing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aggregate Crushing Value Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCEL607 - TRANSPORTATION ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Aggregate Crushing Value Test # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV) Test** is a standard laboratory test prescribed by **IS 2386 (Part IV) - 1963** that quantitatively measures the **resistance of coarse aggregate to crushing under a gradually applied compressive load**. The ACV is defined as the **percentage of fines (material passing the 2.36 mm IS sieve) generated when a standard aggregate specimen is subjected to a standardized load of 40 tonnes applied over 10 minutes**. Mathematically expressed as: $$\text{Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV)} = \frac{\text{Mass of fraction passing 2.36 mm sieve}}{\text{Total mass of the test specimen}} \times 100$$ The test is fundamentally a **mechanical durability index** used to ascertain the suitability of aggregates for use in **flexible and rigid pavement construction**, particularly for **base course, sub-base course, and bituminous macadam layers**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Imagine squeezing a bag of marbles under a hydraulic press.** When you release the pressure, some marbles would have been crushed into powder, some would have broken into fragments, and the strongest ones would remain intact. The **Aggregate Crushing Value Test** is essentially the same experiment, but conducted under highly controlled laboratory conditions using standardized rock chips instead of marbles. A **lower ACV percentage** means the aggregate is **stronger and tougher** — only a small fraction broke under the 40-tonne load. A **higher ACV** indicates the aggregate is **weaker** and will crush easily under traffic wheel loads, making it unsuitable for high-stress pavement layers. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standardized Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > The following parameters are **IS 2386 (Part IV) — 1963** standardized values and must be memorized as board-exam constants: > > - **Standard Test Load:** **40 tonnes (≈ 400 kN)** > - **Load Application Rate:** **4 tonnes per minute (uniform)** > - **Total Loading Duration:** **10 minutes** > - **Specimen Size:** Aggregate passing **12.5 mm** IS sieve and retained on **10 mm** IS sieve > - **Cylindrical Mould Internal Dimensions:** Diameter **15.0 cm**, Height **13.5 cm** (with detachable base plate) > - **Tamping Rod:** Straight steel rod of **16 mm diameter**, **45–60 cm length**, with one end rounded into a **bullet nose** > - **Final Sieve for Fines:** **2.36 mm** IS sieve > - **Tamping per Layer:** **25 strokes** distributed uniformly > - **Number of Layers for Filling Mould:** **3 equal layers** ## 1.4 KTU Syllabus Significance This experiment falls under the **"Strength Characteristics of Aggregates"** cluster in Module 2 of the Transportation Engineering Lab. The ACV test, along with its sibling tests (Aggregate Impact Value, Los Angeles Abrasion Value, and 10% Fines Value), forms the **backbone of aggregate acceptance criteria** for all road and airfield pavement design exercises in subsequent theory courses such as **PCCET601 — Highway & Traffic Engineering**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Basis of the Test The ACV test operates on the fundamental engineering principle that **compressive failure in granular aggregates is dominated by the weakest grain boundaries, micro-cracks, and mineral cleavage planes**. When a standard 40-tonne load is applied: 1. **Inter-particle contact points** experience extreme stress concentrations (theoretical contact stresses can reach **hundreds of MPa**, far exceeding the bulk aggregate compressive strength). 2. **Brittle fracture** initiates at the **weakest planes of weakness** within the aggregate particle. 3. Fines (< 2.36 mm) are generated through a combination of **crushing, splitting, and abrasion** mechanisms. 4. The **mass of fines generated is proportional to the overall mechanical weakness** of the aggregate sample. > [!NOTE] > **Why does the test specify only 12.5 mm to 10 mm size?** This narrow size range eliminates the **size-effect variable** in crushing strength. Larger aggregates have different failure modes (more chipping at edges) while smaller aggregates have too many contact points. The 10–12.5 mm range gives **reproducible, comparable results** across different aggregate sources. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula / Value | Units | Remarks | |---|-----------|-----------------|-------|---------| | 1 | Aggregate Crushing Value | $ACV = \dfrac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ | % | $W_1$ = total dry mass; $W_2$ = mass passing 2.36 mm | | 2 | Standard Applied Load | $L = 40$ | tonnes (or 400 kN) | IS 2386 (Part IV) | | 3 | Loading Rate | $r = 4$ | tonnes/minute | Must be uniform | | 4 | Loading Duration | $t = 10$ | minutes | After load is fully applied, hold 10 min | | 5 | Specimen Mass | $W_1 \approx 2500$ to $3000$ g | grams | Depends on specific gravity | | 6 | Maximum ACV for Surface Course | $ACV_{max} = 30$ | % | Bituminous wearing course | | 7 | Maximum ACV for Base Course | $ACV_{max} = 45$ | % | Granular / WBM base | | 8 | Mould Volume | $V = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (15.0)^2 \times 13.5$ | cm³ | Internal volume of mould | | 9 | Aggregate Bulk Density in Mould | $\rho = \dfrac{W_1}{V}$ | g/cm³ | Indicates compaction quality | | 10 | Crushing Strength (theoretical) | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{400{,}000}{\pi (75)^2}$ | N/mm² (MPa) | ~ 22.6 MPa nominal contact stress | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Memory Trick:** The four key aggregate strength tests share a "rule of 40": > - ACV → Load = **40 tonnes** > - AIV → Blows = **15** (not 40, but related family) > - LA Abrasion → 500 revolutions + 1000 revolutions > - 10% Fines Value → Load to produce 10% fines > > Always remember **ACV = 40 tonnes, 10 minutes, 2.36 mm sieve**. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The ACV test is **non-negotiable** in the following real-world engineering scenarios: 1. **IRC (Indian Roads Congress) Specifications:** IRC:111-2009 and MORTH (Ministry of Road Transport & Highways) specifications mandate ACV testing for acceptance of coarse aggregates in **bituminous concrete, dense graded macadam, and WBM (Water Bound Macadam)**. 2. **Quality Control at Crushing Plants:** Aggregate producers run daily ACV tests to certify that their **crushed stone product** meets highway project specifications. 3. **Comparison of Aggregate Sources:** Engineers use ACV values to **rank quarry sources** when multiple suppliers are available. 4. **Airfield Pavement Design:** For runway base courses, even stricter ACV thresholds (often $\leq 25\%$) are enforced by ICAO/AAI guidelines. 5. **Pavement Failure Investigation:** Forensic engineers often reproduce ACV tests on **reclaimed aggregate from failed pavements** to identify whether inferior aggregate strength contributed to distress. ## 2.4 Relationship with Other Aggregate Strength Tests > [!NOTE] > **ACV vs. AIV (Aggregate Impact Value):** Both are quasi-static/dynamic compressive indices, but ACV is **slower (quasi-static, 10 minutes)**, while AIV is **instantaneous impact**. AIV is generally **higher numerically** than ACV for the same aggregate because dynamic loads are more destructive. > > **ACV vs. Los Angeles Abrasion:** LAA test combines **abrasion + impact** in a rotating steel drum with steel balls, simulating **combined traffic and weathering stresses**. LAA values are typically **higher** than ACV for the same aggregate. > > **ACV vs. 10% Fines Value (TFV):** TFV is a **direct strength measure** (load to produce exactly 10% fines), whereas ACV is a **deformation measure** (fines produced at fixed 40-tonne load). TFV is preferred for high-quality aggregates with ACV < 10%. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Procedure & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Step-by-Step Laboratory Procedure > [!IMPORTANT] > The following 12-step procedure is the **canonical IS 2386 (Part IV) protocol** expected verbatim (or near-verbatim) in KTU lab record books and ESE viva-voce answers. ### Step 1 — Specimen Preparation - Dry the aggregate sample in an **oven at 100–110 °C** for **4 hours** until constant mass. - Allow to cool to room temperature in a **desiccator** (to prevent moisture re-absorption). - Sieve the aggregate through a **12.5 mm IS sieve** → discard the fraction passing. - Sieve the over-size through a **10 mm IS sieve** → discard the fraction retained. - **Retain the fraction passing 12.5 mm and retained on 10 mm** as the test specimen. ### Step 2 — Filling the Cylindrical Mould - Place the cylindrical mould (15 cm dia × 13.5 cm height) on its **detachable base plate**. - Fill the mould with the prepared aggregate in **3 equal layers**. - **Compact each layer with 25 strokes** of the **16 mm tamping rod**, distributing strokes uniformly across the surface. - After the **3rd layer**, strike off the top surface level with the **straightedge of the tamping rod** so that the mould is filled exactly to its rim. ### Step 3 — Weighing the Test Specimen - Weigh the mould + base plate + compacted aggregate → $M_1$ (g). - Empty the aggregate, clean the mould, and weigh mould + base plate alone → $M_2$ (g). - Calculate the **net mass of the test specimen**: $$W_1 = M_1 - M_2$$ ### Step 4 — Mounting in the Compression Testing Machine - Place the **mould with the specimen and base plate** centrally on the **lower platen** of the **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** or **Compression Testing Machine (CTM)**. - Position the upper platen to make firm contact with the top of the aggregate. - **Critical alignment:** Ensure the load axis is **perfectly vertical and coaxial** with the mould axis to prevent eccentric loading. ### Step 5 — Load Application - Apply the load **uniformly at a rate of 4 tonnes per minute** (i.e., ~ 4 t/min ≈ 6.67 kN/s). - The total target load is **40 tonnes (≈ 400 kN)**. - Total time to reach full load: **10 minutes**. - The reading on the load dial must be **continuously monitored**. ### Step 6 — Load Release - After the full **40-tonne load has been applied**, **hold for a brief moment** to allow full stress distribution, then **release the load gradually** to zero. - Carefully remove the mould from the CTM. ### Step 7 — Separation and Sieving - Carefully **transfer the entire crushed aggregate** from the mould onto a clean tray. - Sieve the entire crushed material through the **2.36 mm IS sieve** for at least **2 minutes** (or until no further material passes). - **Weigh the fraction that passes 2.36 mm** (the fines) → $W_2$ (g). ### Step 8 — Calculation of ACV $$\text{ACV} (\%)= \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$$ ### Step 9 — Reporting - Report the ACV to the **nearest whole number** (or to one decimal place if required by lab manual). - Mention the **aggregate source, type (crushed/uncrushed/gravel), and visual description** (e.g., "granite, angular, grey"). ### Step 10 — Repeatability Check - Conduct the test on **at least two specimens** from the same sample. - The **mean of two consistent readings** (within ± 2% of each other) is reported as the representative ACV. ### Step 11 — Clean-Up - Clean the mould, tamping rod, and sieves thoroughly. - Return all apparatus to designated storage. ### Step 12 — Safety Sign-Off - Sign the lab record, get it counter-signed by the faculty in-charge. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Format > [!NOTE] > Below is the **archetypal KTU calculation problem** that appears in lab record viva, semester practical exams, and ESE module questions. ### Solved Numerical Example **Problem Statement:** In an Aggregate Crushing Value test conducted on a sample of crushed granite aggregate, the following observations were recorded: - Mass of mould + base plate + compacted aggregate, $M_1 = 6420$ g - Mass of empty mould + base plate, $M_2 = 3920$ g - Mass of fines passing 2.36 mm sieve after crushing, $W_2 = 712$ g **Determine:** (a) The Aggregate Crushing Value of the sample. (b) State whether the aggregate is suitable for use in:     (i) Bituminous surface course     (ii) Water Bound Macadam (WBM) base course ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Calculate the total mass of the test specimen.** $$W_1 = M_1 - M_2 = 6420 - 3920 = 2500 \text{ g}$$ **Step 2: Identify the mass of fines generated.** $$W_2 = 712 \text{ g}$$ **Step 3: Apply the ACV formula.** $$\text{ACV} = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$$ **Step 4: Substitute the numerical values.** $$\text{ACV} = \frac{712}{2500} \times 100$$ **Step 5: Simplify.** $$\text{ACV} = 0.2848 \times 100 = 28.48\%$$ **Step 6: Round off to nearest whole number for KTU reporting.** $$\boxed{\text{ACV} \approx 28\%}$$ **Step 7: Interpretation against standard specifications.** | Application | Maximum Permissible ACV | Computed ACV | Verdict | |-------------|------------------------|--------------|---------| | Bituminous surface course | $\leq 30\%$ | $28\%$ | ✅ **Suitable** | | WBM base course | $\leq 45\%$ | $28\%$ | ✅ **Suitable** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Inference:** The granite aggregate is **moderately strong** and can be used in both surface and base course applications, but its use in heavy-traffic National Highway surface courses warrants additional **Los Angeles Abrasion** and **Aggregate Impact Value** tests for complete characterization. ## 3.3 Generalization — Multi-Layer Compacted Density Derivation The **bulk density of the aggregate in the mould** is computed as: $$\rho_{bulk} = \frac{W_1}{V_{mould}}$$ where the mould volume is: $$V_{mould} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times d^2 \times h$$ Substituting $d = 15.0$ cm, $h = 13.5$ cm: $$V_{mould} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (15.0)^2 \times 13.5$$ $$V_{mould} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 225 \times 13.5$$ $$V_{mould} = 0.7854 \times 3037.5$$ $$V_{mould} \approx 2385.4 \text{ cm}^3$$ For the example above: $$\rho_{bulk} = \frac{2500}{2385.4} \approx 1.048 \text{ g/cm}^3$$ The **theoretical nominal contact stress** under 40-tonne load is: $$\sigma_c = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{400{,}000 \text{ N}}{\pi \times (75 \text{ mm})^2}$$ $$\sigma_c = \frac{400{,}000}{17671.5}$$ $$\sigma_c \approx 22.6 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 22.6 \text{ MPa}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Important Distinction:** The 22.6 MPa is the **average** stress over the mould cross-section. The **actual inter-particle contact stresses** are **orders of magnitude higher** (locally 100s of MPa) because stress is concentrated at the limited number of particle-to-particle contact points. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the ACV Test Setup The following block diagram maps the **complete physical and functional interaction** of the ACV test apparatus and procedure: ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Aggregate Stockpile] --> B[Sieve 12.5 mm IS] B --> C[Sieve 10 mm IS] C --> D[Retained Fraction 10 to 12.5 mm
Specimen Preparation] D --> E[Oven Drying
100 to 110 degrees Celsius, 4 hours] E --> F[Cooling in Desiccator] F --> G[Cylindrical Mould 15 cm dia x 13.5 cm height
with Detachable Base Plate] G --> H[Layer 1 Fill + 25 Tamping Strokes] H --> I[Layer 2 Fill + 25 Tamping Strokes] I --> J[Layer 3 Fill + 25 Tamping Strokes] J --> K[Strike off Top Surface
Weigh Specimen W1] K --> L[Place Mould in CTM
Universal Testing Machine] L --> M[Apply Load 4 t per minute
up to 40 tonnes in 10 minutes] M --> N[Release Load Gradually] N --> O[Transfer Crushed Aggregate to Tray] O --> P[Sieve through 2.36 mm IS Sieve] P --> Q[Weigh Fines Passing 2.36 mm
Mass W2] Q --> R[Compute ACV percent
ACV = W2 over W1 times 100] R --> S{Interpretation
ACV less than 30 percent
equals Surface Course OK} S --> S1[ACV less than 30 percent
Suitable for Bituminous Surface Course] S --> S2[ACV between 30 and 45 percent
Suitable for Base Course Only] S --> S3[ACV greater than 45 percent
Reject for Road Use] ``` ## 4.2 Apparatus Layout Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Apparatus_Set direction TB Node1[Cylindrical Mould
15 cm dia x 13.5 cm height
Steel Construction] Node2[Detachable Base Plate
Bolted to Mould Bottom] Node3[Tamping Rod
16 mm dia x 45 to 60 cm length
Bullet Nose Tip] Node4[Compression Testing Machine
Capacity 100 to 200 tonnes
Load Dial and Platen] Node5[IS Sieve Set
12.5 mm, 10 mm, 2.36 mm] Node6[Weighing Balance
Accuracy 0.1 g] Node7[Oven 100 to 110 degrees Celsius] Node8[Desiccator with Silica Gel] end Node1 --> Node4 Node3 --> Node1 Node5 --> Node1 Node6 --> Node1 Node7 --> Node1 Node8 --> Node1 ``` ## 4.3 Decision Logic Flow — Aggregate Acceptance ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Start: ACV Test Completed
Value Computed] --> Eval{ACV percent} Eval -->|ACV less than 20| A1[Excellent Quality
Suitable for High Stress
Heavy Traffic Surface] Eval -->|ACV 20 to 30| A2[Good Quality
Suitable for Normal Bituminous
Surface Course] Eval -->|ACV 30 to 45| A3[Fair Quality
Suitable for Base Course
and Sub base Only] Eval -->|ACV greater than 45| A4[Poor Quality
Reject for Pavement Use
Consider for Lean Concrete Only] A1 --> End[Decision Logged in Lab Record] A2 --> End A3 --> End A4 --> End ``` > [!NOTE] > The threshold values in the decision diagram above are simplified for **engineering teaching purposes** and align with MORTH/IRC specifications for general road construction. Project-specific specifications may impose stricter limits for **National Highway, Expressway, or Runway** applications. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Define Aggregate Crushing Value. State the standard load and loading rate as per IS 2386 (Part IV).** **Model Answer:** > **Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV)** is defined as the percentage of fines (material passing the 2.36 mm IS sieve) generated when a standard aggregate specimen (passing 12.5 mm and retained on 10 mm) is subjected to a compressive load under specified test conditions. > > As per **IS 2386 (Part IV) — 1963**: > - **Standard test load:** 40 tonnes (≈ 400 kN) > - **Loading rate:** 4 tonnes per minute (uniform) > - **Total loading duration:** 10 minutes --- > **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — December 2023] **State the recommended maximum ACV values for (i) bituminous surface course and (ii) WBM base course aggregate. Why is a lower ACV preferred?** **Model Answer:** > Recommended maximum ACV values per **MORTH / IRC specifications**: > > - (i) **Bituminous surface course:** $\leq 30\%$ > - (ii) **Water Bound Macadam (WBM) base course:** $\leq 45\%$ > > A **lower ACV is preferred** because it indicates that the aggregate has higher resistance to crushing under traffic wheel loads, which translates to better **load-bearing capacity**, **lower pavement deformation**, and **longer service life** of the road. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern) > **Q3A.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice] > > **(a)** [7 marks] Describe with a neat sketch the **apparatus and procedure** for determining the Aggregate Crushing Value of a coarse aggregate sample. State the relevant IS code. > > **(b)** [7 marks] The following observations were taken during an ACV test on a sample of crushed basalt: > - Mass of mould + aggregate (compacted) = 6.85 kg > - Mass of empty mould = 4.12 kg > - Mass of fines passing 2.36 mm sieve after crushing = 760 g > > Compute the ACV and comment on its suitability for: > (i) Bituminous concrete surface course > (ii) Granular sub-base ### Model Solution for Q3A #### Part (a) — Apparatus and Procedure **Relevant IS Code:** **IS 2386 (Part IV) — 1963** **Apparatus Required:** 1. **Cylindrical mould** — internal diameter 15.0 cm, internal height 13.5 cm, with a detachable base plate (steel construction). 2. **Tamping rod** — straight steel rod of 16 mm diameter, 45–60 cm long, with one end rounded into a bullet nose. 3. **Compression Testing Machine (CTM)** — capable of applying 40 tonnes load, with a uniform loading rate of 4 tonnes/min. 4. **IS Sieves** — 12.5 mm, 10 mm, and 2.36 mm. 5. **Weighing balance** — accuracy 0.1 g. 6. **Oven** — thermostatically controlled at 100–110 °C. 7. **Desiccator** — for cooling dried samples. **Procedure (Step-by-Step):** 1. Obtain aggregate passing 12.5 mm and retained on 10 mm IS sieve. 2. Oven-dry the aggregate at 100–110 °C for 4 hours; cool in desiccator. 3. Place the cylindrical mould on its base plate; fill in **3 equal layers**, tamping each layer with **25 strokes** of the 16 mm tamping rod. 4. Strike off the surface level with the tamping rod; weigh the mould + aggregate = $M_1$. 5. Empty the mould, weigh the empty mould + base plate = $M_2$. 6. Net specimen mass $W_1 = M_1 - M_2$. 7. Place the mould with specimen in the CTM; apply load uniformly at **4 t/min** until reaching **40 tonnes** in 10 minutes. 8. Release load; transfer crushed aggregate to a tray; sieve through **2.36 mm** IS sieve. 9. Weigh the fraction passing 2.36 mm = $W_2$. 10. **ACV (%) = (W₂ / W₁) × 100**. #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Given:** - $M_1 = 6.85$ kg $= 6850$ g - $M_2 = 4.12$ kg $= 4120$ g - $W_2 = 760$ g **Step 1: Compute net specimen mass.** $$W_1 = M_1 - M_2 = 6850 - 4120 = 2730 \text{ g}$$ **Step 2: Compute ACV.** $$\text{ACV} = \frac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100 = \frac{760}{2730} \times 100$$ $$\text{ACV} = 0.2784 \times 100 = 27.84\%$$ $$\boxed{\text{ACV} \approx 28\%}$$ **Step 3: Suitability Assessment.** | Application | Permissible ACV | Computed ACV | Verdict | |-------------|-----------------|--------------|---------| | (i) Bituminous concrete surface course | $\leq 30\%$ | $28\%$ | ✅ **Suitable** | | (ii) Granular sub-base | $\leq 45\%$ | $28\%$ | ✅ **Suitable** | **Conclusion:** The crushed basalt aggregate with ACV of 28% is **acceptable for both surface and sub-base applications**. --- > **Q3B.** [KTU University Exam — December 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice — Alternative] > > **(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **significance** of the Aggregate Crushing Value test in highway engineering. Discuss how ACV varies with **(i) aggregate type**, **(ii) aggregate size**, and **(iii) moisture content**. > > **(b)** [7 marks] Two aggregates, **A** (limestone) and **B** (laterite), were tested for ACV with the following results: > - Aggregate A: $W_1 = 2500$ g, $W_2 = 425$ g > - Aggregate B: $W_1 = 2600$ g, $W_2 = 1040$ g > > Compute and compare their ACVs. Which aggregate is more suitable for a heavily-trafficked National Highway surface course? Justify. ### Model Solution for Q3B #### Part (a) — Significance of ACV Test **Significance in Highway Engineering:** The ACV test is a fundamental **strength index** for coarse aggregates used in pavement construction. Its significance includes: 1. **Quality Assurance:** It certifies that aggregate delivered to the project site meets the minimum strength requirements of MORTH/IRC specifications. 2. **Pavement Design Input:** It influences the **structural layer coefficient** used in flexible pavement design (IRC:37) and the **effective elastic modulus** in rigid pavement design (IRC:58). 3. **Source Selection:** Engineers use ACV to **compare multiple quarry sources** and select the most suitable aggregate for the project. 4. **Performance Prediction:** Aggregates with low ACV exhibit **lower rutting, lower fatigue cracking, and longer pavement life** under repeated traffic loading. 5. **Forensic Diagnosis:** ACV retesting of in-service aggregate helps identify causes of premature pavement failures. **Variation of ACV:** **(i) With Aggregate Type:** - **Igneous rocks (granite, basalt):** Low ACV (15–30%) → strong, preferred for surface courses. - **Metamorphic rocks (gneiss, quartzite):** Moderate ACV (20–35%) → generally suitable. - **Sedimentary rocks (limestone, sandstone):** Higher ACV (25–45%) → less preferred, may be unsuitable for high-traffic surface courses. - **Laterite and weathered aggregates:** Highest ACV (often > 45%) → typically rejected for bound layers. **(ii) With Aggregate Size:** - The test standardizes size at 10–12.5 mm to eliminate size-effects. - **Larger aggregates** (e.g., 20 mm) have **lower ACV** because of fewer contact points and lower stress concentration. - **Smaller aggregates** have **higher ACV** due to more inter-particle contact points. - Hence, the 10–12.5 mm fraction gives a **conservative (worst-case)** estimate of aggregate crushing strength. **(iii) With Moisture Content:** - **Dry aggregate:** ACV is lowest (most representative baseline value). - **Saturated surface-dry (SSD) aggregate:** ACV increases marginally. - **Wet aggregate (with free water):** ACV increases by 5–15% due to **pore-water pressure** reducing effective inter-particle friction and facilitating fracture propagation. - Hence, IS 2386 mandates oven-dry specimens for reproducible results. #### Part (b) — Numerical Comparison **Given Data:** | Aggregate | $W_1$ (g) | $W_2$ (g) | |-----------|-----------|-----------| | A (Limestone) | 2500 | 425 | | B (Laterite) | 2600 | 1040 | **Step 1: Compute ACV for Aggregate A.** $$\text{ACV}_A = \frac{425}{2500} \times 100 = 17.0\%$$ **Step 2: Compute ACV for Aggregate B.** $$\text{ACV}_B = \frac{1040}{2600} \times 100 = 40.0\%$$ **Step 3: Comparison and Recommendation.** | Property | Aggregate A (Limestone) | Aggregate B (Laterite) | |----------|------------------------|------------------------| | ACV | $17\%$ | $40\%$ | | Strength | Strong | Weak | | Suitability for NH surface course ($\leq 30\%$) | ✅ Suitable | ❌ Not suitable | **Conclusion:** - **Aggregate A (limestone)** is **more suitable** for the heavily-trafficked National Highway surface course. Its ACV of 17% is **well within the permissible 30%** limit, indicating strong resistance to crushing under heavy traffic. - **Aggregate B (laterite)** with ACV of 40% **exceeds the 30% threshold** for surface course and is also **borderline for base course (max 45%)**. It should be **rejected for the surface course** and may be considered for **sub-base or low-volume road applications only**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Converting kg to g **consistently** is critical. Forgetting to convert $M_1$ and $M_2$ from kg to g is the most common error. > 2. **Wrong Sieve Used:** Students often write "2.36 mm" as "2.36 cm" or "0.236 mm". Always state the **IS sieve designation correctly**. > 3. **No Repetition:** ACV should be the **mean of two consistent specimens**. Single-test results are not accepted. > 4. **No Justification:** When asked "suitable or not?", the student must **state the permissible limit** and then give a clear Yes/No verdict with a single-line justification. > 5. **Forgetting IS Code:** Always cite **IS 2386 (Part IV) — 1963** in the procedure section. Examiners specifically look for this citation. > 6. **Conflating ACV with AIV:** Aggregate Crushing Value (40 t load) ≠ Aggregate Impact Value (15 blows). These are **two different tests**. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for ACV Test** - [x] **Full name:** Aggregate Crushing Value Test - [x] **IS Code:** IS 2386 (Part IV) — 1963 - [x] **Standard Load:** 40 tonnes (400 kN) - [x] **Loading Rate:** 4 tonnes per minute - [x] **Loading Duration:** 10 minutes - [x] **Specimen Size:** Passing 12.5 mm, retained on 10 mm - [x] **Mould:** 15.0 cm dia × 13.5 cm height - [x] **Tamping Rod:** 16 mm dia, 45–60 cm long, bullet nose - [x] **Layers:** 3 equal layers - [x] **Tamping per layer:** 25 strokes - [x] **Fines Sieve:** 2.36 mm IS sieve - [x] **Formula:** $ACV = \dfrac{W_2}{W_1} \times 100$ - [x] **Surface Course Limit:** $\leq 30\%$ - [x] **Base Course Limit:** $\leq 45\%$ - [x] **Lower ACV = Stronger Aggregate** - [x] **Higher ACV = Weaker Aggregate** - [x] **Unit Conversions:** 1 tonne = 1000 kg = 10 kN - [x] **Nominal Contact Stress:** ~ 22.6 MPa - [x] **Report Precision:** Nearest whole number (or one decimal place) - [x] **Comparison Hierarchy:** ACV (lowest) < LAA < AIV (highest) for same aggregate - [x] **Moisture Effect:** ACV increases with moisture → use oven-dry specimens - [x] **Always cite IS Code** in the answer > **Final Examiner's Note:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme lab examination, the ACV test question is almost always asked in **Part B with internal choice**. The question is typically split into (a) a descriptive procedure/apparatus question worth 7 marks and (b) a numerical calculation worth 7 marks. Memorize the **40 tonnes / 4 t/min / 10 minutes / 2.36 mm** quartet — these four numbers alone can fetch 4–5 marks even in a poorly-structured answer.

topics.title: Shape Test: Angularity Number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL607 - TRANSPORTATION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Shape Test: Angularity Number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Angularity Number** is defined (as per **IS 2386 – Part I, 1963**) as *"the amount by which the percentage of voids in a coarse aggregate, compacted in a specified manner, exceeds **33%**."*

Mathematically, it quantifies the **angularity, sharpness, and lack of roundness** of the individual particles of a coarse aggregate. A higher numerical value indicates a more **angular / flaky / elongated** particle shape, while a value close to **zero** indicates a well-rounded aggregate (e.g., river gravel).

The reference constant **33 %** represents the theoretical minimum void content that a perfectly rounded (spherical) single-sized aggregate will exhibit even after compaction — any deviation above this baseline is attributed to the **angular geometry** of the particles locking against each other.

| Property | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Angularity Number – Rounded Gravel | $\mathrm{AN}$ | 0 – 2 |
| Angularity Number – Crushed Stone (Normal) | $\mathrm{AN}$ | 5 – 8 |
| Angularity Number – Highly Angular (Crushed Rock) | $\mathrm{AN}$ | 9 – 12 |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Marble Jar"**
> Imagine filling a transparent jar with **marbles** (perfectly round). When shaken, the marbles settle tightly and the empty space between them is *minimum*. Now, replace the marbles with **broken pieces of a brick** (angular). When poured and tamped into the same jar, the sharp edges and corners **interlock**, leaving far more empty space — even though the jar is "full". The *extra emptiness* you see in the brick-filled jar, **over and above the marble baseline**, is exactly the **Angularity Number** expressed as a percentage.

## 1.3 Standard Reference & Governing Code

- **Bureau of Indian Standard:** **IS 2386 (Part I) – 1963** — Methods of Test for Aggregates for Concrete (Particle Size and Shape).
- **IRC / MORT&H** specifications further prescribe acceptance limits for aggregates used in **WBM, WMM, and bituminous layers** based on the angularity number.

## 1.4 Why is Angularity Number Tested in Transportation Engineering?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **flexible pavements** (bituminous roads), the **skeleton of crushed aggregates** carries the wheel load. Angular particles **interlock mechanically** and provide:
> - **Higher Marshall Stability** of bituminous mixes.
> - **Improved resistance to rutting** and permanent deformation.
> - **Better shear strength** in granular sub-base and base courses (WBM, WMM).
>
> Rounded aggregates (low AN), although easy to compact, tend to **slip and shift** under traffic — leading to rutting. Hence, **MORTH (Section 400/500)** mandates a *minimum angularity index* for aggregates used in the **binder course and wearing course**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Basis — The Mechanics of Voids

When a single-sized coarse aggregate is placed in a rigid cylindrical measure and compacted by **tamping in three layers (25 strokes per layer, with a 16 mm rod)**, the resulting bulk density depends on:

1. **Particle geometry** — angular particles bridge and form larger void pockets.
2. **Surface texture** — rough surfaces increase frictional resistance and prevent sliding.
3. **Particle packing** — single-sized fractions cannot fill the inter-particle voids (no fines are present).
4. **Compaction energy** — standardized tamping defines a reference energy input.

Since the test uses **single-sized aggregate** (no fines), the **only variable** governing the void content is the **shape of the particles**. This isolation makes the test a direct measure of angularity.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Test Concept

- **Step 1:** Fill a known volume $V$ of a cylinder with single-sized coarse aggregate (e.g., 20 mm – 16 mm fraction).
- **Step 2:** Compact in **3 equal layers**, each tamped **25 times** with a **16 mm × 600 mm** rounded tamping rod.
- **Step 3:** Strike off the top surface flush with the rim and weigh the aggregate. Obtain mass $m$.
- **Step 4:** Compute the **bulk density** $\rho_b$ of the compacted aggregate.
- **Step 5:** Compute the **apparent (solid) density** using the **specific gravity $G$** of the aggregate.
- **Step 6:** Compute the **percentage of voids** in the compacted aggregate.
- **Step 7:** Subtract the **reference constant 33** from the percentage voids to get the **Angularity Number**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following is the *complete formula set* required to solve any KTU 2024 Scheme problem on Angularity Number. Memorize these — they appear in nearly every Part-B variant.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bulk Density | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | $\mathrm{g/cm^3}$ or $\mathrm{kg/m^3}$ |
| 2 | Apparent (Solid) Density | $\rho_s = G \cdot \rho_w$ | $\mathrm{g/cm^3}$ |
| 3 | Volume of Solids | $V_s = \dfrac{m}{G \cdot \rho_w}$ | $\mathrm{cm^3}$ |
| 4 | Volume of Voids | $V_v = V - V_s$ | $\mathrm{cm^3}$ |
| 5 | Percentage Voids | $\%V_v = \left(\dfrac{V_v}{V}\right)\cdot 100$ | $\%$ |
| 6 | Percentage Voids (alt form) | $\%V_v = \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{G \cdot \rho_w}\right)\cdot 100$ | $\%$ |
| 7 | **Angularity Number** | $\mathbf{AN = \%V_v - 33}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Equivalent direct form | $AN = 67 - \left(\dfrac{m}{G \cdot \rho_w \cdot V}\right)\cdot 100$ | dimensionless |

Where:
- $m$ = mass of aggregate filling the cylinder (in grams)
- $V$ = volume of the cylindrical measure (in $\mathrm{cm^3}$)
- $G$ = **specific gravity** of the aggregate
- $\rho_w$ = density of water = **$1\ \mathrm{g/cm^3}$**
- **33** = reference constant (in %) for spherical particle baseline

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Angularity Number directly influences:
> - **Marshall Mix Design (MORTH Sec. 500):** Voids in Mineral Aggregate (VMA) is controlled partly by the angularity of coarse aggregate.
> - **WBM / WMM (MORTH Sec. 400):** Interlocking of angular fragments gives the layer its load-bearing capacity.
> - **Selection of crusher type:** Jaw / Cone / VSI crushers produce aggregates of differing AN; engineers select crusher geometry to meet specification.
> - **Rutting prediction:** A low AN aggregate is often blended with crushed fines to "lock" the matrix in bituminous layers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedure, Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Apparatus Required (per IS 2386 Part I)

| S.No. | Equipment | Specification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cylindrical metal measure | Capacity **3 litres** (typically **15 cm dia × 17 cm ht**) |
| 2 | Tamping rod | **16 mm dia**, **600 mm long**, one end rounded (bullet-nose) |
| 3 | Weighing balance | Accuracy **± 0.5 g** |
| 4 | Straight edge / scrapper | For levelling the top surface |
| 5 | IS Sieves | For obtaining single-sized fraction (e.g., **20 mm and 16 mm**) |
| 6 | Thermometer / oven | For moisture control (sample tested in **SSD condition**) |

## 3.2 Sample / Material Requirement

- The aggregate is tested in **single-sized fractions** (not graded). Common fractions:
  - 20.0 mm – 16.0 mm
  - 16.0 mm – 12.5 mm
  - 12.5 mm – 10.0 mm
  - 10.0 mm – 6.3 mm
- The sample must be in **oven-dry** or **saturated surface-dry (SSD)** condition.

## 3.3 Detailed Test Procedure

> [!NOTE]
> This is the procedure examiners expect you to write verbatim in a KTU viva or 14-mark descriptive question.

1. **Prepare the single-sized fraction** by sieving the coarse aggregate through a pair of IS sieves (e.g., 20 mm and 16 mm). Discard the undersize and oversize.
2. **Weigh the empty cylindrical measure** $m_1$ (in grams).
3. **Fill the measure in three equal layers** with the single-sized aggregate.
4. **Tamp each layer 25 times** with the 16 mm tamping rod. Distribute the strokes uniformly across the surface. Allow the rod to penetrate only into the layer being compacted (do not hit the bottom hard).
5. After the third layer is tamped, **strike off the surface** flush with the rim using a straight edge so the measure is *level-full*.
6. **Weigh the measure + aggregate** $m_2$ (in grams).
7. Determine the **specific gravity $G$** of the aggregate by standard pycnometer method (as per IS 2386 Part III) — or use the value supplied in the question.
8. Apply the formulas below to compute the **Angularity Number**.

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

### Given Data
- Volume of cylindrical measure, $V = 3000\ \mathrm{cm^3}$
- Mass of empty measure, $m_1 = 2150\ \mathrm{g}$
- Mass of measure + compacted aggregate, $m_2 = 6650\ \mathrm{g}$
- Specific gravity of aggregate, $G = 2.70$
- Density of water, $\rho_w = 1.00\ \mathrm{g/cm^3}$

### Step 1 — Mass of Aggregate

$$
m = m_2 - m_1
$$

$$
m = 6650 - 2150 = 4500\ \mathrm{g}
$$

### Step 2 — Bulk Density of Compacted Aggregate

$$
\rho_b = \frac{m}{V}
$$

$$
\rho_b = \frac{4500}{3000} = 1.50\ \mathrm{g/cm^3}
$$

### Step 3 — Apparent (Solid) Density of Aggregate

$$
\rho_s = G \cdot \rho_w
$$

$$
\rho_s = 2.70 \times 1.00 = 2.70\ \mathrm{g/cm^3}
$$

### Step 4 — Volume of Solids in the Cylinder

$$
V_s = \frac{m}{G \cdot \rho_w}
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{4500}{2.70 \times 1.00} = 1666.67\ \mathrm{cm^3}
$$

### Step 5 — Volume of Voids in the Cylinder

$$
V_v = V - V_s
$$

$$
V_v = 3000 - 1666.67 = 1333.33\ \mathrm{cm^3}
$$

### Step 6 — Percentage of Voids

$$
\% V_v = \left(\frac{V_v}{V}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\% V_v = \left(\frac{1333.33}{3000}\right) \times 100 = 44.44\ \%
$$

### Step 7 — Angularity Number

$$
AN = \% V_v - 33
$$

$$
\boxed{AN = 44.44 - 33 = 11.44}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** An Angularity Number of **11.44** indicates a **highly angular, crushed-stone type** aggregate with excellent interlocking characteristics — highly suitable for **WBM, WMM, and bituminous binder/wearing courses** in road construction.

## 3.5 Equivalent Short-Cut Derivation (Show in Exam for Marks)

Combining the formulas directly:

$$
AN = \left[ 1 - \frac{m}{G \cdot \rho_w \cdot V} \right] \times 100 - 33
$$

$$
AN = 67 - \left( \frac{m}{G \cdot \rho_w \cdot V} \right) \times 100
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
AN = 67 - \left( \frac{4500}{2.70 \times 1.00 \times 3000} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
AN = 67 - \left( \frac{4500}{8100} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
AN = 67 - 55.56 = 11.44
$$

Both methods yield the same result — examiners accept either, but the **short-cut form is faster** in the exam hall.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Influence of Parameters on AN

| Parameter | If Value Increases | Effect on AN | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass of aggregate $m$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ (AN decreases) | More solid volume → fewer voids |
| Specific gravity $G$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ (AN increases slightly) | For same mass, solid volume is less → more voids reported |
| Cylinder volume $V$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | Larger cylinder has more total voids for same mass |
| Aggregate shape (rounded → angular) | $\uparrow$ angularity | $\uparrow$ (AN increases) | Angular particles bridge and interlock, creating extra voids |
| Tamping effort (over-compaction) | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | More voids collapse; reportable AN drops |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Apparatus Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Coarse Aggregate Stockpile] --> B[IS Sieve 20 mm]
    B --> C[Retained on 20 mm Discarded]
    B --> D[Passing 20 mm]
    D --> E[IS Sieve 16 mm]
    E --> F[Passing 16 mm Discarded]
    E --> G[Retained on 16 mm Single Sized Fraction 20 to 16 mm]
    G --> H[Oven Dried or SSD Sample]
    H --> I[Fill Cylindrical Measure 3 Litres in 3 Layers]
    I --> J[Layer 1 Tamp 25 strokes with 16 mm rod]
    I --> K[Layer 2 Tamp 25 strokes]
    I --> L[Layer 3 Tamp 25 strokes]
    L --> M[Strike off surface flush with straight edge]
    M --> N[Weigh Cylinder plus Aggregate = m2]
    N --> O[Subtract empty cylinder mass m1]
    O --> P[Compute Bulk Density m divided by V]
    P --> Q[Use Specific Gravity G from Pycnometer Test]
    Q --> R[Compute Percentage Voids]
    R --> S[Subtract Reference Constant 33]
    S --> T[Final Angularity Number]
```

## 4.2 Procedural Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        I1[Empty Cylinder Mass m1]
        I2[Sample Mass m after compaction]
        I3[Specific Gravity G from lab]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE[Computation Stage]
        C1[Compute Bulk Density]
        C2[Compute Apparent Density]
        C3[Compute Percentage Voids]
        C4[Subtract 33 to get AN]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Stage]
        O1[Angularity Number AN]
        O2[Classification Round Crushed or Highly Angular]
    end

    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C1
    I2 --> C3
    I3 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> O1 --> O2
```

## 4.3 Test Setup — Schematic Description (Verbal Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Metal Cylinder 15 cm dia x 17 cm ht, 3 L capacity] --> B[Standing on flat rigid floor]
    C[Tamping Rod 16 mm dia x 600 mm long, bullet-nose tip] --> D[Operated manually, 25 strokes per layer]
    E[Coarse Aggregate Single size 20 to 16 mm] --> A
    D --> A
    F[Weighing Balance 0.5 g accuracy] --> G[Records m1 and m2]
    G --> H[Calculated Angularity Number]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

### Q1. Define Angularity Number. Mention the relevant IS code for its determination.
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Angularity Number** of a coarse aggregate is defined (as per **IS 2386 Part I – 1963**) as the amount by which the percentage of voids in the aggregate, when compacted in a prescribed manner, **exceeds 33 %**. Mathematically,
> $$\mathrm{AN} = \%V_v - 33.$$
> It is a numerical index of particle angularity — higher values indicate sharper / more angular aggregates.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

### Q2. Why is angularity number considered an important property of aggregates used in road construction?
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
