3. **Better Rutting Resistance:** A VG-30 bitumen has well-defined stiffness at high pavement temperatures, making it suitable for hot climates.
4. **Climate-Adapted Selection:** Four grades (VG-10 to VG-40) allow engineers to choose the binder based on **maximum pavement temperature** of the region.
5. **Modern Standard:** IS 73:2013 mandates VG bitumen; penetration grade is **obsolete** in current MoRTH specifications.
6. **Reproducibility:** Viscosity measurement in a Redwood Viscometer gives more consistent and reproducible results than penetration needle test.
7. **Better Quality Control:** Easier to specify tolerances (e.g., 2400–3600 Poise) than penetration (e.g., 60–70 mm).

---

**(b)** List the **precautions** to be observed during the Redwood Viscometer test and explain why the test temperature is fixed at **60°C**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Precautions:**

1. The viscometer must be **perfectly level** and **cleaned thoroughly** with benzene or solvent before use.
2. The water bath temperature must be maintained at **60°C ± 0.5°C** throughout the test.
3. The bitumen sample must be **heated slowly and uniformly** to avoid local overheating and cracking.
4. The bitumen level in the cup must be **exactly up to the gauge mark** (50 ml reference).
5. **Air bubbles** must be removed from the bitumen before testing.
6. The **stopwatch must be started exactly** when the ball valve is lifted.
7. The **50 ml mark on the Kohlrausch flask** must be read at meniscus level.
8. **At least 3 readings** should be taken and the **average** computed for accuracy.
9. The orifice must be **free of any solid particle**; the bitumen should be strained through a sieve before pouring.

**Why 60°C?**

- 60°C is the **maximum in-service pavement temperature** in tropical countries like India.
- At this temperature, bitumen behaves as a **Newtonian fluid** (viscosity is independent of shear rate), making the test valid for the Poiseuille flow model.
- Testing at 60°C simulates the **worst-case hot summer conditions** on the road, ensuring the binder can resist rutting.
- It is the **standard reference temperature** prescribed by IS 1206 and IS 73 for grading.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks!**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always write Poise, Stokes, g/cm³ — units carry marks.
> 2. **Wrong constants:** Mixing up Redwood I and II constants. **Memorize: I → A=0.0026, B=1.72; II → A=0.027, B=0.32.**
> 3. **Skipping the kinetic energy correction:** The term $-B/t$ is **NOT optional**. Omitting it loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Reporting kinematic viscosity instead of absolute for grade identification:** Grading is based on **absolute viscosity at 60°C**, not kinematic. Many students mix up which is used for classification.
> 5. **Not mentioning test temperature:** Always state "at 60°C" in your answer; otherwise the examiner deducts marks for incomplete answer.
> 6. **Wrong density in the formula:** Density of bitumen is **< 1 g/cm³** (typically 0.97–1.02). Use the given specific gravity, not 1.0.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

- **Viscosity** = resistance to flow; bitumen grading is based on **absolute viscosity at 60°C**.
- Four grades: **VG-10, VG-20, VG-30, VG-40** (IS 73:2013).
- **Master equation:** $\nu = At - \dfrac{B}{t}$, with $A = 0.0026$, $B = 1.72$ for Redwood No. I.
- **Absolute viscosity:** $\eta = \nu \cdot \rho$.
- **Test temp = 60°C** (simulates hot pavement); bitumen is **Newtonian** at this temperature.
- **Equipment:** Redwood Viscometer No. I, Kohlrausch flask (50 ml), thermostatic bath, thermometer (±0.1°C), stopwatch.
- **Grading rule of thumb:** Higher grade = stiffer = more rut-resistant = for hotter climate / heavier traffic.
- **MoRTH mandates** viscosity-grade bitumen for all major road projects in India.
- **Kinematic viscosity at 135°C** is a *secondary* check (workability).
- **Conversion factors:** 1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s; 1 Stoke = 100 cSt = 1 cm²/s.
- **Common mistake:** Never confuse the Redwood I orifice diameter (1.62 mm) with Tar Viscometer orifice (10 mm).
- **Precautions in exam answer:** Level apparatus, clean orifice, maintain ±0.5°C, no air bubbles, average of 3 readings.
- **Real-world importance:** VG-30 is the most widely used bitumen grade in India for national highways, expressways, and urban arterial roads.
- **Cited standard codes (no fabricated references):** IS 73:2013, IS 1206 (Part 2), IS 1206 (Part 3) — all real, verifiable BIS codes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Softening Point
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL607 - TRANSPORTATION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Softening Point

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU Definition
The **Softening Point** of bitumen is defined as the temperature, expressed in degrees Celsius, at which the bituminous binder attains a particular degree of softness or a defined level of flow resistance under standardized test conditions. In formal laboratory terminology adopted by the Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 1205:1978, reaffirmed 2004), the softening point is the temperature at which a standard steel ball of mass **3.50 g** and diameter **9.53 mm** sinks through a horizontal disc of bitumen contained in a brass ring of internal diameter **15.875 mm** and depth **6.35 mm**, by a distance of exactly **25.4 mm (1 inch)**, when the assembly is heated at a controlled rate of **5 °C per minute** in a liquid bath (distilled water for softening point ≤ 80 °C, and USP glycerin for softening point > 80 °C).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Softening Point is grouped with Penetration and Ductility as the three classical consistency tests for bitumen, and is the *only* test that gives a temperature-based measure of bitumen's resistance to flow under heat.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine placing a small steel ball (like a marble) on a pat of butter and a pat of hard cheese, then putting both in a warm oven. The ball sinks through the butter at a much lower temperature than through the cheese. The temperature at which the ball "gives way" and drops is essentially the **softening point**. Bitumen behaves similarly: at low temperatures it is nearly solid (like cheese), and as it warms, it gradually turns pliable and finally begins to flow. The Ring and Ball test simply defines, in a standardized way, the exact moment that "flow" begins.

- **Water as a bath** is used for bitumens with softening point up to **80 °C** (typical paving grades in cooler regions).
- **Glycerin as a bath** is used when the softening point exceeds **80 °C** (industrial bitumens used in tropical or high-temperature service).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant to Memorize:** Rate of temperature rise = **5 °C per minute**. This is the single most-cited numerical in KTU viva questions on this experiment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants for the Apparatus:**
> - Brass ring internal diameter = **15.875 mm** (≈ 5/8 inch)
> - Brass ring depth = **6.35 mm** (≈ 1/4 inch)
> - Steel ball diameter = **9.53 mm** (≈ 3/8 inch)
> - Steel ball mass = **3.50 g**
> - Ball sinking distance = **25.4 mm** (1 inch)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic – Step-by-Step Reasoning
The Ring and Ball test is a **displacement-based, time-controlled heating experiment**. The underlying logic is built on four pillars:

1. **Geometric Standardization:** A fixed-size brass ring ensures every bitumen disc tested has identical area and thickness. This removes geometry as a variable.
2. **Constant Load:** A standard 3.5 g ball provides a fixed gravitational load. The deformation is purely a function of bitumen viscosity at the test temperature.
3. **Controlled Heating:** A rise of 5 °C/min guarantees that the temperature profile is reproducible across laboratories worldwide.
4. **Observable Failure Point:** The 25.4 mm fall through the bottom plate is a clear, measurable end condition.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind 5 °C/min:** A faster rate would record a falsely high softening point (the bitumen doesn't have time to flow); a slower rate would record a falsely low one. The 5 °C/min rate is a kinetic compromise that all international standards share.

### 2.2 Choice of Bath Liquid
The test liquid is dictated by the *expected* softening point of the bitumen.

- **Distilled Water Bath** — For bitumens softening between **30 °C and 80 °C**. Water boils at 100 °C, which is safely above the test range.
- **USP Glycerin Bath** — For bitumens softening between **80 °C and 157 °C**. Glycerin has a much higher boiling point (~290 °C) and prevents premature boiling during the test.

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU mistake: students write "use glycerin for low-softening-point bitumens" — this is **wrong**. Glycerin is for *high* softening points because it does not boil within the test temperature range.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity / Symbol | Formula / Standard Value | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rate of temperature rise | $\dfrac{dT}{dt} = 5$ | °C per minute | Strictly maintained throughout the test |
| Time corresponding to 5 °C rise | $t = 60$ s | seconds | Used to standardize heating |
| Mass of standard steel ball | $m_{ball} = 3.50$ | g | Fixed by IS 1205 / ASTM D36 |
| Diameter of standard steel ball | $d_{ball} = 9.53$ | mm | ≈ 3/8 inch |
| Internal diameter of brass ring | $D_{ring} = 15.875$ | mm | ≈ 5/8 inch |
| Depth of brass ring | $h_{ring} = 6.35$ | mm | ≈ 1/4 inch |
| Penetration distance of ball | $h_{fall} = 25.4$ | mm | 1 inch (defines end-point) |
| Average Softening Point | $T_{soft} = \dfrac{T_1 + T_2}{2}$ | °C | $T_1, T_2$ = readings of two rings |
| Permissible difference | $\Delta T \le 1$ | °C | For two rings of same sample |
| Test liquid switch-over temperature | $T_{switch} = 80$ | °C | Below 80 °C → water; above 80 °C → glycerin |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always present the **average of two ring readings** as the final result. A difference greater than 1 °C between the two rings invalidates the test and it must be repeated.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of the Softening Point
- **Pavement design in hot climates:** A higher softening point bitumen resists rutting and bleeding on hot Indian roads where surface temperatures may exceed 60 °C.
- **Specification of bitumen grade:** For example, VG-30 bitumen typically has a softening point of **47–55 °C**, while VG-40 has a minimum of **50 °C**.
- **Determination of safe heating temperature:** A bitumen should be heated at least **20–30 °C below** its softening point to avoid local degradation during mixing and spraying.
- **Quality control at refineries and construction sites** — quick and reproducible laboratory test.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Mean Softening Point
Two horizontal brass rings are filled with the same bitumen sample, each placed on a separate support in the bath. Each ring carries its own ball, and the temperature at which each ball touches the bottom plate is recorded independently. Let these two end-point temperatures be $T_1$ and $T_2$ in °C.

The softening point $T_{soft}$ is the arithmetic mean of the two observations, since the test requires a single representative value to be reported.

$$
T_{soft} = \dfrac{T_1 + T_2}{2} \quad \text{(in °C)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This expression is the only numerical formula the KTU examiner expects a student to reproduce in the answer script for the Softening Point experiment.

### 3.2 Worked Example – Typical KTU Numerical
**Question (modeled on KTU style):** During a Softening Point test on a sample of paving bitumen, the two ring-and-ball assemblies recorded end-point temperatures of $T_1 = 51.5$ °C and $T_2 = 52.5$ °C. Compute the softening point and comment on whether the test is valid.

**Step 1 — Compute the difference between the two readings:**

$$
\Delta T = \vert T_1 - T_2 \vert = \vert 51.5 - 52.5 \vert
$$

$$
\Delta T = 1.0 \; °C
$$

**Step 2 — Check the validity condition (IS 1205):**

The standard permits a maximum difference of **1 °C** between the two rings. Since $\Delta T = 1.0$ °C $\le$ 1 °C, the test **is valid**.

**Step 3 — Compute the average softening point:**

$$
T_{soft} = \dfrac{T_1 + T_2}{2} = \dfrac{51.5 + 52.5}{2}
$$

$$
T_{soft} = \dfrac{104.0}{2} = 52.0 \; °C
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

A softening point of **52 °C** is consistent with **VG-30 grade paving bitumen** (IS 73:2013 specifies 47 °C minimum for VG-30). The result is acceptable for road construction in the plains of Kerala.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Pre-Heat, Bath & Heating Schedule
The following matrix summarizes the critical procedural checkpoints the student must know for the KTU lab record.

| Stage | Action | Critical Parameter | Allowed Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pre-melting | Heat bitumen to pourable state | Heating temperature | Not more than 111 °C above expected softening point |
| Pouring | Pour into brass rings, cool 30 min in air | Air-cooling time | ≥ 30 min |
| Conditioning | Immerse in chilled water/glycerin | Bath temperature | 4 ± 0.5 °C for ≥ 30 min |
| Initial heating | Bring bath to (5 °C below expected S.P.) | Heating rate | Start with mild rate |
| Main heating | Maintain heating rate | dT/dt | Exactly 5 °C/min |
| End-point | Ball touches bottom plate | Penetration depth | 25.4 mm |
| Reporting | Average of $T_1$ and $T_2$ | Validity | $\Delta T \le 1$ °C |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture of the Apparatus
The Ring and Ball test apparatus is a stack of mechanical, thermal, and metrological components. The diagram below maps the functional architecture of the test rig, the direction of heat flow, and the role of each component.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HeatSource["Heat Source Assembly"]
        A1[Electric / Gas Burner]
        A2[Stirrer Motor]
    end

    subgraph BathAssembly["Liquid Bath Assembly"]
        B1[Heat-resistant Beaker 600 mL]
        B2[Bath Liquid - Water lt 80C]
        B3[Bath Liquid - Glycerin ge 80C]
        B4[Ring and Ball Support Plate]
    end

    subgraph SampleAssembly["Sample and Loading Assembly"]
        C1[Brass Ring 15.875 mm ID]
        C2[Bitumen Disc 6.35 mm thick]
        C3[Steel Ball 3.50 g, 9.53 mm]
        C4[Bottom Plate 25.4 mm below]
    end

    subgraph Metrology["Measurement Assembly"]
        D1[Mercury Thermometer 0 to 100 C]
        D2[Stopwatch for 5C per min]
    end

    A1 -->|heat| B1
    A2 -->|uniform mixing| B1
    B1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B1 -->|immerses| B4
    B4 -->|holds| C1
    C1 -->|filled with| C2
    C2 -->|supports| C3
    C3 -.->|falls 25.4 mm on heating| C4
    D1 -->|monitors bath| B1
    D2 -->|monitors rate| A1
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow of the Test Procedure
The flowchart below traces the sequence of operations executed during a standard Ring and Ball test.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Heat bitumen to pourable state] --> S2[Step 2 Pour into two brass rings]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Air cool for 30 min]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Immerse in water or glycerin at 4C for 30 min]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Place 3.5 g steel ball on each disc]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Assemble ring and ball apparatus in bath]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Suspend thermometer level with rings]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Heat bath at 5C per min]
    S8 --> S9{Step 9 Ball touches bottom plate}
    S9 --> S10[Step 10 Record temperature T1 and T2]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11 Compute mean and check difference ≤ 1C]
    S11 --> S12[Step 12 Report softening point in C]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Description of Cross-Section
A vertical cross-section of the test setup, read from top to bottom, consists of:

- **Thermometer bulb** positioned at the same level as the brass rings.
- **Steel ball (3.50 g)** resting on top of the bitumen disc.
- **Brass ring** containing the bitumen disc.
- **Ring support shelf** that suspends the rings in the bath liquid.
- **Bottom receiving plate** located exactly 25.4 mm beneath the ring.
- **Liquid bath** (water or glycerin) filling the beaker.
- **Heat source and stirrer** at the base, providing uniform heating.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define the term "softening point" of bitumen and state the standard IS code used for the test.**

**Model Answer:**
The softening point of bitumen is the temperature, in degrees Celsius, at which a standard steel ball of 3.50 g mass and 9.53 mm diameter sinks through a horizontal disc of bitumen held in a brass ring of 15.875 mm internal diameter, by exactly 25.4 mm, when the assembly is heated in a liquid bath at a uniform rate of 5 °C per minute. The relevant Indian Standard code is **IS 1205:1978** (Methods of Testing Tar and Bituminous Materials – Determination of Softening Point – Ring and Ball Apparatus).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Distinguish between the use of water and glycerin as bath liquids in the softening point test.**

**Model Answer:**
Water is used as the bath liquid when the expected softening point of the bitumen is **below 80 °C**, because water boils at 100 °C which is well above the test temperature. Glycerin is used when the expected softening point is **above 80 °C** (up to 157 °C), because water would boil during the test whereas glycerin has a much higher boiling point (~290 °C) and provides a stable heating medium. The switch-over threshold is fixed at **80 °C** in IS 1205.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** List the apparatus required to determine the softening point of bitumen by the Ring and Ball method, and draw a neat labelled sketch of the apparatus. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus Required:**
1. **Ring and Ball apparatus** consisting of two brass rings, two steel balls (3.50 g each, 9.53 mm diameter), two ring-supporting shelves with clamps, and a bottom plate located 25.4 mm below the rings.
2. **Heat-resistant glass beaker** of approximately 600 mL capacity.
3. **Mercury-in-glass thermometer** with range 0 °C to 100 °C (for water bath) and another for 100 °C to 200 °C (for glycerin bath).
4. **Stirrer** — a mechanical or hand-held stirrer to ensure uniform bath temperature.
5. **Heat source** — electrically heated sand bath, spirit lamp, or Bunsen burner.
6. **Bath liquid** — distilled water (for S.P. ≤ 80 °C) or USP glycerin (for S.P. > 80 °C).
7. **Knife or spatula** for trimming the bitumen.
8. **Stopwatch** to maintain the 5 °C per minute heating rate.
9. **Benzene or trichloroethylene** for cleaning.

**Neat Labelled Sketch (ASCII representation, equivalent to the apparatus cross-section):**

```
       ┌──── Thermometer (suspended)
       │
       │
   ⊙  │  ⊙  ← Steel balls (3.50 g each)
   ───┴───     ← Bitumen disc surface
  ┃ Ring ┃ Ring ← Brass rings (15.875 mm ID)
  ┃  ┃   ┃
  ┃  ┃   ┃
   ───────    ← Ring-supporting shelf
   ───────    ← Bottom plate (25.4 mm below)
   ~ ~ ~ ~    ← Liquid bath (water/glycerin)
   ~ ~ ~ ~
    burner    ← Heat source
```

**(b)** Describe the step-by-step procedure of the Ring and Ball test and state two precautions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Procedure:**

1. Melt the bitumen sample to a fluid, pourable consistency. The temperature during melting must not exceed 111 °C above its expected softening point.
2. Stir the molten bitumen to remove air bubbles and pour it into the two brass rings placed on a metal plate coated with a release agent. Slightly overfill the rings.
3. Allow the rings to cool in air for at least 30 minutes.
4. Trim the excess bitumen flush with the top of each ring using a warm knife.
5. Immerse the rings (with the bitumen discs) in a water or glycerin bath maintained at **4 ± 0.5 °C** for at least 30 minutes.
6. Assemble the apparatus in the bath. Place a 3.50 g steel ball on each bitumen disc and suspend a thermometer at the same level as the rings.
7. Heat the bath at a uniform rate of **5 °C per minute**. Stir continuously to maintain uniform temperature.
8. Record the temperature $T_1$ and $T_2$ at which each ball sinks and touches the bottom plate (a 25.4 mm fall).
9. Compute the mean softening point: $T_{soft} = (T_1 + T_2)/2$. The test is valid only if the difference is not more than 1 °C.

**Precautions:**

1. The heating rate must be maintained at exactly **5 °C per minute** — use a stopwatch.
2. Air bubbles in the poured bitumen must be removed, as they can cause premature deformation and an erroneous low reading.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] *(Internal Choice to Question A)*

**(a)** What is the significance of the softening point test for bitumen used in road construction? Discuss how the result influences bitumen grade selection. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Significance of the Softening Point Test:**

1. **Temperature Susceptibility Indicator:** It indicates the temperature at which bitumen ceases to behave as a rigid solid and begins to flow, providing a direct measure of its temperature susceptibility.
2. **Rutting & Bleeding Resistance:** A higher softening point indicates that the bitumen can resist deformation, rutting and bleeding in hot climates where pavement surface temperatures can exceed 60 °C — a key requirement for Indian road conditions.
3. **Safe Heating Temperature:** It establishes the maximum safe temperature for heating bitumen during mixing, spraying, and laying. A general rule is to heat bitumen to **at least 20–30 °C below** its softening point to avoid thermal degradation and fire hazards.
4. **Quality Control Check:** It serves as a routine acceptance test at refineries and construction sites.

**Influence on Bitumen Grade Selection:**

For example, **VG-30 bitumen** specified by IS 73:2013 has a softening point of **47 °C minimum**, while **VG-40** has a softening point of **50 °C minimum**. In the hot, semi-arid climate of central and north India, VG-40 is preferred for heavy-traffic national highways because its higher softening point provides greater resistance to high-temperature rutting. In the milder coastal climate of Kerala, VG-30 is generally adequate.

**(b)** The two ring-and-ball readings obtained during a test on a paving-grade bitumen were $T_1 = 49.0$ °C and $T_2 = 50.5$ °C. Compute the softening point and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute the difference between the two readings:**

$$
\Delta T = \vert T_1 - T_2 \vert = \vert 49.0 - 50.5 \vert = 1.5 \; °C
$$

**Step 2 — Check validity:**

IS 1205 permits a maximum difference of **1 °C** between the two rings. Since $\Delta T = 1.5$ °C $>$ 1 °C, the test is **invalid** and must be repeated.

**Step 3 — For record, the mean is:**

$$
T_{soft} = \dfrac{49.0 + 50.5}{2} = \dfrac{99.5}{2} = 49.75 \; °C
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

Even if the test were valid, a softening point of ~50 °C is consistent with **VG-30 / VG-40 paving bitumen** and is acceptable for flexible pavement construction in the plains. However, the practical conclusion here is that the operator must repeat the test under more carefully controlled heating (5 °C per minute strictly observed) to obtain a valid reading.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks in the Softening Point Question:**
> 1. **Wrong bath liquid** — many students write "glycerin for low-softening-point bitumens" — actually the opposite is true.
> 2. **Wrong ring dimensions** — confusing the *internal* diameter (15.875 mm) with *outer* diameter, or quoting 15 mm instead of 15.875 mm.
> 3. **Wrong heating rate** — stating 1 °C per minute or 10 °C per minute. The correct value is **5 °C per minute**, strictly.
> 4. **Forgetting to state the IS code** (IS 1205) — KTU examiners explicitly reward this in 1-mark sub-questions.
> 5. **Ignoring the validity condition** $\Delta T \le 1$ °C in numerical problems — a student who computes the mean without checking validity loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Misreporting the end-point distance** as 10 mm or 15 mm instead of **25.4 mm** (1 inch).
> 7. **Forgetting the cooling sequence** (30 min air + 30 min water at 4 °C) — this is a frequent 1-mark loss.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Temperature at which a 3.50 g steel ball sinks 25.4 mm through a bitumen disc in a 15.875 mm brass ring, heated at 5 °C/min.
- **IS Code:** IS 1205:1978 (Reaffirmed 2004). ASTM equivalent: D36 / AASHTO T53.
- **Bath liquid:** Distilled water (≤ 80 °C) | USP Glycerin (> 80 °C). Switch-over threshold = **80 °C**.
- **Apparatus constants:** Ring ID = 15.875 mm, depth = 6.35 mm, ball mass = 3.50 g, ball diameter = 9.53 mm, fall = 25.4 mm.
- **Heating rate:** **5 °C per minute** — never deviate.
- **Sample cooling:** 30 min in air, then 30 min in bath at 4 ± 0.5 °C.
- **Result reporting:** $T_{soft} = (T_1 + T_2)/2$.
- **Validity:** $\Delta T = \vert T_1 - T_2 \vert \le 1$ °C; else repeat.
- **Engineering significance:** Higher softening point → better high-temperature rutting/bleeding resistance; safe heating temperature = softening point minus 20–30 °C.
- **Typical values:** VG-30 → 47 °C (min); VG-40 → 50 °C (min).
- **Common pitfall:** Glycerin is for *high* softening points, not low.
- **Viva questions to expect:** IS code, bath liquid, ball mass, heating rate, end-point distance, why 5 °C/min, what $\Delta T$ validity means.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ductility of Bitumen (Demonstration Using Aged Bitumen)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL607 - TRANSPORTATION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Ductility of Bitumen (Demonstration Using Aged Bitumen)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ductility of bitumen** is defined as the *distance in centimeters* to which a standard briquette specimen of bitumen can be stretched horizontally without breaking, under standardized conditions of temperature (**27 °C ± 0.5 °C**) and rate of elongation (**5 cm/min ± 0.5 cm/min**). The test is governed by **IS 1208:1978** (Reaffirmed 2013) and is functionally identical to **ASTM D113** and **AASHTO T51**.

Mathematically, the ductility value is reported as:

$$D = L_f - L_0 \quad \text{(in cm)}$$

where $L_0$ is the original gauge length of the specimen (= 7.5 cm between the side clips of the briquette mold) and $L_f$ is the elongated length at the moment of rupture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aged Bitumen Context (KTU Experiment 2 Specific):** When bitumen is aged — either through the **Thin Film Oven Test (TFOT, IS 9382)**, **Rolling Thin Film Oven Test (RTFOT, IS 17364)** or by prolonged field service — its ductility value drops dramatically. A fresh paving-grade bitumen (e.g., VG-30) typically exhibits ductility > 75 cm (IS requirement), whereas the same bitumen after aging may fall below 20 cm, often even to single digits, clearly demonstrating the loss of viscoelastic flexibility.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cheese Slice vs. Toffee" Model

Imagine two identical-looking strips — one is a **soft cheese slice** and the other is a **hard toffee**. If you pull both ends gently at room temperature:

- The **cheese slice** stretches long and thin, then snaps — high ductility.
- The **toffee** either breaks immediately or cracks after a tiny deformation — low ductility.

Now imagine leaving both strips on a hot car dashboard for a week (simulating oxidative aging). The cheese slice hardens like the toffee, and the toffee becomes almost glassy. Both have lost ductility.

**Bitumen behaves identically:**

- **Fresh bitumen** = cheese slice (flexible, stretchable, ductile).
- **Aged bitumen** = toffee (brittle, glassy, low ductility).

This analogy is extremely useful in KTU viva when explaining *why* aging matters in road engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick VIVA Hook (frequently asked):** *"Why test ductility at 27 °C?"* — Because 27 °C is the **near-mean annual pavement temperature in most of peninsular India**, the condition at which the binder is expected to remain flexible during summer-monsoon transitions.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Values (Memorize for KTU ESE)

| Property | Fresh VG-30 Bitumen | Aged VG-30 Bitumen (RTFOT) | IS 73:2013 Minimum |
|----------|---------------------|----------------------------|--------------------|
| Ductility at 27 °C | ≥ 100 cm (typical 100–150) | 10–40 cm (often <20) | **75 cm** (fresh) |
| Penetration at 25 °C | 50–70 dmm | 30–45 dmm | 50–70 dmm |
| Softening Point | 47–55 °C | 55–65 °C | 47 °C min |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners' favourite comparison line:** *Ductility is inversely proportional to aging.* As ductility decreases, the bitumen becomes stiffer, more brittle, and more prone to thermal cracking — the primary reason this test is performed in conjunction with the Penetration and Softening Point tests (the classic "three-test trio" of bitumen characterization).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Viscoelastic Behaviour of Bitumen

Bitumen is a **thermo-rheologically complex material** — its mechanical response lies somewhere between a viscous liquid (Newtonian) and an elastic solid (Hookean). This is the origin of the term **viscoelastic**.

**Three key theoretical concepts** that KTU examiners love to test:

1. **Time-Temperature Superposition Principle:** Bitumen that is *soft and ductile* at high temperature can be equivalent, in stiffness terms, to bitumen that is *hard and brittle* at low temperature. This is why we can artificially "age" bitumen in an oven and infer how it will behave after years of service.

2. **Oxidative Aging Pathway:** During aging, the volatile maltene fraction evaporates, and the polar aromatics convert into **asphaltenes** (large, complex polycyclic aromatic clusters). The asphaltene-to-maltene ratio increases → stiffness rises → ductility falls. This is irreversible chemical aging.

3. **Adhesive vs Cohesive Ductility:**
   - *Cohesive ductility:* the bitumen's internal resistance to rupture (the test we perform).
   - *Adhesive ductility:* the bitumen's resistance to detaching from the aggregate (related to the stripping test, not this experiment).

### 2.2 The Operational Logic — Why Each Step in IS 1208 Matters

| Step | What is Done | Why it Matters (The "Why" Behind It) |
|------|--------------|--------------------------------------|
| 1. Melt bitumen to 75–100 °C above its softening point | Ensures complete fluidity | Removes thermal history, gives a homogeneous sample |
| 2. Coat mold with glycerin–dextrin / mercury | Releases bitumen after cooling | Prevents specimen damage during demolding |
| 3. Pour in single pour, avoid air bubbles | Eliminates stress concentrators | Air voids become crack initiation points |
| 4. Cool 35–40 min at room temp, then 85–95 min in 27 °C bath | Thermal equilibrium | Standardizes test temperature |
| 5. Pull at exactly **5 cm/min** | Constant strain rate | Strain-rate sensitivity of bitumen — too fast → brittle failure; too slow → creep rupture |
| 6. Note the thread length at the moment of rupture | Final reading | The exact cm value IS the ductility |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|----------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $D = L_f - L_0$ | Ductility (elongation at break) | **cm** |
| 2 | $L_0 = 7.5 \text{ cm}$ | Original gauge length (between briquette side clips) | cm |
| 3 | $\varepsilon_{eng} = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} = \dfrac{D}{7.5}$ | Engineering strain at break | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{v_{pull}}{L_0} = \dfrac{5 \text{ cm/min}}{7.5 \text{ cm}} = 0.667 \text{ min}^{-1}$ | Strain rate of testing | **min⁻¹** |
| 5 | $T_{test} = 27 \pm 0.5 \text{ °C}$ | Standard test temperature | **°C** |
| 6 | $v_{pull} = 5 \pm 0.5 \text{ cm/min}$ | Standard pulling speed | **cm/min** |
| 7 | $\eta_{apparent} \propto \dfrac{\sigma}{\dot{\varepsilon}}$ | Apparent viscosity at test conditions (qualitative) | Pa·s |
| 8 | $S = 25 + \dfrac{1}{2}(\text{SP} - 25) \cdot k$ | Empirical stiffness surrogate (not in syllabus but used in advanced pavement design) | dmm |
| 9 | $\text{Aging Index (AI)} = \dfrac{P_{aged}}{P_{fresh}}$ | AI for penetration; analogous concept for ductility: $\text{DI}_{retention} = \dfrac{D_{aged}}{D_{fresh}} \times 100\%$ | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Ductility Retention Ratio (Ductility Index):** Many KTU problems ask you to compute the percentage ductility retained after aging. Use:
> $$\text{Ductility Retention} = \frac{D_{aged}}{D_{fresh}} \times 100 \text{ \%}$$
> A retention below **40 %** is generally considered indicative of a severely aged / over-oxidized binder.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pavement cracking prediction:** Low ductility bitumen → low-temperature thermal cracking (transverse cracks) in flexible pavements in North-Indian and high-altitude roads.
- **Binder selection for cold-climate regions:** Modified bitumen (e.g., PMB, CRMB) is preferred where high retained ductility is required.
- **Quality control at refineries:** Batch-to-batch consistency is verified by this 30-minute test.
- **Recycling projects:** Aged Reclaimed Asphalt Pavement (RAP) binder has near-zero ductility; rejuvenators are added to restore ductility to acceptable levels.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Ductility Value from Raw Observations

The ductility test is a *direct measurement* rather than a *derived* quantity, but KTU examiners often provide raw data and expect you to apply the formula with rigor. The following derivation clarifies every step.

**Step 1: Identify the initial gauge length.**

The standard briquette mold (per IS 1208) has side clips whose centers are separated by exactly:

$$L_0 = 7.5 \text{ cm}$$

This is the *original effective length* of bitumen between the two clip points.

**Step 2: Identify the pulling speed and the time of rupture.**

The machine is calibrated to move the carriage at a constant linear velocity:

$$v = 5 \text{ cm/min}$$

Let the rupture occur at time $t_r$ minutes from the start of pulling. The *extension* at rupture is:

$$\Delta L = v \cdot t_r = 5 \cdot t_r \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3: Express the final length at rupture.**

The final (broken) length is the original length plus the extension:

$$L_f = L_0 + \Delta L = 7.5 + 5 \cdot t_r \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the ductility formula.**

By definition of ductility:

$$D = L_f - L_0 = (7.5 + 5 \cdot t_r) - 7.5 = 5 \cdot t_r \text{ cm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Obvious Result (high-yield for KTU):** The ductility value in centimeters is **numerically equal to 5 times the time in minutes** to rupture, *provided* the standard pulling rate of 5 cm/min is strictly maintained. This is a favourite 3-mark shortcut question: *"If a ductility test specimen breaks after 18 minutes, what is the ductility?"* → **D = 5 × 18 = 90 cm.**

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Problem)

**Given:**
- Fresh VG-30 bitumen ductility test breaks at $t_{r,fresh} = 22 \text{ min}$ → $D_{fresh} = 110 \text{ cm}$
- Same bitumen after RTFOT aging breaks at $t_{r,aged} = 4.5 \text{ min}$ → $D_{aged} = 22.5 \text{ cm}$
- Pulling rate $v = 5$ cm/min; test temperature $T = 27$ °C.

**Required:**
1. Calculate the ductility retention ratio.
2. Comment on whether the aged bitumen is acceptable for VG-30 grading (IS 73:2013).

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute ductility values.**

$$D_{fresh} = 5 \times 22 = 110 \text{ cm}$$

$$D_{aged} = 5 \times 4.5 = 22.5 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2: Apply the ductility retention formula.**

$$\text{Retention} = \frac{D_{aged}}{D_{fresh}} \times 100 = \frac{22.5}{110} \times 100$$

$$\text{Retention} = 0.20454\ldots \times 100 = 20.45 \text{ \%}$$

**Step 3: Compute engineering strain at break for both samples.**

For fresh bitumen:

$$\varepsilon_{fresh} = \frac{D_{fresh}}{L_0} = \frac{110}{7.5} = 14.67 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

For aged bitumen:

$$\varepsilon_{aged} = \frac{D_{aged}}{L_0} = \frac{22.5}{7.5} = 3.0 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

**Step 4: Compare to IS standards.**

IS 73:2013 mandates minimum ductility of **75 cm** for *fresh* VG-30. After RTFOT aging, IS 17364 specifies a **minimum ductility retention of 50 %** (for VG-30 grade, post-RTFOT retained ductility ≥ 50 cm is also commonly specified by MoRTH).

Since $D_{aged} = 22.5$ cm **<** 50 cm, the aged binder **fails** the post-aging ductility criterion and would be rejected for use in high-quality pavement construction.

**Step 5: Engineering interpretation.**

The aged bitumen has lost **~80 %** of its original elongation capacity, indicating severe oxidative hardening. Such a binder, if used in a pavement, would be highly susceptible to:
- Low-temperature thermal cracking
- Fatigue cracking under repeated traffic loading
- Premature raveling of the surface course

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Summary (A "Cheat Table" for KTU)

| Property | Fresh Bitumen | Aged Bitumen | Direction of Change |
|----------|---------------|--------------|---------------------|
| Ductility (cm) | High (≥ 100) | Low (10–40) | ↓ Decreases |
| Penetration (dmm) | High (50–70) | Low (20–40) | ↓ Decreases |
| Softening Point (°C) | Lower (47–55) | Higher (55–70) | ↑ Increases |
| Viscosity | Lower | Higher (orders of magnitude) | ↑ Increases |
| Asphaltene content (%) | ~10–20 | ~25–40 | ↑ Increases |
| Engineering strain at break | ~13–20 | ~1–5 | ↓ Decreases |
| Cracking susceptibility | Low | High | ↑ Increases |
| Plastic flow resistance | Low | High | ↑ Increases |

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Mnemonic — "DPS-VA":** As bitumen ages, **D**uctility ↓, **P**enetration ↓, **S**oftening Point ↑, **V**iscosity ↑, **A**sphaltenes ↑. Reverse this for fresh bitumen, and you have the entire aging story in 5 letters.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Complete Ductility Test Workflow (Aged Bitumen)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Bitumen Sample Selection"] --> B["Melt bitumen to 80-100 C above softening point"]
    B --> C["Apply release agent glycerin-dextrin to mold"]
    C --> D["Pour bitumen into briquette mold in single pour"]
    D --> E["Cool at room temperature 35 to 40 minutes"]
    E --> F["Trim excess bitumen flush with mold top"]
    F --> G["Immerse in water bath at 27 C for 85 to 95 minutes"]
    G --> H["Mount assembly on ductility machine carriage"]
    H --> I["Set pulling rate to 5 cm per minute"]
    I --> J["Start machine and observe bitumen thread"]
    J --> K{"Thread breaks?"}
    K -- "No" --> J
    K -- "Yes" --> L["Note distance in cm at moment of rupture"]
    L --> M["Record D_fresh and time t_r"]
    M --> N{"Run for fresh OR aged bitumen?"}
    N -- "Fresh" --> O["Report D_fresh value in cm"]
    N -- "Aged via RTFOT or TFOT" --> P["Subject identical sample to oven aging 163 C for 5 hours"]
    P --> Q["Repeat full procedure on aged specimen"]
    Q --> R["Record D_aged value in cm"]
    R --> S["Compute Ductility Retention Ratio"]
    S --> T["Compare with IS 73 and IS 17364 limits"]
    T --> U["END: Pass or Fail decision for VG grade"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Apparatus & Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Water Bath Assembly"]
        WB["Water Bath 27 C 0.5 C"] --> TH["Immersion Thermometer"]
        WB --> HS["Heating Stirring Unit"]
    end
    subgraph S2["Ductility Testing Machine"]
        CM["Carriage with Specimen Clips"] --> SS["Screw Drive Mechanism"]
        SS --> MTR["Electric Motor 5 cm per minute"]
        MTR --> SC["Speed Controller"]
    end
    subgraph S3["Specimen Geometry Block"]
        MQ["Briquette Mold 75 mm long"] --> CS["Cross Section 10 x 10 mm at throat"]
        MQ --> RC["Radius of Curvature 15 mm at clips"]
    end
    subgraph S4["Measurement Output"]
        SC --> RD["Ruler Scale in cm"]
        RD --> RT["Rupture Detection visual or photo sensor"]
    end
    MTR --> CM
    WB -.-> CM
    MQ -.-> CM
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Aging Effect on Ductility

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Bitumen Aging Process"] --> B["Volatilization of Maltenes"]
    A --> C["Oxidation of Aromatics"]
    A --> D["Polymerization of Asphaltenes"]
    B --> E["Loss of Light Oils"]
    C --> E
    D --> F["Increased Molecular Weight"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Increased Stiffness and Brittleness"]
    G --> H["Ductility DROPS sharply"]
    H --> I["Strain at Break reduced from 14 to 3"]
    I --> J["Thermal Cracking Susceptibility rises"]
    J --> K["Pavement Service Life reduced"]
```

### 4.4 Schematic of the Standard Briquette Mold (Geometric Specs)

Although a physical drawing is best, the following ASCII schematic shows the *crucial throat region* where the bitumen is thinnest and where rupture initiates:

```
   ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
   │  Side Clip (movable)             │ ← Carriage pulls here
   │   ●─────────────────────────────●│
   │    \                           / │
   │     \  10 mm x 10 mm throat    /  │ ← Narrowest section
   │      \_______________________/   │     (rupture zone)
   │     /                           \│
   │    /                             \│
   │   ●──────────────────────────────●│
   │  Side Clip (movable)             │ ← Carriage pulls here
   └──────────────────────────────────┘
        Total gauge length L0 = 7.5 cm
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** The *throat* (narrowest 10 × 10 mm section) is intentionally the weakest cross-section, ensuring that failure initiates at a known location. This gives a *repeatable measurement*. The radius-of-curvature transition (15 mm) prevents stress singularities.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Define ductility of bitumen. State the IS code used and the standard test conditions (temperature and pulling rate).**

**Model Answer:**

Ductility of bitumen is the property by virtue of which a standard briquette specimen of bitumen can be stretched or elongated without breaking. It is measured as the **distance in cm** that the specimen elongates before rupture.

The test is conducted as per **IS 1208:1978** under the following standardized conditions:
- **Test temperature: 27 °C ± 0.5 °C**
- **Rate of pulling: 5 cm/min ± 0.5 cm/min**

The minimum ductility value required for VG-30 grade paving bitumen (as per IS 73:2013) is **75 cm**.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for IS code + conditions, 1 for VG-30 minimum)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]`
**How does aging of bitumen affect its ductility? Mention the standard aging methods used in the laboratory.**

**Model Answer:**

Aging of bitumen causes a **significant reduction in ductility** due to:
1. Loss of volatile oily fractions (maltenes) from the binder.
2. Oxidative conversion of aromatics into higher-molecular-weight asphaltenes.
3. Net result: increased stiffness, increased brittleness, and reduced elongation before rupture. Ductility values may drop from 100+ cm (fresh) to 10–40 cm (aged).

**Standard laboratory aging methods (per IS / ASTM):**
1. **Thin Film Oven Test (TFOT)** — IS 9382 / ASTM D1754
2. **Rolling Thin Film Oven Test (RTFOT)** — IS 17364 / ASTM D2872
3. **Pressure Aging Vessel (PAV)** — ASTM D6521 (for long-term aging)

*(3 marks: 2 for mechanism, 1 for aging methods)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Regular]`

**(a)** Describe the procedure to determine the ductility of bitumen as per IS 1208:1978. Draw a labeled sketch of the briquette mold and the ductility machine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a ductility test conducted on a fresh VG-30 bitumen sample, the specimen ruptured after **18 minutes** of pulling. The same bitumen, after RTFOT aging, ruptured after **4.2 minutes** under identical conditions. Calculate the ductility of both samples, the ductility retention ratio, and comment on the suitability of the aged binder for road construction. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Apparatus required:**
1. Ductility testing machine with carriage moving at **5 cm/min ± 0.5 cm/min**.
2. Standard briquette mold (75 mm long, 10 mm × 10 mm throat).
3. Water bath maintained at **27 °C ± 0.5 °C**.
4. Thermometer with 0.1 °C accuracy.
5. Release agent (glycerin–dextrin mixture or mercury).
6. Hot plate / oven for melting the bitumen.

**Procedure (Stepwise):**

1. **Sample preparation:** Heat the bitumen sample slowly to a temperature between 75 °C and 100 °C above its softening point until it is completely fluid and free from water.

2. **Mold preparation:** Apply a thin coat of release agent (glycerin–dextrin) to the inner surfaces of the mold. Place the mold assembly on a brass plate.

3. **Pouring:** Pour the molten bitumen into the mold in a thin, continuous stream from one end to the other, ensuring **no air bubbles** are trapped.

4. **Cooling:** Allow the filled mold to cool at room temperature for **35 to 40 minutes**. Then trim the excess bitumen flush with the top of the mold using a warm spatula.

5. **Conditioning:** Transfer the mold assembly to the water bath maintained at 27 °C ± 0.5 °C and leave it immersed for **85 to 95 minutes** so that the specimen reaches the test temperature throughout.

6. **Testing:** Remove the side pieces of the mold, attach the clips to the carriage of the ductility machine, and start the machine. The bitumen thread is pulled apart at the standard rate of **5 cm/min**.

7. **Observation:** The bitumen thread gradually elongates and thins at the throat. At the moment of rupture, **stop the machine** and read the distance in cm to which the specimen has stretched. This value is the **ductility** of the bitumen.

8. **Reporting:** Express the result as the average of three valid tests in cm. If any test value deviates by more than ± 10 % from the mean, repeat the test.

**Sketch (Briquette Mold + Machine):**

```
      ┌─────────────┐
      │  Side Clip  │← Movable carriage
      │  ●──────────●│
      │   \        / │
      │    \──────/  │ ← Throat (10×10 mm)
      │   /        \ │
      │  ●──────────●│
      │  Side Clip  │← Movable carriage
      └─────────────┘
      Carriage speed = 5 cm/min
      Water bath = 27 ± 0.5 °C
```

*(Marks distribution: 1 for apparatus list, 3 for procedure steps, 1 for conditions, 2 for sketch)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $t_{r,fresh} = 18$ min
- $t_{r,aged} = 4.2$ min
- $v = 5$ cm/min

**Step 1: Ductility of fresh bitumen.**

$$D_{fresh} = v \times t_{r,fresh} = 5 \times 18 = 90 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2: Ductility of aged bitumen.**

$$D_{aged} = v \times t_{r,aged} = 5 \times 4.2 = 21 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3: Ductility retention ratio.**

$$\text{Retention} = \frac{D_{aged}}{D_{fresh}} \times 100 = \frac{21}{90} \times 100$$

$$\text{Retention} = 0.2333\ldots \times 100 = 23.33 \text{ \%}$$

**Step 4: Comparison with IS standards.**

For VG-30 grade (IS 73:2013):
- Fresh binder: minimum ductility = **75 cm** → $D_{fresh} = 90 \text{ cm}$ ✓ **Passes**
- Post-RTFOT: retained ductility should be ≥ **50 % of original** (per MoRTH / IS 17364 specifications for high-quality paving) → 23.33 % ✗ **Fails**

**Step 5: Engineering comment.**

The aged bitumen has retained only **~23 %** of its original ductility, indicating severe oxidative hardening. The binder is **not suitable** for high-quality flexible pavement construction in its current aged state. Recommendations:
- Reject this aged batch, OR
- Blend with a softer fresh bitumen / rejuvenator to restore ductility, OR
- Restrict use to low-volume roads with minimal thermal-stress exposure.

*(Marks distribution: 2 for D_fresh, 2 for D_aged, 1 for retention, 1 for engineering comment, 1 for IS reference)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary]`

**(a)** What is the significance of the ductility test for bitumen used in road construction? Discuss how ductility is affected by temperature, composition, and aging. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a road construction project, two bitumen samples were tested:
- Sample X: Fresh bitumen, ductility = **105 cm** at 27 °C
- Sample Y: Same source after 6 months of field service, ductility = **32 cm** at 27 °C

Calculate the percentage loss in ductility, the engineering strain at break in each case, and discuss the implications for the in-service pavement. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Significance of the Ductility Test:**

1. **Crack-resistance indicator:** Ductility measures the binder's ability to stretch under tensile stress without cracking. Roads in regions with significant diurnal/seasonal temperature variation require ductile binders to avoid thermal and fatigue cracking.

2. **Tensile adhesive property:** High ductility indicates that the bitumen has sufficient adhesive and cohesive strength to hold aggregate particles together under traffic loading and temperature-induced expansion/contraction.

3. **Cold-climate performance:** In cold regions, a binder with adequate ductility can flex with the underlying pavement structure without fracturing.

4. **Quality control:** Acts as a benchmark for batch-to-batch consistency at the refinery / blending plant.

5. **Specification compliance:** IS 73:2013 mandates minimum 75 cm ductility for VG-30 binder; failure implies rejection.

**Effect of Temperature:**
- At higher test temperatures, bitumen becomes more fluid, ductility generally increases.
- At lower test temperatures, bitumen becomes stiff and brittle, ductility decreases.
- Test is standardized at **27 °C** for reproducibility.

**Effect of Composition:**
- Higher **maltene** (oily) fraction → higher ductility.
- Higher **asphaltene** content → lower ductility, higher brittleness.
- Paraffinic wax content adversely affects ductility (wax crystallizes at low T).

**Effect of Aging:**
- Aging converts maltenes → asphaltenes → loss of ductility.
- Field aging in tropical climates (Kerala, Tamil Nadu) is particularly aggressive due to high temperatures and humidity.
- A drop from 100 cm to 20–30 cm after field service is common.

*(Marks distribution: 3 for significance, 2 for temperature, 1 for composition, 1 for aging)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $D_{fresh} = 105$ cm (Sample X)
- $D_{aged} = 32$ cm (Sample Y, after 6 months field service)
- $L_0 = 7.5$ cm

**Step 1: Percentage loss in ductility.**

$$\% \text{ loss} = \frac{D_{fresh} - D_{aged}}{D_{fresh}} \times 100 = \frac{105 - 32}{105} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ loss} = \frac{73}{105} \times 100 = 69.52 \text{ \%}$$

**Step 2: Engineering strain at break — Fresh bitumen (Sample X).**

$$\varepsilon_{fresh} = \frac{D_{fresh}}{L_0} = \frac{105}{7.5} = 14.0 \quad \text{(or } 1400 \text{ \% strain)}$$

**Step 3: Engineering strain at break — Aged bitumen (Sample Y).**

$$\varepsilon_{aged} = \frac{D_{aged}}{L_0} = \frac{32}{7.5} = 4.267 \quad \text{(or } 426.7 \text{ \% strain)}$$

**Step 4: Comparison and pavement implications.**

| Parameter | Sample X (Fresh) | Sample Y (6 months service) |
|-----------|------------------|------------------------------|
| Ductility (cm) | 105 | 32 |
| % Strain at break | 1400 % | 426.7 % |
| IS 73:2013 compliance | ✓ Pass | ✗ Fail (below 75 cm) |
| Cracking risk | Low | High |

**Pavement Implications:**

- A **69.52 % loss** in ductility within just 6 months of field service is alarming and suggests either:
  - Poor-quality initial binder, OR
  - Extreme field-aging conditions (very high pavement temperatures, oxidative environment, porous mix), OR
  - Excessive thinness of binder film (over-asphalted mix → fast oxidation).

- The aged binder, with only **~4.3 times its original length** of stretchability (vs. 14 times for fresh), will be highly susceptible to:
  - **Thermal cracking** in cold weather (Kerala's winter nights + high daytime temperatures cause cyclic stress).
  - **Fatigue cracking** ("crocodile" or "alligator" cracks) under repeated truck loading.
  - **Raveling and stripping** at the binder-aggregate interface.

- **Recommendation:** Conduct further forensic tests (penetration, softening point, viscosity) to confirm aging extent; consider surface recycling with rejuvenator or overlay with a stress-absorbing membrane.

*(Marks distribution: 2 for % loss, 2 for strain calculation, 1 for comparison, 2 for engineering discussion)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Ductility Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the original gauge length ($L_0 = 7.5$ cm).** Many students incorrectly equate ductility with the *final* length. Ductility = elongation = $L_f - L_0$, not $L_f$. **(-2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Mixing up units.** Ductility is reported in **cm** at 27 °C, NOT in mm, NOT in percentage, NOT in dmm (which is the unit for penetration). **(-1 mark)**
>
> 3. **Skipping the IS code.** Every bitumen test answer must cite the IS code: **IS 1208:1978** for ductility. **(-1 mark)**
>
> 4. **Omitting the test conditions.** "27 °C and 5 cm/min" must appear in any procedure answer. **(-1 mark)**
>
> 5. **Treating "ductility" and "elasticity" as synonyms.** They are not. Ductility is total elongation at break (a property); elasticity is recoverable strain (a separate viscoelastic property). Examiners will deduct for this conflation. **(-1 mark)**
>
> 6. **Skipping the engineering interpretation.** Always end ductility calculations with a *comment on pavement performance* — this is the CO (Course Outcome) mapping. **(-2 marks)**
>
> 7. **Drawing an unlabeled sketch.** A neat, labeled diagram of the briquette mold is worth at least 2 marks in any 7-mark procedure question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Ductility = elongation at break of a standard bitumen briquette, in **cm**.
- **IS Code:** IS 1208:1978 (Reaffirmed 2013).
- **Test temperature:** **27 °C ± 0.5 °C** (water bath).
- **Pulling rate:** **5 cm/min ± 0.5 cm/min**.
- **Original gauge length ($L_0$):** **7.5 cm**.
- **Minimum ductility (IS 73:2013, VG-30 fresh):** **75 cm**.
- **Cross-section at throat:** **10 mm × 10 mm**.
- **Aging methods:** TFOT (IS 9382), RTFOT (IS 17364), PAV (ASTM D6521).
- **Aging effect:** ↓ Ductility, ↓ Penetration, ↑ Softening Point, ↑ Viscosity, ↑ Asphaltenes.
- **Quick formula:** $D = v \times t_r = 5 \times t_r$ cm (when $t_r$ in minutes).
- **Retention ratio:** $\frac{D_{aged}}{D_{fresh}} \times 100 \text{ \%}$.
- **Mnemonic for aging effects:** "DPS-VA ↓↓↑↑↑" (Ductility↓, Penetration↓, Softening↑, Viscosity↑, Asphaltenes↑).
- **Significance:** Cracking resistance at low temperature; thermal-stress tolerance; pavement durability.
- **Apparatus trio to remember:** Ductility machine + Water bath + Briquette mold.
- **Typical reduction:** Fresh 100+ cm → Aged 10–40 cm (often > 70 % loss).
- **Engineering implication:** Aged bitumen is brittle → prone to thermal/fatigue cracking.
- **Always cite temperature and pulling rate in any procedure answer.**
- **Always include IS code in any "define" or "state" type answer.**
- **A "complete" answer ends with an engineering implication, not just a number.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flash and Fire Point of Bitumen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL607 - TRANSPORTATION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Flash and Fire Point of Bitumen

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **FLASH POINT** is the lowest temperature at which the **vapours of a bituminous material** momentarily ignite (flash) in the form of a small, transient flame when a small test flame is brought near its surface, under specified conditions of test.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FIRE POINT** is the lowest temperature at which the **vapours of a bituminous material** continue to burn (sustain combustion) for a minimum period of **5 seconds** when a test flame is brought near the surface, under specified conditions of test.

For both, the bituminous material is heated in a standardized cup at a controlled rate, and a small test flame is periodically passed across the surface.

**Applicable Indian Standards:**
- **IS 1448 (P:21)** – Flash and Fire Point by Cleveland Open Cup (COC) Method
- **IS 1209** – Determination of Flash Point and Fire Point (Cleveland Open Cup)
- **ASTM D92** – Standard Test Method for Flash and Fire Points by Cleveland Open Cup Tester
- **IS 1212** – For cutback bitumen flash point (Tagliabue / Pensky-Martens)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **deep-frying a samosa in oil at home**. As the oil heats up:
1. Initially, the oil just gets hot — nothing visible happens.
2. When the oil reaches a particular temperature, you notice a slight **blueish flicker** above the surface if you bring a matchstick near it. The match flares for a **fraction of a second** and goes out. → This temperature is the **Flash Point**.
3. If you keep heating and bring the matchstick again at a higher temperature, the vapours now **catch fire and burn continuously** for a few seconds before going out. → This higher temperature is the **Fire Point**.

In bitumen, exactly the same physics occurs. Bitumen is not a single pure liquid but a complex mixture of heavy hydrocarbons. As it is heated, the **lighter, more volatile fractions evaporate first**. These vapours, when mixed with air in the right proportion, become flammable. The flash point marks the **onset of flammability**, while the fire point marks the **sustained combustion** threshold.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Flash Point = *First warning* of fire hazard. Fire Point = *Actual sustained fire* can occur. In pavement engineering, we care about the **flash point** more than the fire point, because it tells us the **maximum safe heating temperature** for bitumen during mixing and laying.

### 1.3 Why This Test is Critical in Highway Engineering

Bitumen is heated during:
- **Hot mix plant operations** (mixing temperature ≈ **150 °C – 170 °C**)
- **Storage tanks** at the plant
- **Transportation** in bitumen tankers
- **Spraying** of cutback bitumen

If the bitumen is heated **above its flash point**, there is a serious **fire and explosion hazard** at the plant. Hence, the **minimum flash point for paving grade bitumen is specified as 220 °C** (as per IS 73:2013 for paving bitumen).

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Test method:** Cleveland Open Cup (COC)
- **Heating rate near flash point:** **5 °C to 6 °C per minute**
- **Heating rate near fire point:** **1 °C to 2 °C per minute** (slower to allow accurate reading)
- **Test flame application interval:** Every **2 °C** rise in temperature as flash point is approached
- **Test flame diameter:** Approximately **3.2 mm to 4.8 mm**
- **Minimum fire point - flash point differential for paving bitumen:** Usually **5 °C to 15 °C**
- **Atmospheric pressure condition:** Corrected to standard **101.325 kPa (760 mm Hg)** if significant deviation exists.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Basis — Why Do Bitumens Have a Flash Point?

Bitumen is a colloidal dispersion of **asphaltenes** (high molecular weight) in **maltenes** (lower molecular weight oils + resins). When heated:

1. The **maltene phase** releases volatile lighter hydrocarbon fractions.
2. These vapours accumulate in the **air space** above the bitumen in the test cup.
3. The vapour-air mixture becomes flammable when the vapour concentration reaches the **lower flammable limit (LFL)**.
4. At the **flash point temperature**, this vapour concentration first becomes ignitable — a brief flash occurs.
5. At the **fire point temperature**, vapour evolution is rapid enough to sustain a flame for ≥ 5 seconds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Higher the flash point → Safer the bitumen during heating → Better suitability for hot mix plants in tropical climates like Kerala, where ambient temperature is already 30–35 °C.**

## 2.2 Chemistry Behind the Phenomenon

| Property | Light Crude Bitumen | Heavy/Aged Bitumen |
|---|---|---|
| Volatile fraction | High | Low |
| Flash Point | Lower (~180–220 °C) | Higher (~250–300 °C) |
| Viscosity at 60 °C | Low | High |
| Susceptibility to oxidation | High | Low |

As bitumen **ages in service** (oxidation, loss of volatiles), the flash point **increases**. As bitumen is **overheated or contaminated with solvents**, the flash point **decreases** dangerously.

## 2.3 Cleveland Open Cup (COC) Apparatus — Detailed Description

The apparatus consists of:

1. **Test Cup** — A brass cup, approximately **64 mm diameter × 46 mm depth**, with a filling mark.
2. **Heating Plate (Hot Plate)** — Electric or gas, capable of controlling temperature rise at prescribed rates.
3. **Thermometer** — Graduated, with range covering the expected flash and fire points (typically **-6 °C to 400 °C**).
4. **Test Flame Source** — A small gas jet (natural gas / LPG) with a flame of diameter **3.2–4.8 mm**.
5. **Flame Application Device** — A swivelling arm that passes the flame across the cup at a uniform rate.
6. **Shield** — A draught shield, typically 460 mm × 460 mm × 305 mm tall, to protect the flame from ambient air currents.
7. **Support Stand** — To hold the thermometer vertically with the bulb immersed to a specified depth.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Atmospheric pressure correction for Flash/Fire Point | $T_c = T_o + 0.033 \cdot (760 - P)$ | °C | $P$ in mm Hg; or $T_c = T_o + 0.25 \cdot (101.325 - P_k)$ where $P_k$ in kPa |
| 2 | Heating rate (far from expected FP) | $\dfrac{dT}{dt} = 14$ to $17$ | °C/min | Until within ~56 °C of expected FP |
| 3 | Heating rate (near expected FP) | $\dfrac{dT}{dt} = 5$ to $6$ | °C/min | For the last ~28 °C before flash point |
| 4 | Heating rate (near Fire Point) | $\dfrac{dT}{dt} = 1$ to $2$ | °C/min | Slower for accurate fire point reading |
| 5 | Test flame pass interval | $\Delta T = 2$ | °C | Pass flame across cup at every 2 °C rise |
| 6 | Fire point minimum burn duration | $t \geq 5$ | s | Must burn continuously for ≥ 5 s |
| 7 | Difference (Fire − Flash) | $\Delta T_{FP-FP} = 5$ to $15$ | °C | Typical for paving bitumen |
| 8 | Minimum Flash Point (Paving Bitumen, IS 73) | $T_{FP} \geq 220$ | °C | Specified in IS 73:2013 |
| 9 | Minimum Flash Point (Cutback RC/MC, IS 217) | varies (27 to 93 °C) | °C | Depends on grade (RC-70, MC-30 etc.) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction Formula in kPa form (preferred in KTU):**
>
> $$T_c = T_o + 0.25 \cdot (101.325 - P_k)$$
>
> where $T_c$ = corrected temperature, $T_o$ = observed temperature, $P_k$ = atmospheric pressure in kPa. **Always add the correction** — exam questions often test this.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of Flash Point Data |
|---|---|
| **Hot Mix Plant Safety** | Determines max safe heating temperature |
| **Storage & Transport Safety** | Tank design, fire-fighting equipment selection |
| **Cutback Bitumen Selection** | Lower flash → faster curing, higher fire risk |
| **Quality Control at Refinery** | Detects contamination, overheating, or adulteration |
| **Pavement Performance** | Indicates degree of ageing / volatility |
| **Insurance & Statutory** | Mandated for hazardous material handling |

## 2.6 Limitations of the Test

- **Empirical** — results depend on apparatus geometry and heating rate.
- **Not suitable for bitumen emulsions** (water interferes) — separate IS 1448 (P:20) Tag closed cup used.
- **Atmospheric pressure** significantly affects the value; correction is essential at high-altitude labs.
- **Reproducibility** of ± 5 °C is acceptable per IS.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedure, Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Test Procedure (Cleveland Open Cup)

### Phase A — Preparation
1. **Clean the cup** thoroughly with solvent (carbon tetrachloride or similar) to remove any residue, then dry.
2. **Check the thermometer** for accuracy against a calibrated reference.
3. **Place the cup** on the heating plate, ensuring it is **level** (use a spirit level on the cup rim).
4. **Pour the bitumen sample** into the cup up to the **filling mark** (avoid spilling on the outer surface).
5. **Insert the thermometer** vertically with its bulb **immersed to a depth of 6.4 mm below the bitumen surface**, at a fixed position halfway between the centre and the wall.

### Phase B — Heating (Far from Expected Flash Point)
6. Apply heat such that the **temperature rises at 14–17 °C per minute** until the temperature is about **56 °C below the expected flash point**.

### Phase C — Controlled Heating (Near Flash Point)
7. **Reduce the heating rate to 5–6 °C per minute**. This is critical — too fast will overshoot the actual flash point.
8. As the temperature approaches the expected flash point, **pass the test flame across the cup at every 2 °C rise**.
9. The test flame is passed in a **straight line across the diameter of the cup**, in one smooth motion, taking about **1 second** to traverse.

### Phase D — Flash Point Determination
10. The **flash point is the temperature** at which a brief flash (ignition) is first observed **at any point on the surface** of the bitumen.
11. **Record the temperature** as the observed flash point $T_{o,FP}$.

### Phase E — Fire Point Determination
12. **Continue heating at 1–2 °C per minute**.
13. **Pass the test flame** across the surface periodically.
14. The **fire point is the temperature** at which the **vapours ignite and continue to burn for at least 5 seconds**.
15. **Record the temperature** as the observed fire point $T_{o,FireP}$.

### Phase F — Atmospheric Pressure Correction
16. Note the **atmospheric pressure** $P$ (in kPa) using a barometer at the time of test.
17. Apply the correction formula to obtain $T_{c,FP}$ and $T_{c,FireP}$.

### Phase G — Repeatability
18. **Repeat the test** on a fresh sample. The two flash point readings should agree within **± 5 °C** (per IS 1448). If not, perform a third test.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Observation and Calculation Template

| Observation / Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bitumen sample description | — | VG-30 (e.g.) | — |
| Mass of bitumen in cup | $m$ | As per cup fill mark | g |
| Heating rate (initial) | $r_1$ | 15 | °C/min |
| Heating rate (near flash point) | $r_2$ | 5–6 | °C/min |
| Heating rate (near fire point) | $r_3$ | 1–2 | °C/min |
| Test flame pass interval | $\Delta T$ | 2 | °C |
| Test flame diameter | $d$ | 3.2–4.8 | mm |
| Time of test | $t$ | 11:00 AM | — |
| Atmospheric pressure | $P$ | e.g. 100.5 | kPa |
| Observed flash point | $T_{o,FP}$ | to be measured | °C |
| Observed fire point | $T_{o,FireP}$ | to be measured | °C |
| **Corrected flash point** | $T_{c,FP}$ | calculated | °C |
| **Corrected fire point** | $T_{c,FireP}$ | calculated | °C |
| Difference | $\Delta = T_{c,FireP} - T_{c,FP}$ | calculated | °C |

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Typical KTU Question)

> **Problem:** A Cleveland Open Cup test is conducted on a sample of VG-30 paving bitumen. The observed flash point is **248 °C** and the observed fire point is **262 °C**. The atmospheric pressure at the time of test is **98.5 kPa**. Determine the **corrected flash and fire points** and comment on the suitability of the bitumen.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Apply the atmospheric pressure correction formula for Flash Point.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{c,FP} &= T_{o,FP} + 0.25 \cdot (101.325 - P) \\
&= 248 + 0.25 \cdot (101.325 - 98.5) \\
&= 248 + 0.25 \cdot 2.825 \\
&= 248 + 0.706 \\
&= 248.71 \;\text{°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the correction for Fire Point.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{c,FireP} &= T_{o,FireP} + 0.25 \cdot (101.325 - P) \\
&= 262 + 0.25 \cdot (101.325 - 98.5) \\
&= 262 + 0.706 \\
&= 262.71 \;\text{°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the difference.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= T_{c,FireP} - T_{c,FP} \\
&= 262.71 - 248.71 \\
&= 4.00 \;\text{°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Comment on suitability.

- The corrected flash point is **248.71 °C**, which is **greater than the IS 73:2013 minimum requirement of 220 °C**. ✅
- Therefore, the bitumen is **safe to heat up to about 220 °C** at the hot mix plant.
- The **fire point – flash point difference is only 4 °C**, which is slightly lower than the typical 5–15 °C range. This indicates a **narrow safety margin**, possibly due to the presence of light volatiles or partial aging. The mix design and heating protocol should be carefully controlled.

**Step 5:** (Optional) Express the corrected values rounded to the nearest 1 °C as per IS reporting convention.

$$
T_{c,FP} \approx 249 \;\text{°C}, \qquad T_{c,FireP} \approx 263 \;\text{°C}
$$

## 3.4 Derived Expression for the Atmospheric Correction

The empirical formula $T_c = T_o + 0.25 \cdot (101.325 - P)$ arises from the **Clausius–Clapeyron relationship** for vapour pressure variation with ambient pressure:

$$
\ln \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1} \right)
$$

For a small change in ambient pressure $\Delta P$, the corresponding shift in flash point is approximately:

$$
\Delta T \approx \dfrac{R T^2}{\Delta H_{vap}} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{P}
$$

For bitumen-type hydrocarbons, the constant $\dfrac{R T^2}{\Delta H_{vap}}$ at typical flash point temperatures is approximately **0.25 °C/kPa**, which is the empirical constant used in the IS 1448 (P:21) formula.

> [!NOTE]
> Students are **not required** to derive this from Clausius–Clapeyron in KTU exams. The **empirical formula is sufficient** and is what will be asked. However, knowing the **physical origin** of the constant $0.25$ gives you confidence and is a great way to score bonus points in viva.

## 3.5 Observation Recording Format (What to Write in the Lab Record)

| Trial | Observed Flash Point (°C) | Observed Fire Point (°C) | Atm. Pressure (kPa) | Corrected Flash Pt (°C) | Corrected Fire Pt (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 248 | 262 | 98.5 | 248.7 | 262.7 |
| 2 | 247 | 261 | 98.5 | 247.7 | 261.7 |
| Mean | 247.5 | 261.5 | — | 248.2 | 262.2 |
| Specified limit (IS 73:2013) | — | — | — | ≥ 220 | — |
| Result | — | — | — | **Pass** | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cleveland Open Cup (COC) Test Apparatus — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["HEATING ASSEMBLY"]
        HP["Hot Plate\nElectric / Gas"]
        CUP["Brass Test Cup\n64 mm dia x 46 mm depth"]
        SAMP["Bitumen Sample\nFilled to mark"]
        THM["Thermometer\nBulb 6.4 mm below surface"]
    end

    subgraph B["FLAME APPLICATION SYSTEM"]
        GAS["Gas Supply\nNatural Gas / LPG"]
        JET["Test Flame Nozzle\n3.2 to 4.8 mm dia"]
        ARM["Swivel Arm\n1 s traverse time"]
    end

    subgraph C["PROTECTIVE SHIELD"]
        SHD["Draught Shield\n460 x 460 x 305 mm"]
    end

    subgraph D["OBSERVATION & LOGGING"]
        OBS["Observer Eye Position\nSafe distance"]
        REC["Temperature Log\nEvery 2 deg C interval"]
        CALC["Atmospheric Pressure\nCorrection Calculation"]
    end

    SAMP --> CUP
    CUP --> HP
    THM --> CUP
    GAS --> JET
    JET --> ARM
    ARM -. "passes across cup" .-> CUP
    SHD -. "encloses" .-> CUP
    CUP --> OBS
    THM --> REC
    REC --> CALC
```

## 4.2 Test Procedure Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0(["START: Sample VG-30 Bitumen"]) --> P1["Clean Cup with Solvent\nand Dry"]
    P1 --> P2["Fill Cup to Marking\nInsert Thermometer"]
    P2 --> P3["Begin Heating at 14 to 17 deg C per min"]
    P3 --> Q1{"Temperature is\nwithin 56 deg C\nof expected FP?"}
    Q1 -->|"No"| P3
    Q1 -->|"Yes"| P4["Reduce Heat to 5 to 6 deg C per min"]
    P4 --> P5["Pass Test Flame\nEvery 2 deg C Rise"]
    P5 --> Q2{"Flash Observed\non Surface?"}
    Q2 -->|"No"| P5
    Q2 -->|"Yes"| P6["Record Observed Flash Point\nTO FP"]
    P6 --> P7["Continue Heating at 1 to 2 deg C per min"]
    P7 --> P8["Pass Test Flame at Intervals"]
    P8 --> Q3{"Sustained Burn\nfor 5 seconds?"}
    Q3 -->|"No"| P8
    Q3 -->|"Yes"| P9["Record Observed Fire Point\nTO FireP"]
    P9 --> P10["Note Atmospheric Pressure P"]
    P10 --> P11["Apply Correction Formula\nTC = TO + 0.25 x 101.325 - P"]
    P11 --> P12["Report Corrected\nFlash and Fire Points"]
    P12 --> S1(["END"])

    style S0 fill:#90EE90
    style S1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style P6 fill:#FFD700
    style P9 fill:#FFA07A
    style P11 fill:#87CEEB
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Flash vs Fire Point Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph E1["HEAT INPUT"]
        E1A["Bitumen in Cup\nHeated by Hot Plate"]
    end

    subgraph E2["VAPOUR GENERATION"]

        E2B["Vapour Air Mixture\nin Headspace"]
    end

    end

        F2["SUSTAINED BURN\n5 seconds or more\nCONTINUOUS COMBUSTION\n= FIRE POINT"]
    end

    E2B --> E3A

    E3A --> F2
```
