# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question Paper, Set A]**

### **Q1. (a) Define Flash Point and Fire Point of a bituminous material. How are they experimentally distinguished?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Concept | Answer |
|---|---|
| **Flash Point** | The **lowest temperature** at which the **vapours of a bituminous material momentarily ignite** (brief flash) when a small test flame is brought near the surface, under specified conditions of test (typically Cleveland Open Cup, IS 1448 P:21). **(1 mark)** |
| **Fire Point** | The **lowest temperature** at which the **vapours sustain combustion for at least 5 seconds** when a test flame is brought near the surface, under the same specified conditions. **(1 mark)** |
| **Distinguishing feature** | The **duration of combustion** is the key differentiator. Flash Point = **transient flash** (less than 1 s). Fire Point = **sustained burn for ≥ 5 s**. The Fire Point is always **higher** than the Flash Point for any given bituminous material. **(1 mark)** |

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question Paper, Set B]**

### **Q1. (b) Why is the Flash Point test important for paving grade bitumen used in hot mix plants?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Safety criterion** — The flash point indicates the **maximum safe temperature** to which bitumen can be heated. IS 73:2013 specifies a **minimum flash point of 220 °C** for paving grade bitumen. **(1 mark)**
2. **Fire hazard assessment** — During hot mix plant operations, bitumen is heated to **150–170 °C**. If heated above the flash point, **vapour ignition** can cause plant fires and explosions. The test allows the engineer to set a **safe operating limit** with a safety margin. **(1 mark)**
3. **Quality indicator** — A **lower-than-expected** flash point indicates **contamination with solvents, overheating, or presence of cutback additives**. Hence the test serves as a **routine QC check** at the bitumen storage terminal. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2 Experiment 2, Set A]**

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**Determine the Flash and Fire Point of the given bitumen sample using the Cleveland Open Cup apparatus. Describe the apparatus, procedure, observations, calculation (with atmospheric pressure correction), and specify the relevant IS code.**

#### (a) **Apparatus Description and IS Code Specification** — **7 Marks**

**Model Answer:**

The test is performed as per **IS 1448 (P:21) : 1992 (Reaffirmed 2014)** — *Methods of Test for Petroleum and its Products — Flash and Fire Point by Cleveland Open Cup Method*.

The **Cleveland Open Cup (COC) apparatus** consists of the following components:

| # | Component | Specification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Test cup** | Brass, internal diameter **64 mm**, depth **46 mm**, with filling mark |
| 2 | **Heating plate** | Electric or gas-fired, capable of controlled heating |
| 3 | **Thermometer** | Stem type, graduated in **1 °C** divisions, range **−6 °C to 400 °C** |
| 4 | **Test flame applicator** | Swivel arm with gas jet; flame diameter **3.2–4.8 mm** |
| 5 | **Draught shield** | Sheet metal, **460 × 460 × 305 mm** tall, three sides closed |
| 6 | **Support stand** | To hold thermometer vertically |
| 7 | **Spirit level** | To ensure the cup is horizontal |

**Working Principle:** The bitumen is heated at a controlled rate in an open cup. A small test flame is passed across the surface at regular temperature intervals. The temperature at which the first brief flash appears is the **flash point**; the temperature at which sustained burning for ≥ 5 s occurs is the **fire point**. **(Full 7 marks)** — Award marks as: IS code reference (1), cup details (1), thermometer (1), flame (1), shield + level (1), working principle (2).

#### (b) **Procedure, Observation Table, and Calculation** — **7 Marks**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 – Sample Preparation (1 mark):** Clean the cup with solvent. Fill with bitumen up to the filling mark. Position the thermometer with bulb **6.4 mm below the bitumen surface**.

**Step 2 – Heating Schedule (1 mark):**
- **Phase 1:** Heat at **14–17 °C/min** until temperature is within **56 °C of expected flash point**.
- **Phase 2:** Reduce to **5–6 °C/min** and pass the test flame across the cup **every 2 °C rise**.

**Step 3 – Flash Point Reading (1 mark):** Note the temperature at which the **first brief flash** appears anywhere on the surface as $T_{o,FP}$.

**Step 4 – Fire Point Reading (1 mark):** Continue heating at **1–2 °C/min**. The **fire point** $T_{o,FireP}$ is the temperature at which the **vapours burn continuously for ≥ 5 s**.

**Step 5 – Observation Table (1.5 marks):**

| Trial | Observed Flash Pt (°C) | Observed Fire Pt (°C) | Atm. Pressure (kPa) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 246 | 260 | 99.0 |
| 2 | 247 | 261 | 99.0 |
| Mean | 246.5 | 260.5 | 99.0 |

**Step 6 – Atmospheric Pressure Correction (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{c,FP} &= 246.5 + 0.25 \cdot (101.325 - 99.0) \\
&= 246.5 + 0.25 \cdot 2.325 \\
&= 246.5 + 0.58 = 247.08 \approx 247 \;\text{°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{c,FireP} &= 260.5 + 0.25 \cdot (101.325 - 99.0) \\
&= 260.5 + 0.58 = 261.08 \approx 261 \;\text{°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Corrected Flash Point = **247 °C**; Corrected Fire Point = **261 °C**. Both exceed the IS 73:2013 minimum flash point of 220 °C, hence the bitumen is **suitable for paving use**. **Difference** = 261 − 247 = **14 °C**, which lies in the normal 5–15 °C range.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2 Experiment 2, Set B]**

### **Question B (14 Marks):**

**A VG-30 grade bitumen sample gives an observed flash point of 252 °C and observed fire point of 268 °C in a Cleveland Open Cup test conducted at an atmospheric pressure of 96.8 kPa. (a) Compute the corrected flash and fire points, commenting on the suitability of the sample. (b) Explain the significance of the test with reference to (i) hot mix plant safety, (ii) quality control, and (iii) atmospheric pressure correction.**

#### (a) **Numerical Computation and Suitability Comment** — **7 Marks**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Identify the relevant IS code and formula (1 mark):**
- Applicable code: **IS 1448 (P:21)**.
- Correction formula: $T_c = T_o + 0.25 \cdot (101.325 - P_k)$ where $P_k$ is in kPa.

**Step 2 – Apply correction to Flash Point (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{c,FP} &= 252 + 0.25 \cdot (101.325 - 96.8) \\
&= 252 + 0.25 \cdot 4.525 \\
&= 252 + 1.13 \\
&= 253.13 \;\text{°C} \approx 253 \;\text{°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Apply correction to Fire Point (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{c,FireP} &= 268 + 0.25 \cdot (101.525 - 96.8) \\
&\text{(same atmospheric pressure used)} \\
&= 268 + 1.13 \\
&= 269.13 \;\text{°C} \approx 269 \;\text{°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Compute the difference (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta = T_{c,FireP} - T_{c,FP} = 269.13 - 253.13 = 16.0 \;\text{°C}
$$

**Step 5 – Suitability comment (2 marks):**
- Corrected flash point **253 °C > 220 °C** (IS 73:2013 minimum) → **bitumen passes the safety criterion**. ✅
- The difference (16 °C) is on the **higher end** of the normal 5–15 °C range, indicating **lower volatile content** (could be slightly aged) — the bitumen will require **slightly higher mixing temperatures** for desired workability.
- Recommended plant mixing temperature: **160 °C** (well below 253 °C flash point → **safe**).

#### (b) **Significance Explanation (3 sub-parts × ~2.3 marks each = 7 marks)**

**(i) Hot Mix Plant Safety (2.3 marks):**
- The flash point (253 °C) represents the **lower bound of flammability**. Plant operators must ensure that **bitumen storage and mixing temperatures are maintained at least 30–50 °C below the flash point** for a safety margin.
- For VG-30 bitumen, mixing temperature in the drum mixer is **155–165 °C** — well within the safe zone.
- Exceeding the flash point leads to **vapour-phase ignition**, which can cause **catastrophic tank fires** if vapours accumulate in confined spaces.

**(ii) Quality Control (2.3 marks):**
- A **drop in flash point** between successive batches at the same source indicates **contamination with light solvents, diesel, or kerosene** — common adulterants.
- A **sudden rise in flash point** may indicate **overheating during storage**, oxidation, or change of source crude.
- Hence the test is a **mandatory QC test** at every lot of bitumen received at the hot mix plant.

**(iii) Atmospheric Pressure Correction (2.3 marks):**
- The flash point is **pressure-dependent** — lower ambient pressure → lower flash point.
- At high-altitude locations (e.g., hill projects in Munnar, Wayanad), atmospheric pressure can be **as low as 90 kPa**.
- The IS 1448 formula **0.25 °C per kPa** deviation accounts for the change in vapour concentration needed to reach the lower flammability limit.
- For KTU exams, the correction is **mandatory** — omitting it results in a **loss of 1–2 marks** per IS valuation norms.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting atmospheric pressure correction** — Most students report observed values directly. **Always correct to 101.325 kPa** using the formula $T_c = T_o + 0.25 \cdot (101.325 - P)$. Cost: **−1.5 to −2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing units in correction formula** — The constant 0.25 corresponds to **kPa**. If pressure is given in **mm Hg** (760 mm Hg at STP), use $T_c = T_o + 0.033 \cdot (760 - P_{mm})$. Mixing up constants is a frequent error.
> 3. **Reporting fire point temperature as flash point** — Read the question carefully. Fire point is always **5–15 °C higher** than flash point.
> 4. **Ignoring IS 73:2013 specification (220 °C minimum)** — Suitability comments are mandatory; numerical values alone are insufficient.
> 5. **Skipping apparatus diagram or schematic** — KTU expects a **labelled sketch of COC apparatus**. Without it, **−1 to −2 marks** in Part B (a).
> 6. **Not mentioning the 5-second burn duration** criterion for fire point — the **5 s** is the most-asked viva question.
> 7. **Heating rate confusion** — Some students write "5–6 °C per minute" for the entire test. Correct answer: **14–17 °C/min initially, then 5–6 °C/min near flash, then 1–2 °C/min near fire point**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Flash & Fire Point of Bitumen**

- ✅ **Flash Point** = temperature of first **transient flash** (vapour ignites momentarily).
- ✅ **Fire Point** = temperature of **sustained burn ≥ 5 seconds**.
- ✅ **Apparatus:** Cleveland Open Cup (COC), as per **IS 1448 (P:21)**.
- ✅ **Cup:** Brass, **64 mm × 46 mm**, with filling mark.
- ✅ **Thermometer bulb depth:** **6.4 mm below bitumen surface**.
- ✅ **Heating rates:** **14–17 °C/min** (initial) → **5–6 °C/min** (near flash point) → **1–2 °C/min** (near fire point).
- ✅ **Flame pass interval:** Every **2 °C** rise near flash point; flame diameter **3.2–4.8 mm**.
- ✅ **Atmospheric correction (kPa):** $T_c = T_o + 0.25 \cdot (101.325 - P)$.
- ✅ **Atmospheric correction (mm Hg):** $T_c = T_o + 0.033 \cdot (760 - P)$.
- ✅ **Minimum Flash Point for paving bitumen (IS 73:2013):** **220 °C**.
- ✅ **Typical Fire − Flash difference:** **5–15 °C**.
- ✅ **Significance:** Plant safety, quality control, contamination detection.
- ✅ **Lower flash point → higher fire hazard → reject or investigate.**
- ✅ **High flash point** generally indicates **aged, oxidized, or low-volatility bitumen** (may need higher mixing temperature).
- ✅ **Test is NOT suitable for bitumen emulsions** (use Tag closed cup, IS 1448 P:20).
- ✅ **Repeatability:** Two trials should agree within **± 5 °C**.
- ✅ **Test flame traverse time:** approximately **1 second** across the cup.
- ✅ **Use a draught shield** to avoid ambient air currents affecting results.
- ✅ **Always report the corrected (not observed) value** in the final result.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Bituminous Mix by Marshall Method of Mix Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL607 - TRANSPORTATION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Bituminous Mix by Marshall Method of Mix Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Marshall Method of Mix Design** is an empirical, laboratory-based, performance-oriented mix design procedure for Hot Mix Asphalt (HMA) developed originally by **Bruce G. Marshall** of the Mississippi Highway Department in **1939**, and later formalized and standardized by the **U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (Waterways Experiment Station, Vicksburg)** during **World War II (1943)** for designing dense-graded bituminous mixes for airfield pavements.

The method is now codified under **ASTM D6926** (specimen preparation) and **ASTM D6927** (stability and flow testing), and is described in detail in the **Asphalt Institute's MS-2 Manual (Mix Design Methods for Asphalt Concrete and Other Hot-Mix Types)**. The procedure determines the **Optimum Binder Content (OBC)** for a given aggregate blend by measuring the **Marshall Stability (kN)**, **Flow (mm)**, **Density**, **Air Voids (Va)**, **Voids in Mineral Aggregate (VMA)**, and **Voids Filled with Asphalt (VFA)** of compacted specimens at varying asphalt contents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Syllabus Highlight:** In PCCEL607, the Marshall Stability test is performed on compacted specimens using a **loading frame with a proving ring / load cell** at a constant deformation rate of **50.8 mm/min (2 inches/minute)**, and the load is applied through curved steel loading heads (testing jig) until specimen failure.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Concrete Cake" Model

Imagine you are baking a fruit cake for a large group:

- **Aggregates (stones, sand)** are like the **flour and fruits** — they form the skeleton/structure.
- **Bitumen (asphalt binder)** is the **butter/egg mixture** — it glues everything together and fills the gaps.
- **Air Voids** are the **tiny air pockets** in the batter — too many and the cake crumbles; too few and it bursts.
- **VMA (Voids in Mineral Aggregate)** is the **total gap-volume between flour particles**, regardless of whether it is filled with butter or air.
- **VFA (Voids Filled with Asphalt)** is the fraction of those gaps actually filled with butter.

If you add too little butter → the cake is dry and weak (low stability). If you add too much butter → the cake is soggy, deforms under weight, and bleeds butter (low stability, excessive flow, low air voids). The Marshall Method is essentially the **engineering recipe-finder** that pinpoints the *exact amount of butter (bitumen)* that produces the strongest, most durable cake (pavement).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Mapping:** The "cake" becomes a **flexible pavement surface course** carrying thousands of vehicles daily. A poor mix = rutting, cracking, bleeding, potholes, and premature failure.

## 1.3 Physical Standards & Test Parameters (Bold Constants)

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Compaction effort (Heavy traffic) | **75 blows per face** (each end) |
| Compaction effort (Light/Medium traffic) | **50 blows per face** |
| Specimen diameter | **101.6 mm (4 inches)** |
| Specimen height | **63.5 mm (2.5 inches) ± 1.27 mm** |
| Hammer weight | **4.536 kg (10 lbs)** |
| Drop height of hammer | **457.2 mm (18 inches)** |
| Loading rate during test | **50.8 mm/min (2 inches/min)** |
| Testing temperature | **60 °C ± 1 °C (140 °F)** |
| Number of asphalt contents tested | **Minimum 5 (variation of 0.5% increments)** |
| Typically tested range | **3%, 3.5%, 4%, 4.5%, 5%, 5.5%, 6% binder by weight of mix** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Breakdown — The Six-Step Design Philosophy

The Marshall Method rests on the philosophy that an ideal bituminous mix must simultaneously satisfy **three pillars of performance**:

1. **Stability** → Resistance to deformation under traffic loads (rutting & shoving resistance).
2. **Durability** → Resistance to weathering, aging, and disintegration (air-void controlled).
3. **Flexibility / Fatigue Resistance** → Ability to bend without cracking (flow & VMA controlled).

To quantify these, the procedure is executed in the following logical sequence:

### Step 1: Aggregate Selection & Gradation
- Select aggregate blend that conforms to the specified **gradation envelope** (e.g., Bituminous Concrete Grade 1 or 2 as per **MoRTH 5th Revision, Table 500-17**).
- Determine **Bulk Specific Gravity of Aggregate (Gsb)**, **Apparent Specific Gravity (Gsa)**, and **Effective Specific Gravity (Gse)**.

### Step 2: Binder Selection
- Select bitumen grade (typically **VG-30 or VG-40** for Indian conditions, or **60/70 penetration grade** for older IS-73 nomenclature).
- Determine **Specific Gravity of Binder (Gb)**.

### Step 3: Specimen Preparation (ASTM D6926)
- For each trial asphalt content, prepare **at least 3 replicate specimens** (recommended 3 to 5).
- Heat aggregates and binder to **mixing temperature** (viscosity of binder ≈ 170 ± 20 cSt).
- Compact at **compaction temperature** (viscosity ≈ 280 ± 30 cSt).
- Compact using the **Marshall Hammer** (drop-hammer compactor).

### Step 4: Volumetric Determination
- Measure **Bulk Specific Gravity of Compacted Mix (Gmb)** using saturated-surface-dry (SSD) method per **ASTM D2726**.
- Determine **Theoretical Maximum Specific Gravity (Gmm)** using **Rice Test (ASTM D2041)**.
- Calculate **Air Voids (Va)**, **VMA**, and **VFA**.

### Step 5: Mechanical Testing (ASTM D6927)
- Condition specimens in water bath at **60 ± 1 °C for 30–40 minutes**.
- Place specimen in the **Marshall testing head** (curved loading strips).
- Apply load at **50.8 mm/min** until peak load (failure) is reached.
- Record **Stability (kN)** and **Flow (mm)**.

### Step 6: Optimum Binder Content (OBC) Selection
Plot the following graphs (binder content on X-axis, property on Y-axis):
1. Stability vs. Binder Content
2. Flow vs. Binder Content
3. Unit Weight (Density) vs. Binder Content
4. % Air Voids (Va) vs. Binder Content
5. % VMA vs. Binder Content
6. % VFA vs. Binder Content

The **OBC** is determined as the **average of three values**:
- **Binder content at peak stability**
- **Binder content at peak unit weight (density)**
- **Binder content at 4% air voids (median design air void target = 3–5% as per MoRTH)**

Then verify that at OBC, the mix satisfies all **Marshall Design Criteria** (limits in Section 2.3).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bulk Specific Gravity of Mix (Gmb) | $\dfrac{W_{dry}}{W_{SSD} - W_{sub}}$ | — | SSD method; ASTM D2726 |
| 2 | Theoretical Max. Specific Gravity (Gmm) | $\dfrac{100}{\dfrac{P_s}{G_{se}} + \dfrac{P_b}{G_b}}$ | — | Rice Test; ASTM D2041 |
| 3 | Effective Specific Gravity of Aggregate (Gse) | $\dfrac{100 - P_b}{\dfrac{100}{G_{mm}} - \dfrac{P_b}{G_b}}$ | — | Back-calculated from Rice |
| 4 | Air Voids (Va) | $\dfrac{G_{mm} - G_{mb}}{G_{mm}} \times 100$ | % | Target = 3–5% (MoRTH) |
| 5 | VMA (Voids in Mineral Aggregate) | $100 - \dfrac{G_{mb} \cdot P_s}{G_{sb}}$ | % | Depends on nominal max size |
| 6 | VFA (Voids Filled with Asphalt) | $\dfrac{VMA - V_a}{VMA} \times 100$ | % | Target = 65–75% (MoRTH) |
| 7 | Unit Weight (Density) of Mix | $\rho_{mix} = G_{mb} \times \rho_{water}$ | kg/m³ or g/cc | $\rho_{water} = 998 \text{ kg/m³} \text{ at } 25°C$ |
| 8 | Corrected Stability | $S_{corr} = S_{measured} \times C$ | kN | C = stability correction ratio (ASTM D6927 Table) |
| 9 | % Aggregate by weight of mix | $P_s = 100 - P_b$ | % | P_b = % binder by weight of mix |
| 10 | Volume of Specimen (V) | $\dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} \times h$ | mm³ or cc | Cylinder: D = 101.6 mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL EXAM TIP:** In KTU evaluations, students often confuse `Va` (air voids in compacted mix) with `VMA` (voids in mineral aggregate). Remember: **VMA is a property of the aggregate skeleton alone** (the total inter-particle void space), while **Va is a property of the compacted mix** (the air that remains unfilled by binder).

## 2.3 Marshall Design Criteria (MoRTH 5th Revision — Table 500-11)

| Property | Light Traffic (50 blows) | Medium/Heavy Traffic (75 blows) |
|---|---|---|
| **Marshall Stability (kN), minimum** | **5.0 (≈ 500 kgf)** | **9.0 (≈ 900 kgf)** |
| **Flow (mm)** | **2 – 4** | **2 – 4** |
| **% Air Voids (Va)** | **3 – 5** | **3 – 5** |
| **% VMA, minimum (Nominal 19 mm)** | **13** | **13** |
| **% VMA, minimum (Nominal 13.2 mm)** | **14** | **14** |
| **% VFA** | **65 – 75** | **65 – 75** |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Highway & Expressway Construction** → NHAI, MoRTH, PWD projects.
- **Airport Runways & Taxiways** → Originally designed for this exact purpose.
- **Urban Arterial & Industrial Pavements** → Truck-heavy loading zones.
- **Bridge Deck Wearing Courses** → Requires precise OBC for thin overlays.
- The Marshall design forms the basis for **Job Mix Formula (JMF)** approval at every asphalt plant in India, enforced by MoRTH Specifications for Road and Bridge Works.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation of Volumetric Relations

### 3.1.1 Bulk Specific Gravity of Compacted Specimen (Gmb)

By Archimedes' principle applied to the SSD-weight method:

Let:
- $W_{dry}$ = weight of dry specimen in air (g)
- $W_{SSD}$ = weight of saturated surface-dry specimen in air (g)
- $W_{sub}$ = weight of specimen submerged in water (g)

The volume of the specimen (volume of solids + volume of impermeable voids + volume of permeable air voids) is:

$$
V = W_{SSD} - W_{sub}
$$

The mass of the specimen (after SSD wiping) is $W_{dry}$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{G_{mb} = \dfrac{W_{dry}}{W_{SSD} - W_{sub}}}
$$

### 3.1.2 Theoretical Maximum Specific Gravity (Gmm) — Rice Method

Assume the mix has $P_s$ % aggregate (by weight) and $P_b$ % binder. The total mass of the mix is normalized to 100 units. The absolute volumes of each component are:

$$
V_{aggregate}^{abs} = \dfrac{P_s}{G_{se}}, \quad V_{binder}^{abs} = \dfrac{P_b}{G_b}
$$

Since the **Rice test** is performed on a *loose* (uncompacted) mix, there are **no air voids** in the sample. The total absolute volume of the mix equals the sum of component absolute volumes. Therefore:

$$
G_{mm} = \dfrac{\text{Total mass}}{\text{Total absolute volume}} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{P_s}{G_{se}} + \dfrac{P_b}{G_b}}
$$

Substituting $P_s = 100 - P_b$:

$$
\boxed{G_{mm} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{100 - P_b}{G_{se}} + \dfrac{P_b}{G_b}}}
$$

> **Why it matters:** $G_{mm}$ represents the *theoretical limit* of density — what the mix would weigh if there were zero air voids. Comparing $G_{mb}$ (actual) with $G_{mm}$ (ideal) gives the air void content.

### 3.1.3 Air Voids (Va) — Direct Comparison

Air voids are simply the fractional volume of the compacted mix occupied by air (entrapped + entrained). By definition:

$$
V_a = \dfrac{V_{air}}{V_{total}} = \dfrac{V_{total} - V_{solids}}{V_{total}}
$$

Translating to specific gravities (specific gravity is the ratio of density to water density, so it represents relative volume per unit mass inversely):

$$
\boxed{V_a (\%) = \dfrac{G_{mm} - G_{mb}}{G_{mm}} \times 100}
$$

### 3.1.4 Voids in Mineral Aggregate (VMA) — Aggregate Skeleton Property

Consider a unit weight (100 g) of compacted mix. The mass of aggregate present is $P_s$ g, which occupies an absolute volume of $P_s / G_{sb}$ cc. The total volume of the compacted mix (in cc) is $100 / G_{mb}$ (since mass = density × volume, and $\rho = G_{mb} \times \rho_{water}$, with $\rho_{water} \approx 1$ g/cc).

Therefore, the volume of voids *between aggregate particles* (regardless of whether filled with binder or air) is:

$$
VMA = \dfrac{100}{G_{mb}} - \dfrac{P_s}{G_{sb}}
$$

Expressing as a percentage:

$$
\boxed{VMA (\%) = 100 - \dfrac{G_{mb} \cdot P_s}{G_{sb}}}
$$

### 3.1.5 Voids Filled with Asphalt (VFA)

Of the total VMA, the fraction occupied by effective binder (not air) is VFA. By definition:

$$
\boxed{VFA (\%) = \dfrac{VMA - V_a}{VMA} \times 100}
$$

### 3.1.6 Stability Correction (ASTM D6927, Table 1)

The measured stability load must be corrected because the standard specimen volume is **~ 510–520 cc**, corresponding to a height-to-diameter ratio of approximately 1. For non-standard volumes, a **dimensionless correction factor C** is applied:

$$
\boxed{S_{corrected} = S_{measured} \times C}
$$

The correction factor **C** is read from the ASTM D6927 correlation table (volume of specimen in cc → C value). Typical values:

| Volume (cc) | C | Volume (cc) | C |
|---|---|---|---|
| 488–499 | 1.00 | 556–566 | 0.93 |
| 500–510 | 0.99 | 567–579 | 0.92 |
| 511–522 | 0.98 | 580–591 | 0.91 |
| 523–535 | 0.97 | 592–604 | 0.90 |
| 536–546 | 0.96 | 605–617 | 0.89 |
| 547–555 | 0.95 | 618–630 | 0.88 |

## 3.2 Complete Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement:** A Marshall specimen has the following data:
- Weight in air (dry): $W_{dry} = 1225.0$ g
- SSD weight in air: $W_{SSD} = 1228.5$ g
- Weight in water: $W_{sub} = 715.0$ g
- Asphalt content: $P_b = 5.0\%$ (by weight of mix)
- $G_{mm}$ from Rice test = 2.480
- $G_{sb}$ (bulk specific gravity of aggregate blend) = 2.685
- $G_b$ (specific gravity of binder) = 1.020
- Specimen height = 64.0 mm, diameter = 101.6 mm
- Measured stability = 12.0 kN, measured flow = 3.2 mm
- Specimen volume (from table) = 514 cc

**Calculate Gmb, Va, VMA, VFA, and corrected stability. Comment on whether the mix is suitable for medium traffic.**

### Step 1: Bulk Specific Gravity of Mix (Gmb)

$$
G_{mb} = \dfrac{W_{dry}}{W_{SSD} - W_{sub}} = \dfrac{1225.0}{1228.5 - 715.0} = \dfrac{1225.0}{513.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{G_{mb} = 2.385}
$$

### Step 2: Air Voids (Va)

$$
V_a = \dfrac{G_{mm} - G_{mb}}{G_{mm}} \times 100 = \dfrac{2.480 - 2.385}{2.480} \times 100
$$

$$
V_a = \dfrac{0.095}{2.480} \times 100 = 3.83\%
$$

$$
\boxed{V_a = 3.83\% \text{ (within 3–5\% target ✓)}}
$$

### Step 3: % Aggregate in the mix (Ps)

$$
P_s = 100 - P_b = 100 - 5.0 = 95.0\%
$$

### Step 4: Voids in Mineral Aggregate (VMA)

$$
VMA = 100 - \dfrac{G_{mb} \cdot P_s}{G_{sb}} = 100 - \dfrac{2.385 \times 95.0}{2.685}
$$

$$
VMA = 100 - \dfrac{226.575}{2.685} = 100 - 84.385 = 15.615\%
$$

$$
\boxed{VMA = 15.62\% \text{ (≥ 13% for 19 mm nominal size ✓)}}
$$

### Step 5: Voids Filled with Asphalt (VFA)

$$
VFA = \dfrac{VMA - V_a}{VMA} \times 100 = \dfrac{15.62 - 3.83}{15.62} \times 100
$$

$$
VFA = \dfrac{11.79}{15.62} \times 100 = 75.48\%
$$

$$
\boxed{VFA = 75.48\% \text{ (slightly above 65–75% upper limit ✗ — borderline)}}
$$

### Step 6: Corrected Stability

For specimen volume = 514 cc, from ASTM D6927 Table 1, **C = 0.98** (interpolated between 0.99 at 500–510 cc and 0.98 at 511–522 cc).

$$
S_{corrected} = 12.0 \times 0.98 = 11.76 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{corr} = 11.76 \text{ kN (≥ 9.0 kN for medium traffic ✓)}}
$$

### Step 7: Comment on Suitability

| Property | Value | Spec (Medium Traffic) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stability | 11.76 kN | ≥ 9.0 kN | ✓ Pass |
| Flow | 3.2 mm | 2 – 4 mm | ✓ Pass |
| Air Voids | 3.83 % | 3 – 5 % | ✓ Pass |
| VMA | 15.62 % | ≥ 13 % | ✓ Pass |
| VFA | 75.48 % | 65 – 75 % | ⚠ Marginal (slightly over) |

**Conclusion:** The mix marginally satisfies Marshall criteria for medium traffic, with VFA slightly above the upper limit. The design is acceptable but a small reduction in binder content (≈ 0.2%) is recommended to bring VFA within range.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Complete Marshall Mix Design Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Aggregate and Binder Selection] --> B[Determine Aggregate Gradation per MoRTH Specs]
    B --> C[Determine Gsb, Gsa, Gb, Gmm of constituents]
    C --> D[Estimate Trial Asphalt Contents: 5 to 6 levels e.g. 3.5% to 6% at 0.5% intervals]
    D --> E[For each binder content: Prepare 3 replicate specimens]
    E --> F[Heat aggregates and binder to mixing temperature]
    F --> G[Mix thoroughly in mechanical mixer]
    G --> H[Compact specimens using Marshall Hammer: 50 or 75 blows per face]
    H --> I[Allow specimens to cool and extract from mold]
    I --> J[Determine Bulk Specific Gravity Gmb via SSD method ASTM D2726]
    J --> K[Measure specimen height and diameter for volume check]
    K --> L[Condition specimens in water bath at 60C for 30-40 min]
    L --> M[Perform Stability and Flow Test in Marshall Loading Frame]
    M --> N[Apply load at 50.8 mm per min until failure]
    N --> O[Record peak load as Stability and deformation as Flow]
    O --> P[Apply Stability Correction Factor C using volume table]
    P --> Q[Calculate Va, VMA, VFA for each binder content]
    Q --> R[Plot 6 graphs: Stability, Flow, Density, Va, VMA, VFA vs Binder Content]
    R --> S[Determine OBC: Average of binder at peak stability, peak density, and 4% air voids]
    S --> T{Check All Marshall Criteria}
    T -->|All Pass| U[Approve Job Mix Formula JMF]
    T -->|Fails| V[Adjust aggregate blend or binder grade and restart from Step C]
    U --> W[END: Mix approved for field production]
    V --> E
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the Marshall Method of Mix Design. State any two design criteria used. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The Marshall Method of Mix Design is an empirical laboratory procedure used to determine the **Optimum Binder Content (OBC)** of a dense-graded Hot Mix Asphalt (HMA). Specimens are compacted with a standard hammer (50 or 75 blows per face) and tested for **Stability** (peak load at 60°C) and **Flow** (deformation at peak load). Volumetric properties (Va, VMA, VFA) are calculated and the OBC is selected as the average of the binder contents at peak stability, peak density, and 4% air voids.

**Two design criteria:**
1. Marshall Stability ≥ 9.0 kN (medium/heavy traffic) or ≥ 5.0 kN (light traffic).
2. Air Voids (Va) = 3% to 5%.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between VMA and VFA. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | VMA (Voids in Mineral Aggregate) | VFA (Voids Filled with Asphalt) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Total void space between compacted aggregate particles | Portion of VMA filled with effective binder |
| **Formula** | $VMA = 100 - \dfrac{G_{mb} \cdot P_s}{G_{sb}}$ | $VFA = \dfrac{VMA - V_a}{VMA} \times 100$ |
| **Depends on** | Aggregate gradation, compaction effort | Both VMA and the amount of binder added |
| **Significance** | Ensures enough room for binder + air voids | Indicates durability; too high → bleeding, too low → cracking |
| **Typical value** | ≥ 13% (19 mm nominal) | 65% – 75% |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Describe the step-by-step procedure for the Marshall Method of Mix Design as per ASTM D6926 and ASTM D6927.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Aggregate Selection:** Choose aggregate blend meeting the gradation envelope (e.g., MoRTH Table 500-17 for BC Grade 2). Determine $G_{sb}$ and $G_{sa}$.
2. **Binder Selection:** Select bitumen grade (VG-30/VG-40). Determine $G_b$.
3. **Trial Binder Contents:** Decide on a minimum of 5 asphalt contents spaced at 0.5% intervals (e.g., 3.5, 4.0, 4.5, 5.0, 5.5, 6.0%).
4. **Specimen Preparation:** For each binder content, prepare 3 replicate specimens. Heat aggregates and binder to mixing temperature. Mix thoroughly. Compact in a heated mold using the Marshall Hammer (75 blows per face for heavy traffic, 50 for light).
5. **Bulk Specific Gravity (Gmb):** Determine $G_{mb}$ using SSD method (ASTM D2726): $G_{mb} = W_{dry} / (W_{SSD} - W_{sub})$.
6. **Theoretical Maximum Specific Gravity (Gmm):** Perform the Rice test (ASTM D2041) on loose mix samples at each binder content.
7. **Volumetric Calculations:** Compute $V_a$, $VMA$, and $VFA$ using standard formulas.
8. **Mechanical Testing (ASTM D6927):** Condition specimens in a water bath at **60°C ± 1°C for 30–40 minutes**. Place in the testing head. Apply load at **50.8 mm/min** until failure. Record peak load (Stability) and corresponding deformation (Flow).
9. **Apply Stability Correction Factor** using ASTM D6927 Table 1 (based on specimen volume).
10. **Graphical Analysis:** Plot the six standard graphs (Stability, Flow, Density, Va, VMA, VFA vs binder content).
11. **OBC Determination:** $OBC = \dfrac{P_{b,peak\;stability} + P_{b,peak\;density} + P_{b,@4\%\;air\;voids}}{3}$
12. **Verification:** Check that the mix at OBC satisfies all Marshall criteria.

**(b) The following data were obtained for a Marshall specimen. Compute the volumetric properties and verify the suitability of the mix for medium traffic.** **(7 marks)**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| W_dry | 1220 g |
| W_SSD | 1224 g |
| W_sub | 710 g |
| G_mm (Rice) | 2.475 |
| G_sb (aggregate) | 2.670 |
| P_b (binder) | 5.0 % |
| Specimen volume | 515 cc |
| Measured stability | 11.0 kN |
| Flow | 3.0 mm |

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Gmb:**
$$
G_{mb} = \dfrac{1220}{1224 - 710} = \dfrac{1220}{514} = 2.374
$$

**Step 2 — Va:**
$$
V_a = \dfrac{2.475 - 2.374}{2.475} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.101}{2.475} \times 100 = 4.08\%
$$

**Step 3 — VMA:**
$$
VMA = 100 - \dfrac{2.374 \times 95.0}{2.670} = 100 - \dfrac{225.53}{2.670} = 100 - 84.47 = 15.53\%
$$

**Step 4 — VFA:**
$$
VFA = \dfrac{15.53 - 4.08}{15.53} \times 100 = \dfrac{11.45}{15.53} \times 100 = 73.7\%
$$

**Step 5 — Corrected Stability (V = 515 cc, C ≈ 0.98):**
$$
S_{corr} = 11.0 \times 0.98 = 10.78 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6 — Comparison with Marshall Criteria (Medium Traffic, 75 blows):**

| Property | Computed | Spec | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stability | 10.78 kN | ≥ 9.0 kN | ✓ Pass |
| Flow | 3.0 mm | 2 – 4 mm | ✓ Pass |
| Air Voids | 4.08% | 3 – 5% | ✓ Pass |
| VMA | 15.53% | ≥ 13% | ✓ Pass |
| VFA | 73.7% | 65 – 75% | ✓ Pass |

**Conclusion:** The mix **satisfies all Marshall design criteria** and is **suitable for medium traffic**.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the significance of Air Voids, VMA, and VFA in bituminous mix design.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Air Voids (Va):** Air voids are the small pockets of air trapped within the compacted bituminous mix (target 3–5%).

- **Too low (<3%):** Mix becomes plastic, prone to **rutting, bleeding, and shoving** under traffic, especially in hot weather. There is insufficient space for binder to expand thermally.
- **Too high (>5%):** Mix is permeable to air and water → accelerated **oxidation, hardening, and moisture damage** → cracking, raveling, and reduced fatigue life.
- **Significance:** Air voids are the most direct indicator of **durability vs. stability trade-off** in the mix.

**Voids in Mineral Aggregate (VMA):** VMA represents the total inter-particle void volume in the compacted aggregate skeleton (typically ≥ 13–14%).

- **Significance:** It defines the "space budget" available for both binder and air. If VMA is too low, the design air voids cannot be achieved without under-filling with binder (causing instability). If VMA is too high, the mix becomes uneconomical (excess binder).
- **VMA is governed by:** Nominal maximum aggregate size, gradation, and compaction effort.

**Voids Filled with Asphalt (VFA):** VFA is the percentage of VMA occupied by effective binder (target 65–75%).

- **High VFA:** Most voids are filled with binder → low air voids → low permeability but risk of bleeding.
- **Low VFA:** Most voids remain as air → mix is dry, prone to cracking and raveling.
- **Significance:** VFA bridges the relationship between **durability (air voids)** and **stability (binder content)**.

**(b) The following data are obtained for 5 trial binder contents. Determine the OBC graphically and verify the design. Aggregate $G_{sb}$ = 2.690, $G_{mm}$ at each binder content given below. Flow values are 2.5, 2.8, 3.1, 3.5, 4.0 mm respectively.** **(7 marks)**

| P_b (%) | Gmb | Gmm | Stability (kN) | Density (g/cc) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3.5 | 2.305 | 2.510 | 9.5 | 2.305 |
| 4.0 | 2.330 | 2.495 | 11.0 | 2.330 |
| 4.5 | 2.350 | 2.480 | 12.5 | 2.350 |
| 5.0 | 2.360 | 2.465 | 12.8 | 2.360 |
| 5.5 | 2.355 | 2.450 | 11.5 | 2.355 |

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute Va for each:**

$$
V_a(3.5) = \dfrac{2.510 - 2.305}{2.510} \times 100 = 8.17\%
$$

$$
V_a(4.0) = \dfrac{2.495 - 2.330}{2.495} \times 100 = 6.61\%
$$

$$
V_a(4.5) = \dfrac{2.480 - 2.350}{2.480} \times 100 = 5.24\%
$$

$$
V_a(5.0) = \dfrac{2.465 - 2.360}{2.465} \times 100 = 4.26\%
$$

$$
V_a(5.5) = \dfrac{2.450 - 2.355}{2.450} \times 100 = 3.88\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute VMA for each** ($P_s = 100 - P_b$):

$$
VMA(3.5) = 100 - \dfrac{2.305 \times 96.5}{2.690} = 100 - 82.70 = 17.30\%
$$

$$
VMA(4.0) = 100 - \dfrac{2.330 \times 96.0}{2.690} = 100 - 83.14 = 16.86\%
$$

$$
VMA(4.5) = 100 - \dfrac{2.350 \times 95.5}{2.690} = 100 - 83.43 = 16.57\%
$$

$$
VMA(5.0) = 100 - \dfrac{2.360 \times 95.0}{2.690} = 100 - 83.34 = 16.66\%
$$

$$
VMA(5.5) = 100 - \dfrac{2.355 \times 94.5}{2.690} = 100 - 82.74 = 17.26\%
$$

**Step 3 — Determine OBC from graphs:**

| Parameter | Binder Content (%) |
|---|---|
| Peak Stability | 5.0 (Stability = 12.8 kN) |
| Peak Density | 5.0 (Density = 2.360) |
| 4% Air Voids | ≈ 4.95 (interpolated between 5.0% and 5.5%) |

$$
OBC = \dfrac{5.0 + 5.0 + 4.95}{3} = \dfrac{14.95}{3} = 4.98\% \approx 5.0\%
$$

**Step 4 — Verify design at OBC = 5.0%:**

- Stability = 12.8 kN ≥ 9.0 kN ✓
- Flow = 3.5 mm ∈ [2, 4] mm ✓
- Va = 4.26% ∈ [3, 5] % ✓
- VMA = 16.66% ≥ 13% ✓
- VFA = (16.66 − 4.26)/16.66 × 100 = **74.4%** ∈ [65, 75] % ✓

**Conclusion:** Mix at **OBC = 5.0%** satisfies all Marshall criteria for medium traffic.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Gsb, Gsa, and Gse** — Students often use $G_{sa}$ (apparent) instead of $G_{sb}$ (bulk) in the VMA formula. Always use $G_{sb}$ for VMA calculation.
> 2. **Forgetting the Stability Correction Factor** — KTU examiners explicitly check this. Not applying the volume-based correction factor `C` from ASTM D6927 Table 1 results in loss of 1–2 marks per question.
> 3. **Mixing up the formula signs in Va** — The correct order is $(G_{mm} - G_{mb})/G_{mm}$, NOT $(G_{mb} - G_{mm})/G_{mm}$. Air voids cannot be negative in a properly compacted mix.
> 4. **OBC averaging** — The three binder contents to be averaged are: at peak stability, peak density, and at 4% air voids (NOT 3% or 5%).
> 5. **Specimen height tolerance** — Specimens outside 63.5 ± 1.27 mm (2.5 ± 0.05 in.) must be discarded per ASTM D6926. Students often retain them.
> 6. **Unit conversion of stability** — Some students leave it in kgf. The standard KTU/SI unit is **kN** (1 kgf = 9.81 N, so 900 kgf ≈ 8.83 kN, often rounded to 9.0 kN in specs).
> 7. **Temperature reporting** — Test must be at **60°C**, not 25°C or 100°C.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
> - **ASTM Standards:** D6926 (preparation), D6927 (testing), D2726 (Gmb), D2041 (Gmm - Rice Test).
> - **Compaction:** 75 blows/face (heavy traffic) or 50 blows/face (light traffic); hammer 4.536 kg; drop 457.2 mm.
> - **Loading Rate:** **50.8 mm/min**; Test Temperature: **60°C**.
> - **Specimen:** 101.6 mm dia × 63.5 mm height (approx. 510 cc volume).
> - **OBC = average of 3 binder contents:** at peak stability, peak density, and **4% air voids**.
> - **Design Va Target:** 3% to 5% (MoRTH); **VMA:** ≥ 13% (19 mm nom) or ≥ 14% (13.2 mm nom); **VFA:** 65% to 75%.
> - **Stability:** ≥ 9.0 kN (medium/heavy) or ≥ 5.0 kN (light); **Flow:** 2 – 4 mm.
> - **Key Formulas (Master These):**
>   * $G_{mb} = \dfrac{W_{dry}}{W_{SSD} - W_{sub}}$
>   * $G_{mm} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{P_s}{G_{se}} + \dfrac{P_b}{G_b}}$
>   * $V_a(\%) = \dfrac{G_{mm} - G_{mb}}{G_{mm}} \times 100$
>   * $VMA(\%) = 100 - \dfrac{G_{mb} \cdot P_s}{G_{sb}}$
>   * $VFA(\%) = \dfrac{VMA - V_a}{VMA} \times 100$
>   * $S_{corr} = S_{measured} \times C$ (C from ASTM D6927 Table 1)
> - **Six Graphs to Plot:** Stability, Flow, Density, Va, VMA, VFA — all versus binder content.
> - **Six Numbers to Report per binder content:** Gmb, Va, VMA, VFA, Stability (corrected), Flow.
> - **Always use $G_{sb}$ (NOT $G_{sa}$ or $G_{se}$) in the VMA formula.**
> - **Asphalt Institute Manual MS-2** is the authoritative reference for Marshall mix design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of MERLIN Apparatus to Determine Road Roughness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL607 - TRANSPORTATION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Use of MERLIN Apparatus to Determine Road Roughness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of MERLIN

**MERLIN (Machine for Evaluating Roughness using Low-cost INstrumentation)** is a portable, low-cost, response-type road roughness measuring instrument developed by the **Transport Research Laboratory (TRL), United Kingdom**. It is designed to measure the longitudinal profile (roughness) of a road surface by recording the cumulative vertical displacement of a wheel-tracing probe relative to a reference datum (chassis) as the device is pushed or pulled along the road.

The instrument was specifically engineered to provide a robust, low-cost alternative to expensive laser profilometers, especially in developing nations and for spot-checking of rural road networks.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCEL607 – Module 2):** The experiment evaluates the student's ability to operate the MERLIN apparatus, record displacement counts, and apply the standard TRL calibration equation to convert raw **MERLIN counts per km** into the universally accepted **International Roughness Index (IRI)** in m/km.

## 1.2 What is Road Roughness?

**Road Roughness** is the measure of irregularities in the road surface that affect vehicle ride quality, safety, and vehicle operating costs. It is a critical functional characteristic of pavement performance.

- **Definition (ASTM E867):** The deviations of a pavement surface from a true planar surface, with characteristic dimensions that affect vehicle dynamics, ride quality, dynamic loads, and drainage.
- **Standard Metric Used:** **International Roughness Index (IRI)** — measured in **m/km** or **mm/m**.
- **Acceptable IRI Limits (as per MoRTH/IRC standards):**
  - Expressways: ≤ **2.0 m/km**
  - National Highways: ≤ **2.5 m/km**
  - Rural Roads (PMGSY): ≤ **3.5 m/km**

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are **blindfolded and asked to feel the surface of a road using a long stick** held in your hand. As you walk forward, the stick rises and falls over every bump and dip. The **cumulative up-and-down movement** your arm records over a kilometre is essentially what the MERLIN measures.

- **The stick** → MERLIN's measuring wheel probe
- **Your arm** → The pivot arm and displacement counter
- **Walking 1 km** → The fixed test distance
- **The total wiggles recorded** → MERLIN Counts (which we mathematically translate to IRI)

> [!TIP]
> Think of MERLIN as a **"budget-friendly stethoscope for a road"** — it listens to the road's heartbeat (surface undulations) and gives the doctor (engineer) a single health number: the IRI.

## 1.4 Key Components of MERLIN

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Chassis (Reference Frame)** | The rigid body resting on a single road wheel and a support foot; provides the reference datum. |
| **Measuring Wheel (Probe Wheel)** | A small diameter wheel (typically 0.254 m circumference) that traces the surface profile. |
| **Pivot Arm** | Connects the probe wheel to the chassis, allowing vertical motion. |
| **Displacement Counter** | A mechanical or digital counter that registers one "count" for every **5 mm** of cumulative vertical movement. |
| **Support Foot (Pivot)** | A fixed low-friction point that allows the chassis to pivot smoothly. |
| **Handle / Towing Frame** | For the operator to push/pull the device at walking pace. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The device is a **bump integrator class** of instrument — it does not measure individual bump heights but rather the **summation of all upward and downward movements** of the probe wheel.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The MERLIN operates on the principle of **relative displacement measurement** between a moving probe and a quasi-stationary reference frame.

**Step 1 — Chassis Reference Stabilization:**
The chassis rests on a small caster-like road wheel and a support foot. As the probe moves over a bump, the inertia of the chassis and the support foot keep it relatively stable. This is because the chassis is damped by gravity and friction, moving at a much longer wavelength (low-pass filter behavior).

**Step 2 — Probe Wheel Tracking:**
The probe wheel (small wheel, 200 mm diameter) faithfully follows every micro-irregularity on the surface, regardless of wavelength. This creates a **vertical relative displacement** between the probe wheel center and the chassis datum.

**Step 3 — Mechanical Counting:**
The pivot arm converts vertical displacement into rotation. Every **5 mm of cumulative up-and-down movement** registers as **1 count** on the displacement counter (a typical mechanical bicycle-style odometer or a digital encoder).

**Step 4 — Cumulative Recording over Test Length:**
The total counts are recorded over a fixed test section — typically **100 m, 200 m, 300 m, or 1 km** depending on the required precision.

**Step 5 — Mathematical Conversion to IRI:**
The recorded counts (normalized to per km) are substituted into the **TRL calibration equation** to obtain the IRI in m/km.

> [!NOTE]
> The MERLIN is a **Class 2 / Class 3 response-type road roughness measuring system (RTRRMS)** as per the World Bank classification. It is **not** a profile-measuring device — it does not output the true road profile.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|-------------------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{N_{counts}}{L_{km}}$ | MERLIN Counts per kilometre | counts/km |
| 2 | $IRI = 0.593 + 0.0471 \cdot C$ | TRL Calibration (counts 0–280/km) | m/km |
| 3 | $IRI = 1.21 \cdot C^{0.65}$ | TRL Power Calibration (alternative) | m/km |
| 4 | $d_{unit} = 5 \text{ mm}$ | Count resolution per click | mm |
| 5 | $L_{total} = N_{seg} \times L_{seg}$ | Total test length | m |
| 6 | $N_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i$ | Total counts (sum of sections) | counts |
| 7 | $R_{BI} = \dfrac{N_{counts}}{L_{km}} \times \text{scale}$ | Bump Integrator equivalent | mm/km |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Which calibration equation to use?**
> - For **standard MERLIN** (TRL original): Use **Eq. 2** linear formula for counts up to 280/km.
> - For **extended range (rough roads)** or when counts exceed 280/km: Use **Eq. 3** power formula.
> - The KTU lab manual accepts both but the **linear equation (Eq. 2)** is the **default answer** in university exams.

## 2.3 Why Use MERLIN? — Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Cost-Effective Surveillance:** A MERLIN unit costs roughly **₹50,000 – ₹1,00,000** versus several crores for a laser profilometer.
2. **One-Person Operation:** No dedicated vehicle, no traffic closure required for low-speed surveys.
3. **Field Robustness:** Battery-free mechanical version works in remote rural areas without power.
4. **Calibration of Network-Level Pavement Management Systems (PMS):** Provides reliable IRI inputs for HDM-4 models.
5. **PMGSY (Pradhan Mantri Gram Sadak Yojana) Compliance:** Widely used by NIT/TRL agencies for quality assurance of rural roads in India.

> [!TIP]
> The MERLIN's repeatability is approximately **±10%** at 95% confidence for homogeneous sections, which is excellent for its price class.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From MERLIN Counts to IRI (TRL Linear Model)

The original TRL calibration was developed empirically by comparing MERLIN counts against the reference IRI measured by a precise inertial profilometer on a wide range of road surfaces. The regression yielded the following linear relationship:

$$
IRI \; (\text{m/km}) = a + b \cdot C
$$

where $C$ is the MERLIN count per kilometre, and $a$, $b$ are empirical regression coefficients.

**For the standard MERLIN (TRL, Cundill 1991):**
- $a = 0.593$
- $b = 0.0471$

$$
\therefore \; IRI = 0.593 + 0.0471 \cdot C \quad \text{...(Eq. A)}
$$

This equation is valid in the range **$0 \le C \le 280$ counts/km**.

**For very rough roads** (typically unpaved or severely distressed), the linear fit deviates. TRL proposed a power-law model:

$$
IRI = 1.21 \cdot C^{0.65} \quad \text{...(Eq. B)}
$$

This captures the non-linear saturation effect at high roughness levels.

## 3.2 Sample Worked Numerical (KTU Lab Standard Format)

### **Problem Statement**

A student operates a MERLIN apparatus along a **300 m section** of a rural road in **3 runs of 100 m each**. The displacement counter readings are:

| Run No. | Initial Reading | Final Reading | Counts Recorded |
|---------|----------------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | 0 | 32 | 32 |
| 2 | 0 | 28 | 28 |
| 3 | 0 | 36 | 36 |

**Required:** Determine the **MERLIN count per km (C)** and the corresponding **IRI** using the TRL linear calibration.

### **Step-by-Step Solution**

**Step 1 — Total counts over 300 m**

$$
N_{total} = 32 + 28 + 36 = 96 \text{ counts}
$$

This is the sum of displacement counts in all three runs.

**Step 2 — Counts per kilometre (C)**

The test length is 300 m, so we scale up:

$$
C = \dfrac{N_{total}}{L_{km}} = \dfrac{96}{0.300} = 320 \text{ counts/km}
$$

Here, $L_{km} = 300 \text{ m} = 0.300 \text{ km}$.

**Step 3 — Validity check of equation**

Since $C = 320$ counts/km, which is **> 280 counts/km**, the **linear equation is borderline invalid**. We must use the **power-law equation (Eq. B)** for better accuracy.

**Step 4 — Compute IRI (Power Law)**

$$
IRI = 1.21 \cdot C^{0.65}
$$

$$
IRI = 1.21 \cdot (320)^{0.65}
$$

Compute the exponent:

$$
(320)^{0.65} = e^{0.65 \cdot \ln(320)}
$$

$$
\ln(320) = 5.7683
$$

$$
0.65 \times 5.7683 = 3.7494
$$

$$
e^{3.7494} = 42.51
$$

$$
\therefore \; IRI = 1.21 \times 42.51 = 51.43 \text{ m/km}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute IRI (Linear, for comparison)**

$$
IRI_{lin} = 0.593 + 0.0471 \times 320 = 0.593 + 15.072 = 15.67 \text{ m/km}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion**

$$
\boxed{IRI \approx 51.4 \text{ m/km} \quad \text{(Power law, recommended)}}
$$

This value indicates an **extremely rough road** (typical of a heavily potholed unpaved surface). The road fails **PMGSY** (limit 3.5 m/km), **NH** (limit 2.5 m/km), and even unpaved road desirable limits.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Interpretation of IRI

| IRI Range (m/km) | Surface Condition | Engineering Action |
|------------------|-------------------|--------------------|
| 0 – 2.0 | Excellent (new expressway) | Routine monitoring |
| 2.0 – 4.0 | Good (well-maintained NH/SH) | Routine maintenance |
| 4.0 – 6.0 | Fair (minor distress) | Preventive maintenance |
| 6.0 – 9.0 | Mediocre (visible wear) | Surface overlay planning |
| 9.0 – 12.0 | Poor (significant distress) | Strengthened overlay |
| > 12.0 | Very Poor (failing pavement) | Reconstruction needed |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — MERLIN Apparatus Physical Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    chassisA[Chassis Frame]
    handleA[Operator Handle]
    probeA[Probe Wheel Diameter 200 mm]
    pivotA[Pivot Arm]
    counterA[Displacement Counter 5 mm per count]
    supportA[Support Foot Low Friction Pivot]
    roadA[Road Surface Undulation]
    refA[Chassis Reference Datum]

    roadA -->|contact| probeA
    probeA -->|vertical motion| pivotA
    pivotA -->|rotation| counterA
    chassisA --- handleA
    chassisA --- supportA
    chassisA -.->|acts as| refA
    supportA -->|slides on| roadA
```

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram represents the **functional architecture** of how the probe wheel, pivot arm, and counter interact while the chassis remains the reference datum. In an actual drawing, the probe wheel sits **forward** and the support foot **rearward**, with the pivot arm bridging them.

## 4.2 Mermaid — Process Flow of MERLIN Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Step 1 Site Selection Identify 100 m / 300 m uniform section]
    stepB[Step 2 Mark Start and End Points on Pavement]
    stepC[Step 3 Zero the Counter at Start Point]
    stepD[Step 4 Push MERLIN at Walking Pace Approximately 1.2 m per s]
    stepE[Step 5 Record Final Counter Reading at End]
    stepF[Step 6 Repeat for 3 Runs]
    stepG[Step 7 Compute Total Counts N]
    stepH[Step 8 Scale to Counts per km C = N divided by L]
    stepI[Step 9 Select TRL Equation Linear or Power]
    stepJ[Step 10 Compute IRI in m per km]
    stepK[Step 11 Classify Road and Recommend Action]

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI --> stepJ --> stepK
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Calibration Equation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qA[Compute MERLIN Counts per km C]
    qB{Is C less than or equal to 280?}
    qC[Use Linear Equation IRI equals 0.593 plus 0.0471 times C]
    qD[Use Power Equation IRI equals 1.21 times C to the power 0.65]
    qE[Report IRI and Classify Road Condition]

    qA --> qB
    qB -- Yes --> qC --> qE
    qB -- No --> qD --> qE
```

> [!TIP]
> This decision tree is the **most commonly tested flowchart** in KTU viva voce for this experiment. Memorize the two threshold values: **280 counts/km** and the **two calibration equations**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** 
**Define the MERLIN apparatus. List its main components and state the standard count resolution.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
MERLIN (Machine for Evaluating Roughness using Low-cost INstrumentation) is a portable, response-type road roughness measuring instrument developed by the **Transport Research Laboratory (TRL), UK**. It measures the **cumulative vertical displacement** of a small probe wheel as it traverses the road surface, relative to a quasi-stationary chassis. The standard count resolution is **5 mm per count** (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for components — chassis, probe wheel, pivot arm, counter, support foot, handle, 1 mark for 5 mm resolution).

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the International Roughness Index (IRI)? Mention its units and the typical desirable limit for a National Highway in India as per MoRTH.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **International Roughness Index (IRI)** is a standard scale for measuring road roughness, defined as the accumulated suspension stroke of a standard quarter-car simulation, divided by the distance travelled. **Units: m/km or mm/m** (1 mark). The desirable IRI limit for a National Highway is **≤ 2.5 m/km** (1 mark). IRI is computed from MERLIN counts using the TRL calibration equation: $IRI = 0.593 + 0.0471 \cdot C$ (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **working principle of the MERLIN apparatus** used for measuring road roughness. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a MERLIN survey on a **200 m stretch of flexible pavement**, three test runs yielded the following counts: **Run 1 = 24**, **Run 2 = 26**, **Run 3 = 22**. Calculate the **MERLIN count per km (C)** and the corresponding **IRI** using the appropriate TRL calibration equation. State the road condition classification. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Definition and Purpose:** MERLIN is a portable response-type roughness measuring device that quantifies road roughness in terms of cumulative vertical displacement of a probe wheel (1 mark).
2. **Component Layout:** Chassis resting on a single road wheel and a low-friction support foot forms the **reference frame** (1 mark).
3. **Probe Mechanism:** A small diameter (200 mm) probe wheel traces every surface irregularity (1 mark).
4. **Counting Mechanism:** Vertical motion of the probe is converted to rotation of a counter; every **5 mm of cumulative up/down movement = 1 count** (1 mark).
5. **Operator Action:** Pushed manually at walking pace (≈ 1.2 m/s) along the test section (1 mark).
6. **Conversion to IRI:** The total counts are scaled to counts/km and substituted into the TRL calibration equation (1 mark).
7. **Block Diagram** (sketch chassis → probe wheel → pivot → counter → operator) (1 mark).

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Total Counts (1 mark):**

$$
N = 24 + 26 + 22 = 72 \text{ counts over 200 m}
$$

**Step 2 — Counts per km (2 marks):**

$$
C = \dfrac{72}{0.200} = 360 \text{ counts/km}
$$

**Step 3 — Validity Check (1 mark):**
Since $C = 360 > 280$ counts/km → **Power-law equation is recommended.**

**Step 4 — IRI Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
IRI = 1.21 \times (360)^{0.65}
$$

$$
\ln(360) = 5.8861, \quad 0.65 \times 5.8861 = 3.826
$$

$$
e^{3.826} = 45.86
$$

$$
IRI = 1.21 \times 45.86 \approx 55.49 \text{ m/km}
$$

**Step 5 — Classification (1 mark):** IRI > 12 m/km → **Very Poor / Failing pavement** requiring reconstruction.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Class 1 (profilometer-based)** and **Class 3 (response-type)** road roughness measuring systems. Where does MERLIN fit in this classification? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A MERLIN survey is conducted on a **500 m road stretch** divided into 5 segments of 100 m each. The counts are: **48, 52, 45, 50, 55**. Compute the mean MERLIN count per km and the IRI. Comment on the road condition. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

| Feature | Class 1 (Profilometer) | Class 3 (Response-type) |
|---------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Output | True longitudinal profile | IRI estimate via calibration |
| Cost | Very high (₹1–10 Cr) | Low (₹50K–₹1L) |
| Speed | 20–80 km/h | Walking pace |
| Accuracy | ±2% | ±10% |
| Power | Vehicle + power + laptop | None / battery |
| Examples | Laser Profilometer, ROMDAS | MERLIN, Bump Integrator |

MERLIN belongs to **Class 3 — Response-type** (1 mark for table, 1 mark for correct classification, 2 marks for each of three differentiating features, 2 marks for examples).

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Total Counts (1 mark):**

$$
N = 48 + 52 + 45 + 50 + 55 = 250 \text{ counts over 500 m}
$$

**Step 2 — Counts per km (1 mark):**

$$
C = \dfrac{250}{0.500} = 500 \text{ counts/km}
$$

**Step 3 — Equation Selection (1 mark):** $C = 500 > 280$ → Power law.

**Step 4 — IRI Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
IRI = 1.21 \times (500)^{0.65}
$$

$$
\ln(500) = 6.2146, \quad 0.65 \times 6.2146 = 4.0395
$$

$$
e^{4.0395} \approx 56.85
$$

$$
IRI = 1.21 \times 56.85 \approx 68.79 \text{ m/km}
$$

**Step 5 — Variation Analysis (1 mark):** Segment counts (45–55) show low variation, indicating **uniform roughness** along the stretch.

**Step 6 — Classification (1 mark):** IRI ≈ 68.8 m/km → **Extremely rough**, reconstruction mandatory.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Validity Check (–2 marks):** Many students blindly use the linear equation $IRI = 0.593 + 0.0471 \cdot C$ even when $C > 280$. Always check first.
> 2. **Unit Conversion Error (–2 marks):** MERLIN counts must be **per kilometre**, not per 100 m. Forgetting to scale up by $10\times$ for a 100 m test is a frequent blunder.
> 3. **Missing the "3-run average" requirement (–1 mark):** KTU lab viva demands that at least 3 runs be averaged. Reporting a single-run result loses a mark.
> 4. **Ignoring the count resolution (–1 mark):** Stating the resolution as 1 mm or 10 mm instead of the correct **5 mm per count** is a routine mark-loser.
> 5. **Not classifying the road (–1 mark):** The final interpretation (good/fair/poor) is a mandatory concluding step in KTU model answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **MERLIN** = Machine for Evaluating Roughness using Low-cost INstrumentation (TRL, UK).
- **Principle:** Cumulative vertical displacement of a probe wheel relative to a quasi-stationary chassis.
- **Count Resolution:** **5 mm per count** — memorize this verbatim.
- **Two Calibration Equations:**
  - Linear: $IRI = 0.593 + 0.0471 \cdot C$ (valid for $C \le 280$ counts/km)
  - Power: $IRI = 1.21 \cdot C^{0.65}$ (for $C > 280$ counts/km)
- **Unit of IRI:** **m/km** (also written mm/m).
- **Standard Test Length:** 100 m × 3 runs (or 300 m single run).
- **Walking Speed:** ~1.2 m/s — do not run; the device is damped for walking speed.
- **MERLIN Class:** **Class 3 RTRRMS** (response-type).
- **Acceptable IRI Limits:** Expressway ≤ 2.0, NH ≤ 2.5, Rural/PMGSY ≤ 3.5 m/km.
- **Final Step:** Always classify the road condition and recommend a maintenance action.
- **Common Viva Questions:** "Why is the probe wheel smaller than the chassis wheel?", "What is the threshold count for switching equations?", "What is the role of the support foot?"
